items.question_text: An industry group, not part of any FAA facility, wants to recommend a procedural change to FAA Order JO 7110.65. Through which channel should the recommendation be submitted?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-8b, recommendations from sources other than FAA personnel should be submitted through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels. The key distinction from 1-1-8a is that non-personnel sources do not route through facility management — they use the channel appropriate to their relationship with the FAA.

choices.text: Directly to facility management at the nearest air traffic facility.
Through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels.
Only through the Federal Register public comment process.
In person at a public FAA town hall meeting only.

items.question_text: The Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations determines that a request requires a clarification rather than an interpretation. What must happen next under FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-9a2(b), when a clarification is required, the request is returned to the OSG. The OSG must provide a written clarification response to the requestor and forward a copy to the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations and AJV-P. Clarification stays within the service area — it does not go to AJV-P for a formal interpretation.

choices.text: The request is forwarded to AJV-P for a published interpretation.
The Service Area Director drafts the clarification personally and sends it directly to the requestor.
The request is returned to the OSG, which provides a written clarification response to the requestor with copies to the Service Area Director and AJV-P.
The request is closed without response because clarification requests do not require written replies.

items.question_text: An Air Traffic Manager submits a request to the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations through the OSG. What trigger condition determines whether the request goes to AJV-P for interpretation rather than being returned for a clarification?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-9a2(a), the trigger is whether more than one interpretation of the language can be inferred. If the language is unambiguous but the field needs explanation, it is a clarification handled by the OSG. If the language itself supports multiple reasonable readings, it requires a formal interpretation from AJV-P.

choices.text: Whether the request was submitted by an Air Traffic Manager or a staff specialist.
Whether the facility has previously received a clarification on the same paragraph.
Whether the request arrived before or after the cutoff date for completion.
Whether more than one interpretation of the language can be inferred.

items.question_text: A controller has completed basic SMS training via eLMS and wants to pursue advanced SMS qualifications. Which of the following does FAA Order JO 7110.65 identify as available advanced SMS training?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-12, additional courses along with Technical Training for SMS Practitioners and SMS Facilitators are available from AJI. SMS practitioners and facilitators are distinct roles with specialized training beyond the basic eLMS course available to all employees.

choices.text: Technical Training for SMS Practitioners and SMS Facilitators, available from AJI.
Only eLMS-based refresher courses; no advanced qualifications are mentioned in the order.
SMS Supervisor certification through the service area office only.
On-the-job SMS training conducted exclusively by the facility Quality Control Group.

items.question_text: A controller reads a procedure that references 'aircraft' and assumes it refers only to the physical airframe, excluding crew members. Is this interpretation consistent with the FAA Order JO 7110.65 definition?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1h, 'aircraft' means the airframe, crew members, or both. The definition is inclusive — it covers the airframe alone, crew alone, or both together — and the controller's interpretation incorrectly excludes crew members from the term.

choices.text: No, because 'aircraft' means the airframe, crew members, or both under the order's definitions.
Yes, because 'aircraft' in FAA usage always means the physical airframe only.
Yes, because crew are addressed separately under 'flight crew' definitions.
No, but only when the procedure explicitly mentions passengers.

items.question_text: A state aircraft is operating under 'due regard' and the pilot has assured separation from all other air traffic and confirmed a monitoring agency for search and rescue. Before the aircraft leaves the ATC frequency, what should occur?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1o2, an understanding between the pilot and controller regarding the intent of the pilot and the status of the flight should be reached before the aircraft leaves ATC frequency. This mutual understanding ensures both parties know what to expect after the frequency change.

choices.text: The pilot simply reports 'leaving frequency' and switches without further coordination.
An understanding between the pilot and controller regarding the pilot's intent and flight status should be reached.
The controller must issue a new transponder code and IFR clearance before the aircraft departs.
The pilot must verbally confirm each of the four operational conditions in use before leaving the frequency.

items.question_text: A controller reads a rule stating that 'singular words include the plural.' A procedure says 'an aircraft must maintain the assigned altitude.' Does this procedure apply when multiple aircraft are assigned the same altitude?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1f, singular words include the plural. 'An aircraft' therefore includes 'aircraft' in the plural, and the procedure applies whether one or multiple aircraft are involved. The singular-to-plural rule ensures procedures written in the singular apply to all instances.

choices.text: No, because 'an aircraft' applies only to a single aircraft by the rules of grammar.
No, unless the procedure is explicitly annotated with a plural marker.
Yes, because singular words include the plural under the order's word meanings.
Yes, but only if the aircraft are of the same type and weight class.

items.question_text: A controller reads that the minimum vectoring altitude for a sector is '4,500 feet altitude.' Under FAA Order JO 7110.65 definitions, what does 'altitude' mean in this context?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1j, 'altitude' means indicated altitude mean sea level (MSL), flight level (FL), or both. The definition is inclusive — it does not default to one or the other. In context, 4,500 feet refers to MSL altitude, but the definition itself covers all three possibilities depending on how the term is used.

choices.text: Always and exclusively indicated altitude MSL.
Height above ground level (AGL) unless stated as MSL.
Pressure altitude as computed by the altimeter setting in use.
Indicated altitude MSL, flight level, or both.

items.question_text: A reader encounters a reference block and a note block on the same page of FAA Order JO 7110.65. How do their functions differ?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-3 and 1-2-4: Notes present statements of fact or prefatory/explanatory material relating to directive material. References direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information such as FAA, NWS, and other agencies' orders, directives, notices, CFRs, and ACs. Notes explain; references point elsewhere.

choices.text: Notes present explanatory material; references direct attention to additional or supporting sources of information.
Both serve the same function — they provide mandatory procedures that supplement the directive text.
References contain explanatory material; notes point to external sources.
Notes create new requirements; references summarize existing requirements.

items.question_text: After using the prescribed phraseology for an approach clearance, a controller believes the pilot did not fully understand. The controller wants to rephrase the message using different wording. Is this permitted under FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.explanation: Per the PHRASEOLOGY note in 1-2-5, controllers may, after first using the prescribed phraseology for a specific procedure, rephrase the message to ensure the content is understood. Good judgment must be exercised when using nonstandard phraseology. The prescribed words must come first; rephrasing is for clarification only.

choices.text: No; once prescribed phraseology is used, the controller may not deviate from it in any way.
Yes; after first using the prescribed phraseology, the controller may rephrase to ensure understanding, exercising good judgment.
Yes; the controller may skip the prescribed phraseology entirely and use any wording that conveys the same meaning.
Only if the controller first obtains supervisor approval to use nonstandard wording.

items.question_text: A controller observes two aircraft converging with decreasing separation. At the same time, a pilot requests an airport advisory. The controller should:
items.explanation: Collision prevention is the primary purpose of the ATC system. While additional services are required when the work situation permits, the controller must first resolve the separation issue before providing the advisory service. The six precluding factors in paragraph 2-1-1c include controller workload, which is directly affected by an active separation task, and higher priority duties, which the separation task represents.

choices.text: Resolve the separation issue first, then provide the advisory service if workload permits.
Respond to the advisory request immediately because additional services are never optional.
Ignore the converging aircraft because the conflict has not yet reached a loss of standard separation.
Delegate the advisory request by telling the pilot to contact FSS, then proceed with separation duties.

items.question_text: A controller is simultaneously alerted to two situations: an aircraft is 500 feet below its assigned altitude and converging with opposite-direction traffic at the same altitude, and a different aircraft reports a suspected fuel leak. Which should the controller address first?
items.explanation: The note under paragraph 2-1-2a states that when more than one action is required, controllers must exercise their best judgment, and 'that action which is most critical from a safety standpoint is performed first.' A low-altitude converging conflict presents immediate collision risk, making it the most safety-critical action. A suspected fuel leak is serious but does not present the same degree of immediate hazard.

choices.text: Immediately hand off the fuel-leak aircraft to another sector and handle the conflict personally.
Address the low-altitude converging conflict because it presents the most immediate safety risk.
Address the fuel leak first because fuel emergencies always take priority over separation.
Address whichever call came in first, following a first-in-first-out protocol.

items.question_text: A controller issues a low-altitude alert to an aircraft. The pilot responds 'roger, we're correcting.' The aircraft's Mode C readout continues to show descent toward terrain. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: the source paragraph permits discontinuation of further alerts once the pilot informs the controller that action is being taken to resolve the situation. A pilot's acknowledgment ('roger') does not by itself confirm action is being taken. However, 'we're correcting' is a statement that action is being taken. Even though the Mode C readout still shows descent, the pilot has indicated corrective action — the controller may continue monitoring but is not required to issue additional alerts unless the situation changes.

choices.text: Issue a second, more urgent alert because the Mode C readout continues to show descent.
Declare an emergency on behalf of the aircraft and coordinate with the operations supervisor.
Transfer the aircraft to the next sector immediately so the next controller can continue monitoring.
Discontinue further alerts because the pilot has informed the controller that action is being taken to resolve the situation.

items.question_text: An aircraft reports a ground-based VOR malfunction. No second aircraft is available for a report, and the standby equipment cannot be activated. What additional information must the controller provide to technical operations personnel?
items.explanation: the source paragraph(5) requires that when continued malfunction is reported or standby cannot be activated, the controller must inform technical operations and request advice on shutdown. Additionally, 'in the absence of a second aircraft report, advise the technical operations personnel of the time of the initial aircraft report and the estimated time a second aircraft report could be obtained.' This provides technicians with critical timeline information for their diagnostic decisions.

choices.text: Only the fact that the standby equipment cannot be activated.
The time of the initial aircraft report and the estimated time a second aircraft report could be obtained.
The aircraft's call sign, type, and destination for the incident report.
No additional information beyond the continued malfunction report is required.

items.question_text: A controller issues a direct-to-fix clearance to an aircraft that will clip the corner of an adjacent sector. The controller knows the adjacent controller is on a landline with another sector. The issuing controller must:
items.explanation: the source paragraph requires that before issuing a control instruction that changes heading, route, speed, or altitude, the controller must ensure coordination with all affected controllers is completed. The note clarifies that ensuring coordination 'does not necessarily imply that the controller issuing the control instruction directly to the pilot has to perform the coordination action.' However, the issuing controller remains responsible for ensuring it was done.

choices.text: Have the pilot contact the adjacent sector directly for clearance through that airspace.
Issue the clearance immediately and notify the adjacent controller afterward if the aircraft only clips a corner.
Ensure coordination has been completed with the affected controller before issuing the clearance, but need not personally perform the coordination call.
Refuse the direct-to request because any route change requires a formal point-out to the adjacent sector.

items.question_text: A controller is preparing to transfer an aircraft to the next sector. The aircraft is 5 miles from the boundary at FL350. Another aircraft at FL350 is 20 miles inside the receiving sector, on the same airway, 25 miles ahead. May the controller transfer control now?
items.explanation: the source paragraph requires that control of an aircraft be transferred 'only after eliminating any potential conflict with other aircraft for which you have separation responsibility.' The trailing aircraft and the aircraft ahead are on the same airway at the same altitude. While standard separation exists (25 miles), the controller must ensure no potential conflict exists at the time of transfer. In this scenario, same-altitude same-route traffic 25 miles apart with the trailing aircraft approaching the sector boundary does not present a conflict — separation is assured.

choices.text: No, same-altitude traffic on the same airway always constitutes a potential conflict regardless of distance.
No, the controller must wait until the aircraft is physically inside the receiving sector.
Yes, but only if the receiving controller verbally accepts responsibility for maintaining separation.
Yes, no potential conflict exists because the aircraft ahead is 25 miles away on the same route, well beyond standard separation minima.

items.question_text: A controller issues 'Contact Los Angeles Center, One Two Three Point Four.' , which element must be communicated when transferring radio communications?
items.explanation: the source paragraph lists three elements for transfer: (1) facility name or location name and terminal function, (2) frequency (with listed exceptions), and (3) time, fix, altitude, or when to contact. The given phraseology correctly includes the facility name and function ('Los Angeles Center') and frequency ('One Two Three Point Four'). The third element (time/fix/altitude) may be omitted when the contact should be made immediately.

choices.text: Facility name/function and frequency, with the time/fix/altitude element implied as immediate.
Facility name and function only; the pilot is responsible for looking up the frequency.
Only the frequency, because the facility name is optional for ARTCC transfers.
Facility name, frequency, and a required time check for the handoff.

items.question_text: A VFR arriving aircraft was receiving radar vectors to the final approach course. The controller discontinues radar vectors 8 miles from the airport and instructs the pilot to proceed visually. The aircraft is following a Boeing 757. Is a wake turbulence cautionary advisory required?
items.explanation: the source paragraph(3) specifically requires a wake turbulence cautionary advisory for 'TERMINAL. VFR arriving aircraft that have previously been radar vectored and the vectoring has been discontinued.' This closes a safety gap: once vectoring stops, the controller no longer ensures the aircraft remains clear of the wake turbulence path, so the pilot must be alerted.

choices.text: Yes, but only if the pilot specifically requests the advisory.
No, because the pilot is now visual and responsible for own separation.
Yes, because VFR arriving aircraft that have previously been radar vectored require the advisory when vectoring is discontinued.
No, because advisory requirements only apply to IFR aircraft.

items.question_text: A tower reports UAS activity near the airport at 1400 local. At 1412, no further reports have been received. When may the controller stop issuing UAS advisories to potentially impacted aircraft?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that for pilot-reported or tower-observed activity, controllers must 'continue to issue advisories to potentially impacted aircraft for at least 15 minutes following the last report.' Since the last (and only) report was at 1400, advisories must continue until at least 1415.

choices.text: When the aircraft that reported the activity has landed.
Immediately at 1412, because 12 minutes have elapsed with no further activity.
Only after a supervisor authorizes discontinuation of the advisory.
At 1415, assuming no further reports have been received.

items.question_text: At a busy tower, a controller is relieved for a break by another certified controller. Both controllers have worked together for years. How must the position transfer be accomplished?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that the transfer of position responsibility must be accomplished in accordance with the Standard Operating Practice for the Transfer of Position Responsibility and appropriate facility directives 'each time operational responsibility for a position is transferred from one specialist to another.' Familiarity between controllers does not waive the SOP requirements. The SOP is a procedural safeguard, not a training tool.

choices.text: In accordance with the SOP for the Transfer of Position Responsibility and facility directives, regardless of how familiar the controllers are with each other.
Only a written briefing is required; verbal coordination between the controllers is optional.
Informally, since both controllers are fully certified and familiar with the position.
The relieving controller may simply plug in and begin working; the outgoing controller remains responsible.

items.question_text: A USAF C-17 is on an instrument approach and the pilot reported 'gear down' when intercepting the localizer. The aircraft passes the outer marker. What should the controller do regarding the wheels down check?
items.explanation: The opening sentence of paragraph 2-1-25 applies only to USA/USN aircraft. For USAF aircraft, the paragraph does not create an obligation — the rule is specifically scoped to 'USA/USN.' Even if it applied, the body text states the reminder is required 'unless the pilot has previously reported wheels down for that approach,' which the pilot did when intercepting the localizer.

choices.text: Issue 'WHEELS SHOULD BE DOWN' because this is an instrument approach.
No check is required; the paragraph applies to USA/USN, not USAF.
Issue 'CHECK WHEELS DOWN' because the aircraft has passed the outer marker.
The check is waived because the pilot already reported gear down before the outer marker.

items.question_text: A controller is working heavy traffic and observes what appears to be a pilot deviation. The controller has multiple aircraft on frequency requiring immediate attention. When should the possible pilot deviation notification be given?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states the notification should be given 'workload permitting.' This creates a two-part test: the controller must issue the notification (it is not optional), but may defer it until workload permits if immediate issuance would compromise safety. The controller should prioritize immediate safety-of-flight duties and issue the notification when workload allows.

choices.text: Immediately, regardless of workload, because any pilot deviation must be addressed without delay.
At the end of the controller's shift, when a written report can be completed.
When workload permits, because the notification requirement is qualified by the controller's ability to issue it safely.
Only if the supervisor authorizes the notification after reviewing the situation.

items.question_text: During EAS FDP operation, when are manual forwarding actions for flight plan information still required?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that during EAS FDP operation, manual forwarding actions 'are required in cases where the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer.' The note clarifies that data is normally exchanged between interfaced automated facilities and recorded automatically, but the manual backup remains when the automatic path fails or is unavailable for a particular facility pair.

choices.text: When the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer.
Only for VFR flight plans, which the computer does not process.
For all flight plans, because manual forwarding is always preferred over computer forwarding.
Manual forwarding is never required during EAS FDP operation.

items.question_text: A civil pilot at a joint-use civil-military airport plans a DVFR departure. What phrase must the pilot include in the initial call-up to the FAA-operated tower?
items.explanation: The note to paragraph 2-2-4 states: 'Civil pilots departing DVFR from a joint-use airport will include the phrase DVFR to (destination) in their initial call-up to an FAA-operated tower.' This is a specific phrase requirement for civil pilots at joint-use airports only.

choices.text: No specific phrase is required beyond a standard VFR departure request.
The phrase 'DVFR to (destination).'
The phrase 'DEFENSE VFR REQUEST.'
A request for a special VFR clearance.

items.question_text: A controller has written control information in the remarks section of a flight progress strip instead of coordinating it by voice. Would this satisfy the control information forwarding requirement?
items.explanation: the source paragraph explicitly states: 'Do not use the remarks section of flight progress strips in lieu of voice coordination to pass control information.' This is a prohibition, not a preference. Control information affecting separation, routing, or coordination must be communicated directly by voice, not left for the next controller to discover in written remarks.

choices.text: Yes, as long as the receiving controller acknowledges the remarks before assuming control.
Yes, written remarks are an acceptable substitute for voice coordination when workload is high.
No, the order expressly prohibits using remarks in lieu of voice coordination for passing control information.
Yes, but only for non-safety-critical control information such as altitude changes.

items.question_text: what additional item beyond the three data verification items in paragraph (a) must also be verified through Service F?
items.explanation: the source paragraph adds that 'Any cancellation message concerning a flight for which flight data has been previously forwarded must also be verified' through Service F. This is separate from the three data items in paragraph (a) (aircraft ID, assigned beacon code, and computer-assigned altitude/route data).

choices.text: The receiving controller's operating initials.
The time the original message was sent.
The flight plan's originating facility identification.
Any cancellation message for a flight whose data was previously forwarded.

items.question_text: A flight departs Seattle, enters Canadian airspace, and then returns to U.S. airspace. , who is initially responsible for forwarding the flight plan data upon the aircraft's return to U.S. airspace?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states: 'Initially, if a flight goes from U.S. airspace into Canadian airspace and returns to U.S. airspace, the Canadian ACC forwards the flight plan data to the appropriate U.S. ARTCC.' The U.S. ARTCC is the recipient, not the originator, for this return segment.

choices.text: The originating U.S. ARTCC retains responsibility and re-sends the data.
The pilot must file a new flight plan upon re-entering U.S. airspace.
The Canadian ACC forwards the flight plan data to the appropriate U.S. ARTCC.
The Seattle ARTCC forwards to the receiving U.S. ARTCC since it was the original departure facility.

items.question_text: An aircraft on an NRP route is moved by ATC to a different altitude due to traffic. Simultaneously, the pilot requests a different route. How should 'NRP' be handled in the remarks section?
items.explanation: the source paragraph requires 'NRP' to be retained when the aircraft is moved due to weather, traffic, or other tactical reasons — ATC-initiated moves preserve NRP status. However, 2-2-15b states that if the route is altered due to a pilot request, 'NRP' must be removed. When both occur, the pilot-requested change controls: 'NRP' must be removed because the route change was pilot-initiated, not purely a tactical ATC move.

choices.text: Contact the originating facility to determine whether NRP status should be maintained.
Retain 'NRP' because the aircraft was moved for traffic, which is a tactical reason.
The controller must decide whether the tactical move or the pilot request was the primary reason for the change.
Remove 'NRP' because the route change was pilot-requested, regardless of the concurrent tactical altitude move.

items.question_text: Under normal operations, how does the ATOP system present oceanic flight progress strip information?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that 'the ATOP system displays information on electronic flight progress strips and, in the event of a catastrophic system failure, will print flight progress strips with data in the corresponding numbered spaces.' Under normal conditions, controllers work with electronic strips; paper strips are a contingency output only.

choices.text: On electronic flight progress strips. Paper strips are only printed in the event of a catastrophic system failure.
On a combination of electronic and paper strips at all times.
On paper flight progress strips printed continuously by the system.
Electronically, with paper backup strips printed every 15 minutes regardless of system status.

items.question_text: Airline A is operating a flight using Airline B's aircraft under an equipment interchange agreement. Which air carrier's company name should be used in the aircraft identity on the flight progress strip?
items.explanation: the source paragraph expressly states: 'Use the operating air carrier's company name in identifying equipment interchange flights.' The operating carrier (the one conducting the flight), not the aircraft owner, is used.

choices.text: Both carriers' names separated by a slash.
The operating air carrier's company name — the one actually conducting the flight.
The aircraft owner's name, since it reflects the actual airframe.
Either carrier's name, at the controller's discretion.

items.question_text: A solo USAF undergraduate student pilot has passed an instrument certification check. What special operational allowance applies to this pilot regarding cloud layers?
items.explanation: The note to paragraph 2-3-7 states: 'USAF solo students who have passed an instrument certification check may penetrate cloud layers in climb or descent only.' This is a limited allowance — they may not operate in IMC for extended periods. The Z suffix remains in the flight plan regardless of certification status.

choices.text: They may file and fly IFR without any restrictions.
They may operate in any IMC conditions as fully qualified instrument pilots.
They may penetrate cloud layers in climb or descent only.
The Z suffix is removed from their flight plan upon instrument certification.

items.question_text: A terminal tower is operating with combined positions on a single frequency. How should ATIS be configured?
items.explanation: The note to paragraph 2-4-1 recommends that when combining local and ground functions on a single frequency, ATIS should be used if available to provide airport information on a separate frequency, reducing the need for voice transmissions on the combined control frequency. This keeps the combined frequency available for essential control communications.

choices.text: ATIS should be suspended during combined operations to avoid frequency congestion.
ATIS should be integrated into the combined control frequency to simplify operations.
ATIS operation is not affected by combined tower positions.
ATIS should provide airport information on a separate frequency to reduce the need for voice transmissions on the combined control frequency.

items.question_text: which specific readback item receives explicit emphasis beyond general clearance readback requirements?
items.explanation: the source paragraph singles out 'hold short instructions, whether a part of taxi instructions or a LAHSO clearance' as requiring specific readback verification. While all clearances and instructions require acknowledgment, hold-short instructions receive this additional emphasis because of their direct safety implications.

choices.text: Altitude assignments above FL180.
Hold short instructions, whether part of taxi instructions or a LAHSO clearance.
Speed adjustments in terminal airspace.
Frequency change instructions to a new sector.

items.question_text: A controller receives a request to relay a personal message from a company dispatcher to a pilot. The message is about the pilot's schedule after landing. May the controller transmit this message?
items.explanation: the source paragraph permits transmitting only 'those messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety.' A personal scheduling message does not meet either criterion. Even well-intentioned non-ATC messages degrade the frequency's availability for safety-critical communications and are prohibited.

choices.text: Yes, because relay of operational information to aircraft is authorized when necessary.
Yes, as long as it is short and the frequency is not congested.
No. Only messages necessary for air traffic control or otherwise contributing to air safety may be transmitted.
The controller may decide based on workload and whether the pilot would find the information useful.

items.question_text: A controller receives a report of phantom transmissions on the approach frequency. Per paragraph 2-4-6, what must be included in the broadcast alert to aircraft?
items.explanation: the source paragraph requires broadcasting an alert to aircraft operating on ALL frequencies within the area where deceptive or phantom transmissions have been received. The example includes warning aircraft to 'exercise extreme caution' and to verify ATC clearances. The broadcast must cover all frequencies, not just the affected one.

choices.text: An alert only on the specific frequency where the phantom transmission was received.
A private message to each aircraft individually to avoid alarming other traffic.
A general broadcast that does not reference the specific location to avoid copycat incidents.
An alert on all frequencies in the affected area, warning aircraft to exercise extreme caution and verify clearances.

items.question_text: A controller has already established contact with an aircraft and has exchanged several transmissions. On the next transmission, what elements may be omitted from the standard radio message format?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that on subsequent radio transmissions from the same sector/position, the ATC unit identification may be omitted. The aircraft identification is still used, but the controller need not restate 'Los Angeles Center' on every transmission once contact is established.

choices.text: The identification of the ATC unit may be omitted.
The aircraft identification may be omitted.
Both the aircraft identification and the ATC unit identification may be omitted.
The word 'over' must be used on every subsequent transmission.

items.question_text: A facility wants to use arrival gate names instead of position identifiers for interphone coordination. , what conditions must be met?
items.explanation: The note to paragraph 2-4-12a states that gate/fix name substitution for position identification may be used, but only when it is 'operationally beneficial, and the procedure fully covered in a letter of agreement or a facility directive.' Both conditions must be met — neither alone is sufficient.

choices.text: It must be operationally beneficial AND fully covered in a letter of agreement or facility directive.
It requires only that the facility manager approve the substitution verbally.
It must be operationally beneficial; written documentation is recommended but not required.
Gate names may be freely substituted whenever they would shorten the coordination call.

items.question_text: When communicating with Air Force One or Air Force Two, should the controller add the heavy or super designator to the call sign?
items.explanation: the source paragraph and c generally require 'super' or 'heavy' as part of the identification for super/heavy aircraft. However, paragraph 2-4-14e specifically provides an exception for Air Force One, Air Force Two, and other designated presidential support aircraft: the heavy/super designator is not added to these call signs. These aircraft are recognized by their unique call signs, and the weight-class designator would be redundant and could cause confusion.

choices.text: Yes, the heavy designator must be used with Air Force One if the aircraft is a heavy-class aircraft.
No. The heavy or super designator is not added to the Air Force One or Air Force Two call sign.
Yes, but only when Air Force One is operating outside of restricted airspace.
The controller should ask the pilot whether to include the designator.

items.question_text: A controller wants to ensure a pilot correctly copies a complex frequency. The controller says 'Contact Approach on One Two Six Point Five Five, One Twenty Six Point Fifty Five.' Is this correct ?
items.explanation: the source paragraph requires that numbers first be stated as specified in 2-4-17 (standard format), and only then may the controller restate in group or single-digit form for clarity. The controller correctly stated the standard digit-by-digit format first, followed by the group form restatement. Skipping directly to the group form is not permitted.

choices.text: No, because the group form must come before the standard form.
No, because restating numbers introduces confusion and should be avoided.
Yes, because the standard 2-4-17 format was stated first, followed by the group-form restatement for clarity.
Yes, because controllers may freely alternate between standard and group form at any time.

items.question_text: Two aircraft with similar sounding identifications are on frequency. When replying to one of them, what identification standard applies?
items.explanation: The introductory text of paragraph 2-4-20 states: 'Use the full identification in reply to aircraft with similar sounding identifications.' This overrides the normal rule that allows using the same identification the pilot used. Full identification removes any ambiguity about which aircraft is being addressed.

choices.text: Use the standard abbreviated format since communications are already established.
Use only the flight number, since the airline prefix is the same for both.
Assign one of the aircraft a temporary call sign to eliminate the conflict.
Use the full identification in reply — do not abbreviate or shorten the call sign.

items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft to enter Class Charlie airspace. , which of the following is the approved wording?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that airspace classes are pronounced using ICAO phonetics and that 'the term Class may be dropped when referring to airspace in pilot/controller communications.' 'Cleared to enter Charlie airspace' uses the ICAO phonetic 'Charlie' and drops the word 'Class' — both permitted simplifications.

choices.text: Cleared to enter Charlie airspace.
Cleared to enter Cee airspace — the letter name is always acceptable.
Cleared to enter Class C airspace.
Cleared to enter Class Charlie airspace — both the word Class and the phonetic must be used.

items.question_text: How is a published Military Training Route (MTR) described ?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that MTRs are identified by stating the letters IR or VR followed by the number. IR designates an IFR MTR, VR designates a VFR MTR. The number is stated in group form.

choices.text: By stating the route designator as individual digits, same as an ATS route.
By stating IR or VR followed by the number of the route in group form.
By stating Military Route followed by the route number.
By stating only the route number, since the IR/VR prefix is understood from context.

items.question_text: A controller describes a fix by saying the aircraft is passing Victor Twelve. Is this an acceptable fix description?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that expressions such as passing Victor Twelve or passing J Eleven must not be used to describe a fix. These expressions describe an airway, not a specific geographic point. A fix must be described by a named location or by radial/localizer/azimuth and distance from a NAVAID.

choices.text: Yes. Airways can serve as fixes when the aircraft is established on the airway.
Yes, provided the controller also states the distance along the airway.
No. Expressions such as passing Victor Twelve are expressly prohibited because they describe an airway, not a fix.
No, but only because the airway designation should include the word Victor after the number.

items.question_text: According to the note , what is the potential scope of weather impact on flight operations?
items.explanation: The note states that every phase of flight has the potential to be impacted by weather, and emphasis must be placed on gathering, reporting, and disseminating weather information. No phase departure, en route, approach, or landing is immune from weather effects.

choices.text: Weather primarily affects VFR operations; IFR flights are largely immune.
Only the departure and approach phases are significantly affected by weather.
En route flight above FL180 is generally free of weather impacts.
Every phase of flight has the potential to be impacted by weather.

items.question_text: what two methods may a controller use to define the area of coverage when issuing weather and chaff information?
items.explanation: the source paragraph requires defining areas in terms of: (1) azimuth by referring to the 12-hour clock and distance from the aircraft, and/or (2) the general width of the area and the area of coverage in terms of fixes or distance and direction from fixes.

choices.text: Azimuth and distance from the aircraft, and/or fixes or distance and direction from fixes with general width.
Only by reference to NAVAIDs, since that is the most precise geographic reference.
Only by latitude and longitude coordinates of the weather cell boundaries.
By stating the METAR station identifier that reported the weather conditions.

items.question_text: A tower controller notices the ceiling appears lower than what the automated weather station is reporting. What must the controller do before transmitting the observed ceiling value?
items.explanation: When tower-observed weather elements differ from those reported by the weather station, the controller must notify the weather station of the difference and obtain authorization before transmitting the specific values. The controller cannot unilaterally substitute their own observation for the official report.

choices.text: Only transmit the difference if an official weather observer is on duty at the tower at that time.
Notify the weather station of the difference and obtain authorization before transmitting the specific ceiling value.
Transmit the tower-observed value immediately, since it represents the actual conditions the pilot will encounter.
Use the automated station value without comment unless the pilot specifically asks about observed conditions.

items.question_text: A pilot of a foreign-registered aircraft requests the altimeter setting in millibars. The controller equipment only displays inches of mercury. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that if a pilot requests the altimeter setting in millibars, the controller must ask the nearest weather reporting station for the equivalent millibar setting. The controller does not perform the conversion.

choices.text: Convert the inches-of-mercury value to millibars using a standard formula and provide it.
Inform the pilot that only inches of mercury are available in U.S. airspace.
Ask the nearest weather reporting station for the equivalent millibar setting.
Issue the inches-of-mercury value and let the pilot perform their own conversion.

items.question_text: When barometric pressure exceeds 31.00 inches Hg, what action must the affected ARTCC take beyond issuing the high-pressure phraseology to individual aircraft?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires affected ARTCCs to coordinate with the appropriate FSS to issue a NOTAM when barometric pressure is above or forecast to be above 31.00 inches Hg. This ensures pilots filing flight plans or checking NOTAMs are aware that high-pressure procedures will be in effect.

choices.text: Contact all adjacent ARTCCs to coordinate a uniform altimeter setting for the region.
Issue a separate ATIS message dedicated solely to the high-pressure procedure.
Suspend all IFR operations until the barometric pressure drops below 31.00 inches Hg.
Coordinate with the appropriate FSS to issue a NOTAM advising that high barometric pressure procedures are in effect.

items.question_text: An airport has operational RVR equipment but the ILS approach is out of service and only a non-precision approach is available. Must the controller still furnish RVR values for the runway in use?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that where RVR equipment is operational, controllers must furnish RVR values irrespective of subsequent operation or nonoperation of navigational or visual aids for the application of RVR as a takeoff or landing minima. The RVR readout is independent of approach aid availability.

choices.text: Yes. RVR values must be furnished regardless of whether the navigational or visual aids using RVR are operational.
No. RVR values are only meaningful when used in conjunction with an operational precision approach.
Only if the pilot specifically requests the RVR value for the approach.
Yes, but only the midpoint RVR should be furnished when the ILS is out of service.

items.question_text: A recorded ATIS broadcast has been interrupted for 14 hours due to equipment maintenance. When the broadcast resumes, how should the first new message be identified?
items.explanation: the source paragraph states that alphabet letter phonetic words are used sequentially from Alpha through Zulu and repeated. However, when a recorded broadcast has been interrupted for more than 12 hours, the first resumed message must be identified with the phonetic word Alpha regardless of the letter previously in use.

choices.text: With the letter that was in use when the interruption occurred, to maintain continuity.
With the phonetic word Alpha, regardless of the letter that was previously in use.
With the next letter in the normal alphabetical sequence from where it left off.
With the letter Zulu to indicate the end of the previous interrupted cycle.

items.question_text: what specific cloud types must always be included in ATIS weather observations when present?
items.explanation: the source paragraph requires ATIS weather observations to always include remarks of lightning, cumulonimbus, and towering cumulus clouds when present. These are convective cloud types with associated hazards that pilots need to know about regardless of other ceiling/sky condition reporting.

choices.text: All cloud layers at or below 5,000 feet AGL.
Only cumulonimbus clouds, as they indicate thunderstorm activity.
Lightning, cumulonimbus, and towering cumulus clouds.
Only precipitation-producing clouds that affect visibility.

items.question_text: In an en route facility, a Radar Controller and a Radar Associate are working the same sector. The Radar Associate takes an automated handoff for an arriving aircraft. What must happen after the handoff is accepted?
items.explanation: the source paragraph specifies that after the Radar Associate accepts or initiates automated handoff actions needed for control transfer, they must ensure the Radar Controller is aware of the action. This maintains team situational awareness despite the division of labor between positions.

choices.text: The Radar Associate must ensure the Radar Controller is aware of the automated handoff action that was taken.
The Radar Associate must transfer responsibility for the aircraft to the receiving sector immediately.
The Radar Controller must personally verify and accept every automated action independently.
No further action is needed; the automated system handles the transfer without team coordination.

items.question_text: Which tower team position has primary responsibility for issuing clearances and ensuring the accuracy of pilot read back?
items.explanation: the source paragraph defines the Flight Data/Clearance Delivery Position as the position that issues clearances and ensures the accuracy of pilot read back. This position is commonly referred to as Clearance Delivery or simply Clearance.

choices.text: Tower Associate, because all position functions can be delegated to the associate.
Flight Data/Clearance Delivery Position, commonly referred to as Clearance.
Local Control, because all clearances ultimately come from the tower cab.
Ground Control, because departure clearances are issued before taxi.

items.question_text: Under what conditions may exceptions to the active-runway coordination requirements be authorized?
items.explanation: The note in 3-1-3 states exceptions may be authorized only as provided in paragraph 1-1-11 (Waivers to This Order) and FAA Order JO 7210.3 paragraph 10-1-7 (Use of Active Runways), where justified by extraordinary circumstances at specific locations. Exceptions are not available through local facility directives alone — they require waiver processes. Even extraordinary circumstances must follow those specific authorization paths.

choices.text: Through the facility manager's verbal authorization whenever extraordinary circumstances exist.
Only as provided in the waiver paragraph 1-1-11 and JO 7210.3 paragraph 10-1-7, where justified by extraordinary circumstances at specific locations.
Any tower supervisor may authorize an exception during low-traffic periods without additional documentation.
No exceptions are permitted — the active-runway coordination rules are absolute and may not be waived.

items.question_text: A controller is about to clear a departing aircraft for takeoff. A known maintenance vehicle is parked on the runway beyond the departure threshold. The vehicle operator has two-way communications and an LOA authorizing RSA operations. What must the controller ensure before the departure begins its takeoff roll?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-5b requires that the runway be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a departing aircraft starts takeoff or a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold. This requirement applies regardless of whether the vehicle has an LOA or two-way communications — the runway must be clear for the departing or landing aircraft. The LOA authorizes RSA operations but does not override the runway-clearance requirement for takeoff and landing.

choices.text: The takeoff clearance may be issued because the vehicle has an LOA and two-way communications, satisfying both safety requirements.
Issue the takeoff clearance and instruct the vehicle to hold its position — position-holding is sufficient to meet the runway-clear requirement.
Ensure the vehicle is clear of the runway before the departing aircraft starts takeoff, because the runway must be free of all known vehicles, equipment, and personnel before takeoff begins.
The vehicle may remain on the runway as long as the pilot is advised of the vehicle's presence before takeoff clearance.

items.question_text: An aircraft performing a wind shear escape maneuver is climbing straight ahead at full power. A second aircraft is 3 miles behind on the same departure route. The second aircraft's pilot has given no indication of any wind shear encounter. What is the controller's correct assumption about the second aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-8d(2) says: unless advised by additional aircraft that they are also performing an escape procedure, do NOT presume that other aircraft in proximity are responding to wind shear alerts/events. The controller must continue providing control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories to the second aircraft. Assuming the second aircraft is also escaping could lead to missed control instructions when the aircraft is actually proceeding normally.

choices.text: Assume the second aircraft is also encountering wind shear and will initiate its own escape — withhold control instructions.
Cancel the second aircraft's departure clearance preemptively because wind shear on the route makes continuation unsafe.
Instruct the second aircraft to follow the escape aircraft's path because wind shear conditions are likely affecting both.
Do not presume the second aircraft is responding to wind shear — continue providing control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories as appropriate.

items.question_text: How is the determination of what constitutes a 'significant disruption' to air traffic operations under the Tarmac Rule handled at different facilities?
items.explanation: The note in 3-1-15 states that facility procedures, including actions that constitute a significant disruption, vary by airport and must be identified in the facility directive pertaining to the Three/Four-Hour Tarmac Rule. There is no universal definition — each airport defines what 'significant disruption' means in its own facility directive, because disruption thresholds depend on local runway configuration, traffic volume, and gate availability.

choices.text: Facility procedures, including what constitutes a significant disruption, vary by airport and must be identified in each facility's Tarmac Rule directive.
A significant disruption is defined uniformly by the DOT rule and applies identically at every U.S. airport.
The controller on duty determines what constitutes significant disruption on a case-by-case basis without referencing any directive.
Significant disruption means any delay exceeding 15 minutes and is not subject to facility-level interpretation.

items.question_text: A pilot asks the tower for the current FICON NOTAM for Runway 36. The controller is handling moderate traffic. What is the controller's obligation regarding this request?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-3-1e(2) states: 'Issue FICON NOTAMs upon pilot request, workload permitting.' The controller must issue the FICON NOTAM when requested by a pilot, but the obligation is qualified by workload — the controller issues it to the extent the traffic situation allows. The request does not need to come from an arriving or IFR aircraft specifically.

choices.text: The controller must issue the FICON NOTAM immediately regardless of workload — pilot requests for NOTAMs are always mandatory and have priority over separation duties.
The controller may never issue FICON NOTAMs — only the airport operator or flight service station may provide NOTAM information.
Issue the FICON NOTAM upon pilot request, workload permitting — it is an on-request service, not an automatic broadcast, and workload is a qualifying factor.
FICON NOTAMs are issued only to IFR aircraft on an instrument approach and only without a pilot request.

items.question_text: Runways 18L and 18R are parallel runways served by separate ILS systems. Runway 18R is closed by NOTAM. Under what condition may the ILS associated with the closed runway 18R still be used for approaches to runway 18L?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-3-2c states that the ILS associated with the closed runway should not be used for approaches UNLESS not using the ILS would have an adverse impact on the operational efficiency of the airport. The default rule is 'do not use' but there is an efficiency exception. The side-step maneuver exception in paragraph 4-8-7 is a separate authorization.

choices.text: Never — the ILS for a closed runway may not be used for any purpose until the runway reopens.
Only when the approach will be a practice instrument approach that does not result in a landing on any runway.
Whenever the pilot requests it, because pilot preference overrides the closed-runway ILS restriction.
Only when not using the ILS would have an adverse impact on the operational efficiency of the airport, per the exception in 3-3-2c.

items.question_text: A NOTAM is in effect closing Runway 27 for construction. The construction supervisor asks the tower to turn on Runway 27's edge lights so workers can see the runway surface area at dusk. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-4-10e says: 'Do not turn on the runway edge lights when a NOTAM closing the runway is in effect.' However, the accompanying note states that the application concerns use for takeoffs/landings/approaches and does NOT preclude turning lights on for use of unaffected portions of a runway for taxiing aircraft, surface vehicles, maintenance, repair, etc. So for maintenance work on a closed runway, the lights may be operated.

choices.text: Yes — the NOTAM restriction on lighting applies to takeoffs, landings, and approaches, but does not preclude lighting for maintenance, repair, or surface vehicle use.
No — when a NOTAM closes the runway, runway edge lights must never be operated under any circumstance.
Yes, but only between sunset and sunrise — nighttime maintenance lighting on closed runways is the only authorized exception.
No — the runway may only be illuminated by portable construction lighting brought in by the maintenance crew.

items.question_text: A pilot requests that runway edge lights be turned off for Runway 18 because the lights are causing glare during a daytime approach. No other aircraft are using or will be affected by the change. Can the controller comply?
items.explanation: Paragraph 3-4-10d(2) permits operating lights differently from the standard rules when requested by a pilot and no other known aircraft will be adversely affected. This applies to both turning lights on and off. The note also explicitly acknowledges that pilots may request lights to be turned on or off contrary to the standard subparagraphs.

choices.text: No — runway edge lights must always follow the standard sunrise/sunset and visibility rules without exception.
Yes — the controller may turn the lights off as pilot-requested because it will not adversely affect other aircraft and the rule permits pilot-requested deviations.
Yes, but only if the facility directive contains a specific provision authorizing on-request light deactivation.
No — pilots may only request lights to be turned ON, not OFF, under paragraph 3-4-10.

items.question_text: A controller must select the runway in use. Which factor takes precedence when choosing among available runways?
items.explanation: The runway most nearly aligned into the wind is the primary consideration when selecting the active runway, provided it is operationally suitable. Other factors like runway length, approach aids, and traffic flow are secondary considerations. The wind alignment serves safety by minimizing crosswind components for arriving and departing aircraft.

choices.text: The runway that yields the highest airport acceptance rate for the current traffic demand.
The longest available runway, regardless of wind direction.
The runway most nearly aligned into the wind, when operationally suitable.
The runway preferred by the majority of pilots on frequency at the time of selection.

items.question_text: A pilot asks the tower about declared distances for Runway 18. How should the controller respond?
items.explanation: The note in 3-7-1 explains that declared distances are published in the Chart Supplement or AIP for pilot preflight planning only. They cannot be used for any ATC purpose. There is no requirement for facility personnel to be aware of them. If pilots inquire, the controller refers them to the Chart Supplement or AIP — they are not used for clearances, separation, or intersection departure advisories.

choices.text: Issue the declared distance for the runway because pilots are entitled to this information as part of ATC services.
State that declared distances are not a real concept in FAA documentation and advise the pilot to use the published runway length instead.
Look up the declared distance in your facility directive and issue it, because controllers must know all published distances for their runways.
Refer the pilot to the Chart Supplement or AIP — declared distances are for pilot preflight planning and cannot be used for any ATC purpose.

items.question_text: An aircraft is taxiing on Taxiway A approaching Runway 18. The tower observes another aircraft converging toward the same intersection from Taxiway B. Both aircraft are following their assigned routes. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: When aircraft on different taxiways are converging toward the same intersection, the controller must issue instructions to ensure separation — either by holding one aircraft short or by providing specific sequencing instructions. The controller maintains positive control over all aircraft on the movement area. Simply letting them 'see and avoid' at a taxiway intersection is not sufficient when both are under ATC control.

choices.text: Issue instructions to hold one aircraft short or provide sequencing to ensure they do not conflict at the intersection.
Allow both aircraft to continue because they are on different taxiways and the pilots will see each other at the intersection.
Switch both aircraft to the same frequency and instruct them to coordinate with each other.
Issue a general traffic advisory and let the pilots decide how to handle the intersection crossing.

items.question_text: A departing pilot calls and states 'have the numbers.' What departure information may the controller omit based solely on the 'have the numbers' statement?
items.explanation: 'Have the numbers' permits omission of runway, surface wind, and altimeter only. It does NOT indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast. Any other ATIS-departure information (such as NOTAMs, braking action, RwyCC) must still be issued unless the pilot separately states the ATIS code. The two omission rules are independent — 'have the numbers' and ATIS code trigger different omission scopes.

choices.text: All ATIS departure information may be omitted — 'have the numbers' is equivalent in effect to stating the ATIS code.
Runway, surface wind, and altimeter only may be omitted — 'have the numbers' triggers a narrower omission than the ATIS code covers.
Only the altimeter setting may be omitted because 'have the numbers' refers exclusively to altimeter information.
No departure information may be omitted — 'have the numbers' has no omission effect for departures and applies only to arriving aircraft.

items.question_text: A controller issues a 'line up and wait' instruction to a departing aircraft. Before the departure clearance can be issued, an emergency aircraft declares a priority landing on the same runway. What must the controller do with respect to the waiting aircraft?
items.explanation: When an aircraft is holding in position on the runway (line up and wait) and an arriving aircraft needs the runway, the controller must cancel the line up and wait clearance and instruct the waiting aircraft to taxi off the runway. The controller cannot clear the arrival to land with another aircraft holding on the same runway. The line-up clearance must be rescinded before the landing aircraft reaches the threshold.

choices.text: Clear the arrival to land behind the waiting aircraft — the holding aircraft will depart after the arrival passes.
Instruct the waiting aircraft to depart immediately to clear the runway before the arrival lands.
Instruct the waiting aircraft to taxi off the runway and cancel the line up and wait clearance before the arrival crosses the threshold.
Tell the arrival to go around — once an aircraft is lined up and waiting, the runway is committed to that departure.

items.question_text: An arriving pilot reports 'have the numbers' and states an ATIS code of 'Information Golf.' The runway has been shortened by construction. What landing information is the controller required to issue?
items.explanation: The pilot stated the ATIS code, which means ATIS-contained landing information may be omitted. However, paragraph 3-10-1m requires that when the runway has been shortened, the controller must ensure the pilot receives the runway number with a shortened announcement for all arriving aircraft if the pilot does not indicate the ATIS code. Since the pilot DID state the ATIS code here, the shortened-runway announcement requirement in 3-10-1m is not triggered — the ATIS code condition is satisfied. The distinction is that 'have the numbers' alone (without ATIS code) would NOT satisfy 3-10-1m.

choices.text: The shortened-runway announcement requirement is not triggered because the pilot stated the ATIS code — information Golf satisfies the condition for ATIS omission.
Issue the runway number with a shortened announcement and all other landing information — stating the ATIS code does not relieve the shortened-runway requirement.
Issue only the runway, wind, and altimeter because the pilot is clearly unfamiliar with the current ATIS information.
Require the pilot to re-state the ATIS code because 'have the numbers' overrides any ATIS code statement for purposes of the shortened-runway rule.

items.question_text: What is a nonapproach control tower's obligation regarding approach information for arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: Nonapproach control towers must forward approach information to arriving aircraft. This is the information the pilot needs to plan the arrival — such as the type of approach to expect — that the tower has received from the approach control facility. The tower acts as a relay for approach clearance information that the pilot needs before reaching the approach phase.

choices.text: The tower has no obligation because approach information is the sole responsibility of the overlying approach control facility.
Forward approach information to arriving aircraft so they can plan their arrival — the tower relays what it received from approach control.
Issue a full weather briefing and all active NOTAMs — approach information alone is insufficient.
Transfer all arriving aircraft to approach control immediately upon contact so the approach controller handles everything.

items.question_text: An aircraft is cruising at FL400 on a magnetic course of 090 degrees when it requests and receives a climb to FL430. What altitudes are correct for each segment of this profile?
items.explanation: At and below FL410, a magnetic course of 0-179 degrees (eastbound) requires odd cardinal altitudes — FL400 is incorrect; the aircraft should have been at an odd altitude such as FL390. Above FL410, eastbound courses require odd cardinal flight levels beginning with FL410, then FL450, FL490 at 4,000-foot intervals. FL430 would be incorrect above FL410 on an eastbound course. This scenario tests recognition that the hemispheric rule must be applied separately on each side of the FL410 boundary, where the interval changes from 2,000 to 4,000 feet.

choices.text: FL390 at/below FL410 and FL450 above FL410.
FL390 at/below FL410 and FL430 above FL410.
FL400 at/below FL410 and FL430 above FL410.
FL410 at/below FL410 and FL450 above FL410.

items.question_text: A higher MEA begins at the RIGEL intersection and no MCA is specified at that fix. When must an aircraft on the route begin its climb to comply with the higher MEA?
items.explanation: When a higher MEA begins at a fix and no MCA is specified, clear the aircraft to begin the climb prior to or immediately after passing that fix. Unlike an MCA case, the rule does not require the aircraft to cross the fix already level at the higher MEA.

choices.text: Require the aircraft to be level at the higher MEA before crossing RIGEL.
Clear the aircraft to begin climbing prior to or immediately after passing RIGEL.
Wait until the aircraft is at least 5 NM beyond RIGEL.
Begin the climb only after the pilot reports unable to maintain the lower MEA.

items.question_text: What operational factors should a controller consider when determining whether to clear an aircraft to a fix other than the destination airport?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-6-1 lists these operational factors: length of delay, holding airspace limitations, navigational aids, altitude, and meteorological conditions. These are considerations the controller weighs when selecting a holding fix. The pilot's fuel status is the pilot's responsibility to declare and manage — it is not a controller consideration for fix selection.

choices.text: Length of delay, pilot fuel remaining, holding airspace limitations, and altitude.
Navigational aids, meteorological conditions, pilot experience level, and delay length.
Length of delay, holding airspace limitations, navigational aids, altitude, and meteorological conditions.
Altitude, meteorological conditions, aircraft equipment suffix, and holding airspace limitations.

items.question_text: The assigned route includes a charted holding pattern. If complete holding instructions are not requested, what must the controller still issue?
items.explanation: For a charted holding pattern included in the assigned procedure or route, the controller may omit the detailed holding elements but must still issue the charted holding direction and the statement "as published." Complete instructions are required if the pilot requests them.

choices.text: Only the holding fix because the chart supplies the direction.
The radial, leg length, and turn direction in every case.
No holding information because the entire clearance is implied by the chart.
The charted holding direction and the statement "as published."

items.question_text: A controller observes intermittent radar presentation that is inadequate for providing separation services but might still be usable for basic traffic advisories. What does the paragraph require?
items.explanation: The personal satisfaction requirement is tied to the service being provided. If the presentation is inadequate for a specific service, the controller must not provide that service. Adequacy is assessed relative to the service intended — a presentation may be adequate for one service but not another.

choices.text: The controller may provide traffic advisories if satisfied the presentation is adequate for that service, but must withhold separation services if the presentation is inadequate for that purpose.
The controller may provide any radar service as long as the system is powered on.
The controller must stop all radar use entirely regardless of the specific service being considered.
The controller should ask a supervisor to decide which services are permitted.

items.question_text: A radar outage occurs at your facility. OS/CIC notification is mandatory. When must adjacent facilities also be advised?
items.explanation: The paragraph makes OS/CIC notification mandatory ('notify the OS/CIC of any radar malfunctions or unexpected outages') while adjacent-facility notification is conditional ('advise adjacent facilities when appropriate'). The appropriate trigger is when the malfunction or outage could affect the adjacent facility's operations or traffic handoff.

choices.text: Only when the outage extends beyond the facility's own delegated airspace.
When appropriate — if the malfunction could affect the adjacent facility's operations.
In every case, simultaneously with the OS/CIC notification.
Only when the adjacent facility calls to ask about the outage.

items.question_text: A controller applies merging target procedures. Two turbojet aircraft in RVSM airspace are vertically separated by 2,000 feet and their targets appear likely to merge. Must traffic information be issued under merging target procedures?
items.explanation: Traffic information under merging target procedures must be issued when targets appear likely to merge UNLESS the aircraft are separated by more than the appropriate vertical separation minima. Since 2,000 feet exceeds the 1,000-foot RVSM vertical minimum, traffic information is not required by this paragraph.

choices.text: Yes — all turbojet aircraft must receive traffic information regardless of separation.
Yes — 2,000 feet is the minimum vertical separation in RVSM airspace.
No — the aircraft are separated by more than the appropriate vertical separation minima.
No — merging target procedures only apply to aircraft below 10,000 feet.

items.question_text: A controller is unable to provide vector service to an aircraft subject to merging target procedures after the pilot requested a vector to avoid merging. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires that if unable to provide vector service, the controller must inform the pilot. This ensures the pilot knows not to rely on a vector for separation from the merging traffic and can take other action if needed.

choices.text: Hand off the aircraft to another controller who can provide vectors.
Issue an altitude change and not mention the inability to vector.
Take no action — vector service is always optional under merging target procedures.
Inform the pilot that vector service cannot be provided.

items.question_text: When does the controller's obligation to provide radar surveillance of outer fix holding pattern airspace begin?
items.explanation: The paragraph states surveillance must be provided 'whenever aircraft are holding there.' The triggering condition is the presence of aircraft holding in the outer fix airspace, regardless of time of day, weather conditions, or pilot request.

choices.text: Whenever aircraft are holding in the outer fix holding pattern airspace.
Only when weather is below VFR minimums.
Only when a pilot requests surveillance while holding.
Only during published holding periods in the facility directive.

items.question_text: An RNAV ATS route has 4 miles of protected airspace on each side of the centerline. An aircraft is observed 3 miles left of centerline on a track parallel to the route. Is a deviation advisory required?
items.explanation: The protected airspace extends 4 miles on each side of centerline. At 3 miles off centerline and on a track parallel to the route, the aircraft remains within protected airspace. The deviation advisory is triggered when an aircraft is 'observed in a position and on a track which will obviously cause the aircraft to deviate from its protected airspace area.' At 3 miles with a parallel track, deviation from protected airspace is not obvious. However, the note also indicates aircraft should remain within 2 miles of centerline — exceeding this may indicate a navigation issue worth monitoring.

choices.text: Required because the aircraft exceeds the 2-mile navigation performance standard.
Not yet required — the aircraft remains within the 4-mile protected airspace and its track is parallel, but the controller should monitor for any track change.
Required immediately — any position off the centerline triggers a deviation advisory.
Not required — deviation advisories apply only to non-RNAV routes.

items.question_text: An aircraft has been in radar contact for 30 minutes and the radar display is functioning normally. The controller wants to verify the aircraft's progress over a fix. May the controller request a position report?
items.explanation: The paragraph states 'If necessary, you may request an aircraft to provide an estimate or report over a specific fix.' While the aircraft discontinues compulsory position reporting after receiving 'radar contact,' the controller retains the authority to request reports when necessary. The 'if necessary' qualifier means the controller should have an operational reason.

choices.text: No — once radar contact is established, the controller may never request a position report.
Yes — but only if the radar display has malfunctioned.
Yes — the controller may request a report when necessary, even with a functioning radar display.
No — position reports can only be obtained from non-radar identified aircraft.

items.question_text: An aircraft is equipped with ADS-B Out but its transponder is inoperative. May the controller assign a beacon code to this aircraft?
items.explanation: The paragraph states beacon code assignments must be made to 'only ADS-B and/or transponder-equipped aircraft.' The note clarifies that ADS-B equipped aircraft are also still required to have an operable transponder, and the ATC-assigned beacon code is one of the required message elements of ADS-B Out. Without an operable transponder, the aircraft cannot receive or transmit a beacon code assignment.

choices.text: No — beacon code assignments require an operable transponder; ADS-B alone does not satisfy the requirement.
No — but the aircraft may still be provided radar services without a code.
Yes — ADS-B Out equipment is sufficient to receive a beacon code assignment.
Yes — but only if the aircraft is in Class A airspace.

items.question_text: A controller instructs an aircraft to go to standby 15 miles from its destination. The aircraft enters a multi-target area where clutter is a problem but is only 10 miles from the airport. May the controller use the standby instruction for clutter reduction at this point?
items.explanation: The paragraph provides two independent grounds for the standby instruction: (a) approximately 15 miles from destination when the controller no longer desires transponder/ADS-B operation, OR (b) when necessary to reduce clutter in a multi-target area. Condition (b) does not have a distance requirement, but the controller must instruct the pilot to return to normal position as soon as possible.

choices.text: No — standby for clutter reduction requires the aircraft to be at least 30 miles from the antenna.
Yes — standby may be used to reduce clutter regardless of distance, provided the pilot is instructed to return to normal as soon as possible.
No — standby for clutter is only available when the aircraft is more than 15 miles from destination.
Yes — but only with prior supervisor approval.

items.question_text: During a period of heavy VFR traffic, the controller's display is saturated with VFR target presentations that are interfering with IFR separation. What action may the controller take regarding Code 1200, 1202, 1203, 1255, and 1277 monitoring?
items.explanation: The paragraph permits temporary discontinuation of VFR code monitoring 'during periods when excessive VFR target presentations derogate the separation of IFR traffic.' This is a workload-relief provision for when VFR targets overwhelm the display and threaten IFR separation quality.

choices.text: Permanently filter those codes from the display for the remainder of the shift.
Continue monitoring — VFR code monitoring may never be interrupted.
Temporarily discontinue monitoring of those VFR codes until the excessive presentation subsides.
Discontinue IFR separation services until the VFR traffic clears.

items.question_text: An aircraft's transponder appears to be malfunctioning. After informing the pilot and issuing reset instructions, what additional notification is required?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires that the subsequent control position in the facility or the next facility, as applicable, be notified when an aircraft transponder is malfunctioning or inoperative. This ensures downstream controllers are aware of the equipment limitation before the aircraft enters their airspace.

choices.text: Notify the OS/CIC only.
Only the pilot needs to be informed — no further notification is required.
Notify all aircraft on the frequency of the malfunctioning transponder.
Notify the subsequent control position in the facility or the next facility, as applicable.

items.question_text: A controller has previously approved an aircraft to operate with a failed transponder. Traffic conditions have since become heavy. On what basis may the controller withdraw the approval?
items.explanation: The paragraph states the controller may disapprove a request or withdraw a previously issued approval 'solely on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.' No other justification is needed — operational factors alone are sufficient grounds.

choices.text: Solely on the basis of traffic conditions or other operational factors.
Only if the aircraft's transponder failure is confirmed by maintenance personnel.
Only with the pilot's concurrence.
Only if the aircraft has entered a different facility's airspace.

items.question_text: A controller accepts an interfacility handoff from another ERAM facility. The Mode C readout shows no unusual indicators. Is Mode C validation required?
items.explanation: ERAM facilities are normally not required to validate Mode C altitude readouts after accepting interfacility handoffs from other ERAM facilities. However, this exemption does not apply after initial track start or track start from coast is required, or during and after the display of a missing, unreasonable, exceptional, or otherwise unreliable Mode C readout indicator.
