items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft for a contact approach, but weather conditions are marginal. What must the controller include in the clearance to address the possibility that the contact approach cannot be completed?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-7e, when weather conditions are such that a contact approach may not be practical, an alternative clearance must be issued. The phraseology appends: IF NOT POSSIBLE, (alternative procedures), AND ADVISE. This gives the pilot a defined backup procedure if conditions prevent completing the contact approach.

choices.text: No alternative is required; the pilot is responsible for determining whether the approach is feasible.
Require the pilot to report the airport in sight every 60 seconds during the approach.
Issue a simultaneous instrument approach clearance as a backup to the contact approach.
Include an alternative clearance using the phrase: IF NOT POSSIBLE, (alternative procedures), AND ADVISE.

items.question_text: At an airport with an operating control tower, the reported ceiling is 800 feet broken. A VFR aircraft requests to depart and operate within the surface area without requesting SVFR. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1b, VFR operations may not be authorized beneath a broken or overcast ceiling within a surface area when the reported ceiling at the primary airport is less than 1,000 feet. A Special VFR clearance is required. Note: VFR operations ABOVE such a ceiling remain authorized under 14 CFR 91.155(a).

choices.text: Deny standard VFR beneath the ceiling and inform the pilot that a Special VFR clearance is required.
Authorize the VFR operation if the pilot can maintain the required cloud clearance of 500 feet below the ceiling.
Authorize the VFR operation using basic VFR minima; the pilot is responsible for staying clear of clouds.
Authorize the operation only if the aircraft is equipped with an operable transponder and Mode C.

items.question_text: A pilot requests a Special VFR clearance to operate in Class G airspace when the visibility is below basic VFR minima. Is this clearance available?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-1a, SVFR operations are authorized only in the surface areas of Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E airspace. SVFR is not authorized in Class G airspace (no surface-area control exists) or in Class A airspace (exclusively IFR).

choices.text: Yes, SVFR is available in all classes of airspace below 10,000 feet MSL.
No, SVFR operations are authorized only in Class B, Class C, Class D, or Class E surface areas.
Yes, SVFR is a pilot authority under 14 CFR 91.157 and does not require controller action.
No, SVFR is only available at airports with a published instrument approach procedure.

items.question_text: Why is radar separation normally not authorized between SVFR fixed-wing aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-3 note 2, SVFR fixed-wing aircraft must maintain 1-mile flight visibility and remain clear of clouds. These requirements can necessitate unpredicted maneuvering to avoid clouds, which makes radar separation unreliable. Nonradar or visual separation must be used instead. Radar separation may, however, be applied for SVFR helicopters when the facility has certified tower radar displays.

choices.text: SVFR fixed-wing aircraft operate only in areas below radar coverage, making radar separation technically impossible.
Radar separation is reserved exclusively for IFR aircraft and may never be applied to SVFR operations.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft must maintain 1-mile flight visibility and remain clear of clouds, which can necessitate unpredicted maneuvering that makes radar separation unreliable.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft are not equipped with transponders, so radar identification is unavailable.

items.question_text: An FSS has been authorized to transmit SVFR clearances for local operations. Under what condition may more than one SVFR aircraft operate in the surface area at the same time under this FSS authorization?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-5b, control facilities may authorize an FSS to transmit SVFR clearances so that normally only one aircraft at a time operates in the surface area. The exception: more than one aircraft may operate if the pilots agree that they will maintain visual separation from each other.

choices.text: When the FSS has radar equipment capable of providing separation between the aircraft.
When each aircraft remains on a discrete frequency assigned by the FSS specialist.
Never; an FSS may never authorize more than one SVFR aircraft in the surface area simultaneously.
When the pilots agree that they will maintain visual separation from each other.

items.question_text: A controller authorizes an aircraft to climb to VFR and needs to specify the area within which the pilot must maintain SVFR conditions until reaching VFR. Which phraseology correctly specifies this area?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-6 phraseology, the controller states: CLIMB TO V-F-R WITHIN (name) SURFACE AREA / WITHIN (a specified distance) MILES FROM (airport name) AIRPORT, MAINTAIN SPECIAL V-F-R CONDITIONS UNTIL REACHING V-F-R. Two options exist for specifying the area: by surface area name or by distance from the airport.

choices.text: CLIMB TO V-F-R WITHIN LINCOLN SURFACE AREA, MAINTAIN SPECIAL V-F-R CONDITIONS UNTIL REACHING V-F-R.
CLIMB VFR WITHIN 5 MILES OF THE AIRPORT, REPORT REACHING VFR CONDITIONS.
CLEARED TO CLIMB VFR, REMAIN CLEAR OF CLOUDS UNTIL VFR-ON-TOP.
MAINTAIN SVFR CONDITIONS WITHIN THE CONTROL ZONE UNTIL ADVISED.

items.question_text: Ground visibility at an airport is officially reported as 3/4 mile. A Part 121 domestic scheduled air carrier is ready to depart under IFR. Does the aircraft need an SVFR clearance to depart?
items.explanation: Per 7-5-7 note, 14 CFR Part 121 permits landing or takeoff by domestic scheduled air carriers where a local surface restriction to visibility is specified in the carrier's operations specifications (OpSpecs). The SVFR requirement for ground visibility below 1 mile does not apply to Part 121 carriers operating under their OpSpecs authorization.

choices.text: Yes, all aircraft need an SVFR clearance whenever ground visibility is below 1 mile.
No SVFR clearance is needed if the carrier's OpSpecs authorize operations at that visibility.
The aircraft may depart under IFR without SVFR, but only if it uses a published instrument departure.
Yes, unless the aircraft is a wide-body jet equipped with autoland capability.

items.question_text: A terminal facility has established procedures for providing basic radar services to VFR aircraft. To which types of airspace must these services be applied?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-1b, basic radar services for VFR aircraft must be applied to Class B, Class C, TRSA, and Class D surface areas where procedures have been established for this purpose. These four airspace/surface types form the domain of the basic radar service program.

choices.text: Only Class B and Class C airspace where separation services are mandatory.
All controlled airspace below FL180 where radar coverage exists.
Class B, Class C, TRSA, and Class D surface areas where procedures have been established.
Only the outer area of Class C and the TRSA service area.

items.question_text: A departing VFR aircraft wants traffic advisories after takeoff. According to the note in 7-6-10, what is expected of the pilot before departure?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-10 note, departing aircraft desiring traffic information are expected to request the service and to state their proposed direction of flight upon initial contact with ground control. This allows the controller to provide the correct departure control frequency and prepare appropriate traffic information after the aircraft is radar identified.

choices.text: File a VFR flight plan with FSS and advise ground control of the filed route.
Contact departure control directly after takeoff and request advisories at that time.
State only the destination airport; the controller will determine the direction of flight from the filed plan.
Request radar traffic advisories and state the proposed direction of flight upon initial contact with ground control.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft is receiving basic radar services when the controller's frequency becomes saturated with IFR arrivals. What principle governs whether radar service must continue?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-11, basic radar services should be provided to the extent possible, workload permitting. This is a qualified obligation — when workload precludes continued service, the controller may terminate radar service to the VFR aircraft. This is different from mandatory separation services which cannot be workload-dependent.

choices.text: Basic radar services should be provided to the extent possible, workload permitting; the controller may terminate when workload precludes continued service.
Basic radar services are mandatory and cannot be terminated for any reason once established.
All VFR services must be immediately terminated whenever IFR workload increases.
The controller must transfer the VFR aircraft to another frequency capable of providing continued radar service.

items.question_text: During hours when the tower is not in operation, a VFR aircraft requests landing information. The controller attempts to obtain wind information from the FSS but the FSS reports it does not have the current wind. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-12 note, wind information is provided from the FSS or WSO when the tower is inoperative. If that information is not available from those sources, the controller must inform the pilot that wind information is not available. The controller should not fabricate or use stale wind data.

choices.text: Provide the last recorded wind reading from before the tower closed.
Inform the pilot that wind information is not available.
Tune the ATIS frequency and relay the automated weather observation.
Ask the pilot for a wind report and use that as the official wind information.

items.question_text: An aircraft requests basic radar service but the primary approach control frequency is fully occupied handling an arrival push. What must the controller do if the pilot still desires the service?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-2b, if an aircraft desires basic radar service but the primary frequency is congested, the controller should provide the service to the extent possible using an available frequency. This could involve using a secondary frequency or coordinating service through another control position.

choices.text: Deny service and instruct the aircraft to try again when the frequency becomes less congested.
Hand off the aircraft to the overlying ARTCC for radar service provision.
Provide the service to the extent possible using an available frequency.
Accept the request but place the aircraft in a holding pattern until the frequency clears.

items.question_text: A faster trailing VFR aircraft is closing on an aircraft established on final to an adjacent parallel runway 2,000 feet away. Wake turbulence separation is required. What restriction applies?
items.explanation: When parallel runways are less than 2,500 feet apart, do not permit an aircraft to overtake another aircraft established on the adjacent final within the facility's area of responsibility when wake turbulence separation is required. The prohibition is conditional, not universal.

choices.text: Overtaking is allowed if both pilots report the other aircraft in sight.
Overtaking is allowed if 1,000 feet vertical separation is maintained.
No restriction applies because the aircraft are assigned different runways.
The trailing aircraft must not overtake while wake turbulence separation is required.

items.question_text: A controller assigned a VFR aircraft in a TRSA an altitude of 4,500 feet on a magnetic course of 270 degrees for separation purposes. The separation requirement no longer exists. The assigned altitude is contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for that direction of flight. What phraseology should the controller use?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-5c, when an altitude assignment that is contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 is no longer needed or the aircraft exits the TRSA, the controller must advise the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes. The prescribed phraseology is: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.

choices.text: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.
RESUME OWN NAVIGATION AND VFR ALTITUDES AT YOUR DISCRETION.
ALTITUDE RESTRICTION CANCELLED, FLY VFR ALTITUDES.
DISREGARD ASSIGNED ALTITUDE, MAINTAIN VFR.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft departs a controlled airport within a TRSA and is receiving TRSA services. The aircraft is now 2 miles from the TRSA boundary. The controller would like to terminate TRSA service. What must the controller continue providing until the aircraft actually leaves the TRSA?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-7c, the controller must provide separation until the aircraft leaves the TRSA. TRSA departure information (frequency, beacon code) and separation services continue through the TRSA boundary. The leaving phraseology may then be issued at the boundary.

choices.text: Traffic advisories only; separation responsibility transfers to the pilot at the TRSA boundary.
Separation until the aircraft leaves the TRSA.
Radar identification until the aircraft reaches 40 NM from the departure airport.
Beacon code assignment only; separation ends when the aircraft reaches cruising altitude.

items.question_text: An aircraft without an operable transponder needs to operate within Class C airspace for an airshow. Under what condition may this be authorized despite the normal equipment requirements?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-1, while two-way radio communications and an operational transponder are normally required for Class C operations, operations without this equipment may be conducted when authorized by Letter of Agreement (LOA), facility directive, or special authorization. This provides operational flexibility for special events and circumstances.

choices.text: Never; the equipment requirements for Class C airspace are absolute and cannot be waived.
Only if the aircraft is accompanied by an FAA-certified chase plane with the required equipment.
By Letter of Agreement, facility directive, or special authorization.
Only during daylight VFR conditions when the pilot can maintain visual separation.

items.question_text: A Class C facility has excellent radar coverage over most of its service area but poor coverage in one quadrant due to terrain. May the controller deny Class C radar service to aircraft in the well-covered portions based on the limited coverage in one area?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-2 note, limited radar coverage in one portion of a Class C area does not justify denial of Class C radar service in the entire area. Service must continue to be provided in portions of the area where adequate radar coverage exists.

choices.text: Yes, limited coverage in any portion permits denial of service throughout the entire Class C area.
Yes, but the controller must issue a NOTAM advising pilots of the service suspension.
No, but service may be reduced to basic radar services only in all portions of the Class C area.
No, limited coverage in one portion does not justify denial of radar service in the entire area.

items.question_text: A hot air balloon is operating in Class C airspace near the final approach path. An IFR arrival is on a 5-mile final. What separation must the controller provide between the balloon and the IFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-6b, hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft. The controller must still issue traffic information and safety alerts as appropriate. The balloon exception exists because balloons have limited maneuverability and cannot maintain standard separation from faster IFR traffic.

choices.text: No separation is required, but traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.
Standard IFR radar separation of 3 miles must be applied.
500 feet vertical separation must be maintained at all times.
Visual separation must be applied; the IFR pilot must report the balloon in sight.

items.question_text: An aircraft departs an uncontrolled airport located under Class C airspace. There is no LOA between the airport and the Class C facility. How should the controller handle this aircraft when it calls for Class C service after departure?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-7c, aircraft departing uncontrolled airports within Class C airspace must be handled using procedures advertised in the Chart Supplement (formerly Airport/Facility Directory). These published procedures provide the method for integrating uncontrolled-field departures into Class C traffic when no formal LOA exists.

choices.text: Deny Class C service and instruct the aircraft to remain clear of Class C airspace.
Handle the aircraft using procedures advertised in the Chart Supplement for that airport.
Provide only basic radar service since the departure airport is uncontrolled.
Treat the aircraft as a VFR pop-up and sequence it behind all primary-airport departures.

items.question_text: What specific equipment must an aircraft have to operate within Class B airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-1b, Class B operations require both: (1) an operable two-way radio capable of communications with ATC on appropriate frequencies for that Class B airspace, AND (2) an operable radar beacon transponder with automatic altitude reporting capability (Mode C) and ADS-B Out equipment. Both items are required, not optional.

choices.text: An operable two-way radio; transponder equipment is recommended but not required for VFR aircraft.
A two-way radio OR a transponder with altitude reporting; at least one of the two.
An operable two-way radio AND an operable radar beacon transponder with automatic altitude reporting capability and ADS-B Out equipment.
A two-way radio, a basic transponder, and an operable DME receiver for navigation within the airspace.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft in Class B airspace is assigned a heading of 360 degrees and altitude of 3,000 feet. The pilot realizes that maintaining this altitude at the assigned location would require flying through clouds, violating VFR cloud-clearance requirements. What is the pilot expected to do?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-2 note 1, a pilot operating in accordance with VFR is expected to advise ATC if compliance with assigned headings, routes, or altitudes will cause violation of any part of the CFR. Flying through clouds would violate VFR cloud-clearance requirements, so the pilot must inform ATC and request an amended clearance.

choices.text: Comply with the ATC clearance without question; ATC instructions override all VFR requirements.
Cancel VFR and request an IFR clearance to operate at the assigned altitude in the clouds.
Deviate from the clearance as needed to maintain VFR and advise ATC after the deviation.
Advise ATC that compliance will cause violation of the CFR and request an amended clearance.

items.question_text: A controller vectors a VFR aircraft on a heading that will take it outside Class B airspace temporarily before it re-enters. What must the controller communicate to the aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-9-3b, aircraft should be vectored to remain in Class B airspace after entry unless operational advantage is lost. If the aircraft will leave and re-enter Class B airspace, the controller must inform the aircraft when it is leaving and when it is re-entering Class B airspace. This is critical because outside Class B, the VFR aircraft loses the protection of Class B separation services.

choices.text: Inform the aircraft when it is leaving and re-entering Class B airspace.
No notification is needed; the pilot is responsible for monitoring airspace boundaries.
Issue a new Class B clearance number each time the aircraft re-enters the airspace.
Hand off the aircraft to the overlying ARTCC until it returns to Class B airspace.

items.question_text: A pilot files a VFR flight plan from Miami to San Juan through the Miami Oceanic CTA at FL 190. Is this operation permitted?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flight plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs are permitted only below FL 180. At FL 190 the aircraft is at or above FL 180 and therefore outside the permitted altitude range.

choices.text: No, VFR flight plans in those CTAs are permitted only below FL 180.
Yes, VFR flight plans are permitted in all Oceanic CTAs at any altitude.
Yes, but only if weather conditions meet 14 CFR 91.155 VFR minimums.
No, VFR flight plans are never permitted in any Oceanic CTA.

items.question_text: A pilot operating under a VFR flight plan in the Oakland FIR asks to continue 120 NM seaward from the shoreline. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-3, VFR flight plan operations within the Oakland FIR are permitted only when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace. At 120 NM, the aircraft would be beyond the permitted distance.

choices.text: Yes, as long as the aircraft remains in controlled airspace.
No, Oakland FIR VFR flight plan operations are limited to less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline.
Yes, but only during daylight hours.
No, VFR flight plans are prohibited in the entire Oakland FIR.

items.question_text: A letter of agreement between two oceanic facilities specifies that action to obtain a missing position report must be taken no later than 8 minutes after the control estimate. The default Chapter 8 deadline is 10 minutes. Which deadline applies?
items.explanation: Per 8-1-6, the 10-minute deadline applies "unless otherwise specified." When an LOA or directive specifies a different deadline, that specified deadline supersedes the default. The controller follows the LOA-specified 8-minute deadline for that operation.

choices.text: The 10-minute Chapter 8 deadline, because LOAs cannot change published minima.
The controller may choose either deadline based on workload.
The 8-minute LOA deadline, because the paragraph permits a different deadline when otherwise specified.
Both deadlines apply; the controller must attempt to obtain the report by the earlier time.

items.question_text: Two aircraft are entering oceanic airspace on courses that diverge by 20 degrees. Radar shows the aircraft are horizontally separated at the edge of known radar coverage, but the separation between them is decreasing. Does separation exist under the course divergence rule?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-2, course divergence separation requires BOTH: (a) courses diverge by at least 15 degrees until oceanic lateral separation is established, AND (b) the aircraft are horizontally radar separated and separation is increasing at the edge of known radar coverage. Decreasing separation fails condition (b).

choices.text: Yes, because the 20-degree divergence exceeds the 15-degree minimum.
No, because both conditions must be met and the separation is decreasing, not increasing.
Yes, because radar separation exists at the edge of coverage, regardless of trend.
No, because course divergence requires at least 30 degrees.

items.question_text: An aircraft is transitioning from oceanic airspace into an area of known radar coverage. Flight data indicates standard transponder equipment, but the controller knows the transponder is inoperative. What does the radar identification transition procedure require regarding transponder status?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-5d, flight data must indicate standard transponder equipment AND there must be no known information that the transponder is not operating. The procedure is disqualified when the transponder is known to be inoperative, even if the flight data indicates the equipment is installed.

choices.text: The procedure may be applied as long as flight data indicates the equipment, regardless of known status.
The procedure may be applied if the radar-identified aircraft can provide visual separation.
The procedure requires both flight data indicating a standard transponder and no known information that it is inoperative; known inoperative status disqualifies it.
The procedure does not require a transponder in oceanic transition airspace.

items.question_text: In addition to oceanic airspace transitions, to which specific control areas does the radar identification transition procedure also apply?
items.explanation: Per 8-5-5g, this procedure is also applicable to aircraft in transit from oceanic airspace into Guam Control Area (CTA), San Juan CTA, and Honolulu CTA radar coverage areas.

choices.text: Anchorage CTA, Oakland CTA, and New York CTA only.
Miami CTA, Houston CTA, and Arctic CTA only.
All CTAs; no specific limitation applies.
Guam CTA, San Juan CTA, and Honolulu CTA.

items.question_text: A controller needs to separate an aircraft from a temporary moving airspace reservation. How does the longitudinal separation method differ from the lateral method?
items.explanation: Per 8-6-3, longitudinal separation clears the aircraft so that the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation. Lateral separation ensures the route protected airspace does not overlap the moving reservation's time-dependent geographical area. Longitudinal is relative to aircraft in the reservation; lateral is relative to the reservation boundary.

choices.text: Longitudinal separation applies a minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft in the reservation; lateral separation prevents protected airspace overlap with the reservation area.
Both methods use the same technique; the only difference is the direction of measurement.
Longitudinal separation uses a buffer of one-half the lateral minimum; lateral separation uses the full minimum.
Longitudinal separation applies only to stationary reservations; lateral applies only to moving ones.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies between supersonic aircraft of different types operating at different Mach numbers?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3a2, 15 minutes applies between aircraft in supersonic flight that do not qualify for the 10-minute provision. The 10-minute minimum requires both aircraft in level flight at the same Mach number or same type in cruise climb.

choices.text: 10 minutes.
15 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to nonturbojet aircraft operating outside the West Atlantic Route System (WATRS)?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3c2, 30 minutes applies between nonturbojet aircraft operating outside the WATRS. The 20-minute minimum under 8-7-3c1 applies only within WATRS.

choices.text: 15 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.
10 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what conditions must be satisfied before an aircraft on a reciprocal track may be cleared to climb or descend through the altitude of another aircraft using ADS-C?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-3e2, an ADS-C position report on at least one aircraft must have been received beyond the passing point, and the aircraft must have passed each other by the applicable separation minimum.

choices.text: Both aircraft must report passing the same waypoint simultaneously.
The pilot of either aircraft requests the climb or descent.
Only after 15 minutes of vertical separation has been maintained.
An ADS-C position report on at least one aircraft has been received beyond the passing point and they have passed by the applicable minimum.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what lateral separation minimum applies between aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval that are operating on routes within WATRS?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-4d, 90 NM or 1.5 degrees latitude applies between aircraft not approved for RNP 4 or RNP 10 that operate on routes or in areas within WATRS, the San Juan CTA/FIR, or the Atlantic portion of the Miami CTA/FIR.

choices.text: 90 NM or 1.5 degrees latitude.
50 NM.
60 NM or 1 degree latitude.
120 NM or 2 degrees latitude.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what lateral minimum applies to supersonic aircraft operating above FL 275?
items.explanation: Per 8-7-4c1, 60 NM or 1 degree latitude applies between supersonic aircraft operating above FL 275.

choices.text: 23 NM.
60 NM or 1 degree latitude.
50 NM.
90 NM or 1.5 degrees latitude.

items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to turbojet aircraft operating below FL 200 in the San Juan Oceanic CTA/FIR outside the WATRS area?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-3c, 20 minutes applies between turbojet aircraft operating below FL 200 in the San Juan Oceanic (outside the WATRS area), Miami Oceanic, and Houston Oceanic CTAs/FIRs.

choices.text: 10 minutes.
15 minutes.
20 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to nonturbojet aircraft operating outside the WATRS in the New York CTA/FIR?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-3d3, 30 minutes applies between nonturbojet aircraft operating outside of the WATRS in the New York CTA/FIR. Inside WATRS the minimum is 20 minutes; outside in NY CTA/FIR it is 30 minutes.

choices.text: 15 minutes.
20 minutes.
10 minutes.
30 minutes.

items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what lateral minimum applies between aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval that are operating east of 55 degrees West?
items.explanation: Per 8-8-4f, 120 NM applies between aircraft operating east of 55 degrees West. West of 55W the minimum for non-approved aircraft is 90 NM; east of 55W it increases to 120 NM.

choices.text: 90 NM.
60 NM.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, reciprocal track aircraft without lateral separation must maintain vertical separation for how long before and after the estimated passing time?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-3a4, where lateral separation is not provided, vertical separation must be provided at least 10 minutes before and after the time the aircraft are estimated to pass or are estimated to have passed.

choices.text: 5 minutes before and after estimated passing.
10 minutes before and after estimated passing.
15 minutes before and after estimated passing.
Vertical separation is not required for reciprocal track aircraft in the Pacific.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, when using distance-based minima without ADS-C, what action must be taken if an aircraft fails to report its position within 3 minutes of the expected time?
items.explanation: Per 8-9-3d3, if an aircraft fails to report its position within 3 minutes after the expected time, the controller must take action to establish communication. If communication is not established within 8 minutes, action must be taken to apply another form of separation.

choices.text: Immediately apply another form of separation.
Wait 10 minutes before taking any action.
Take action to establish communication; if not established within 8 minutes, apply another form of separation.
No action is required as distance-based minima remain valid without position reports.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, when DME/RNAV longitudinal separation is applied in the North Pacific high-control areas, what is the maximum duration for which the procedure may continue without direct voice communications?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-3d3, application of DME/RNAV separation without direct voice communications may not continue for more than 90 minutes.

choices.text: 30 minutes.
60 minutes.
120 minutes.
90 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, when DME reports are used to establish longitudinal distance between two turbojet aircraft on the NOPAC route system, what minimum applies?
items.explanation: Per 8-10-3d1, 30 NM applies between aircraft when DME reports or radar observations are used to establish the distance. Otherwise at least 40 NM based on RNAV must be applied.

choices.text: 30 NM with DME; 40 NM with RNAV.
20 NM with DME; 30 NM with RNAV.
40 NM with DME; 50 NM with RNAV.
50 NM regardless of method.

items.question_text: A facility has a written agreement with the local flight inspection unit that all traffic and intention exchanges will be handled through the operations supervisor rather than by direct controller-pilot contact. A flight inspection aircraft enters the sector. Must the controller maintain direct contact with the pilot?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-1, maintain direct contact and exchange traffic and intention information 'unless otherwise agreed to.' The written agreement creates an exception — the controller follows the agreed-upon alternative coordination method rather than direct pilot contact.

choices.text: No. The 'unless otherwise agreed to' qualifier permits an alternative coordination method when a written agreement exists.
Yes. Direct pilot contact is mandatory for all flight inspection operations regardless of agreements.
No, but only because flight inspection aircraft operating under an agreement are treated as routine IFR traffic.
Yes, unless the pilot specifically requests relay-only communication through the supervisor.

items.question_text: A flight inspection aircraft requests a clearance that conflicts with three departure releases scheduled in the next 4 minutes. The controller asks the pilot to accept a 10-minute delay so the departures can be released first. No emergency exists. Is the delay request permissible?
items.explanation: Per 9-1-2a, the controller must clear the aircraft 'according to pilot request as soon as practicable' and must not ask the pilot to deviate from planned action 'except to preclude an emergency situation.' A routine sequencing delay is not an emergency — the controller must clear the aircraft as requested rather than impose a delay for departure releases.

choices.text: Yes. Routine departure sequencing takes priority over flight inspection clearance requests.
No. A routine sequencing delay is not an emergency situation, so the controller must not ask the pilot to deviate from the planned action.
Yes, but only if the delay is 5 minutes or less and the pilot does not object.
No, but only because flight inspection aircraft have unconditional priority over all other traffic.

items.question_text: A military aircraft's flight plan does not contain 'dangerous cargo' or 'inert devices' in the remarks, but the pilot states 'inert devices' during initial radio contact. Does special handling for dangerous materials apply?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-1a, special handling applies when the words appear in the flight plan remarks OR when the pilot uses the words in radio communication. Either trigger alone activates the special handling requirement — both are not required.

choices.text: No. Both the flight plan remarks AND radio communication must contain the trigger words for special handling to apply.
No. Radio communication alone is never sufficient; the flight plan remarks must always contain the trigger words.
Yes. The pilot's use of the words in radio communication is sufficient to trigger special handling even without flight plan notation.
Yes, but only if the aircraft is also carrying materials classified as dangerous under military regulations.

items.question_text: A law enforcement aircraft conducting surveillance requests communication on a separate frequency. The sector has only one available frequency and is moderately busy. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12a1, the controller must communicate with law enforcement aircraft on a separate and unique communications frequency 'whenever possible.' The qualifier 'whenever possible' means the controller is not required to create a separate frequency when resources do not permit it, but should do so when feasible.

choices.text: Refuse the request because a separate frequency is only available when the sector has no other traffic.
Immediately create a separate frequency by splitting the sector, regardless of staffing or equipment constraints.
Grant the request but instruct the law enforcement aircraft to monitor both frequencies simultaneously.
Provide a separate frequency whenever possible — if resources do not permit it, the obligation does not apply.

items.question_text: A supersonic flight plan above FL 600 is filed 10 hours before the proposed departure time. The originating ARTCC determines the route is straightforward and no special coordination is needed. Can the originating ARTCC waive the 16-hour advance filing requirement?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-14a, flight plans involving supersonic flight are required 16 hours in advance, but 'the originating ARTCC, where the flight plan is first filed, may waive the 16-hour advance filing requirement.' The waiver authority rests solely with the originating ARTCC.

choices.text: Yes. The originating ARTCC may waive the 16-hour advance filing requirement.
No. The 16-hour requirement is absolute and cannot be waived by any facility.
Yes, but only the Command Center can approve the waiver, not the originating ARTCC.
No. Waiver requires coordination with and approval from all ARTCCs along the route of flight.

items.question_text: A pilot's intended landing airfield is within a forecast hazardous radiation area. The controller informs the pilot of this. What must the controller then request from the pilot?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-16b, when an airfield of intended landing lies within a reported or forecast hazardous radiation area, the controller must inform the pilot AND 'request the pilot to advise his/her intentions.' The pilot retains decision authority on whether to land there.

choices.text: Request the pilot to divert to the nearest unaffected airfield immediately.
Request the pilot to advise his or her intentions regarding the landing.
Request the pilot to hold outside the area until the radiation hazard dissipates.
Request the pilot to declare an emergency before proceeding to the affected airfield.

items.question_text: A SAMP aircraft requests a climb from FL 280 to FL 340 for a sampling mission. The sector has moderate traffic at adjacent altitudes, and approving the request would require reclearing two other IFR aircraft. What authority does the controller have to accommodate the SAMP request?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, the controller must 'approve inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes to the maximum extent possible' and 'other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that requests by SAMP aircraft are approved.' The controller has explicit authority to reclear other IFR traffic to prioritize SAMP mission requests.

choices.text: Deny the request because other IFR aircraft already occupy the adjacent altitudes and cannot be moved.
Approve the request only if the two other aircraft volunteer to change altitude without a controller-initiated reclearance.
Reclear the other IFR aircraft to accommodate the SAMP aircraft's request to the maximum extent possible.
Place the SAMP aircraft in a holding pattern until the other aircraft have cleared the requested altitudes on their own.

items.question_text: A stationary ALTRV has been issued for a space launch, creating an Aircraft Hazard Area (AHA). A small airport that normally receives IFR approach services lies within the AHA boundary. No real-time start/end notifications are being provided to the affected facility. What is the correct action?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-2d2, the controller must not provide ATC services to aircraft at airports that lie within an AHA unless real-time notifications of the actual start and end of activity are provided via ATO Space Operations coordination. Without those notifications, ATC services to that airport are suspended for the duration of the ALTRV.

choices.text: Continue providing normal IFR approach services to the airport since the ALTRV is for space operations, not aviation.
Provide ATC services only to IFR aircraft; VFR aircraft may operate without restriction.
Provide services only during the launch window itself; the airport may operate normally at all other times.
Do not provide ATC services to aircraft at airports within the AHA without real-time start/end notifications.

items.question_text: An LOA permits transit of a Restricted Area with using agency coordination. The using agency instructs you to clear the aircraft at 5,000 feet through the area, but you have nonparticipating IFR traffic at 5,000 feet that cannot be moved in time. What must you do?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-4b, if unable to comply with the using agency's instructions or clearances, clear the aircraft in accordance with 9-3-2, Separation Minima. This means treating the aircraft as a nonparticipant and applying the standard separation buffer from the SUA boundary rather than attempting a transit you cannot safely execute.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft in accordance with the nonparticipant separation minima in paragraph 9-3-2.
Clear the aircraft through the area at 5,000 feet anyway since the using agency has authorized the transit.
Hold the aircraft outside the area until the nonparticipating traffic has passed, then proceed with the using agency's instructions.
Request the using agency to temporarily deactivate the Restricted Area for the transit.

items.question_text: A civil-operated Air Force type aircraft requests radar vectoring to a jettison drop area. The controller checks facility records and finds no written agreement with this operator. Can vectoring service be provided?
items.explanation: Per 9-5-1, civil operators and civil aircraft may receive vectoring service for jettisoning external stores only when a written agreement is in effect. Unlike USAF/ANG/Air Force Reserve aircraft (which may receive service at any time), civil operators require the written agreement that relieves the FAA of liability.

choices.text: Yes. All aircraft carrying external stores may use the drop area regardless of military or civil status.
No. Civil operators require a written agreement to be in effect before vectoring service can be provided.
Yes, but only if the civil aircraft declares an emergency before jettisoning.
No. Jettison drop areas are only available to U.S. military aircraft in all circumstances.

items.question_text: A parachute operation is authorized in Class B airspace within the designated jump area. The controller observes that a developing weather cell will move through the jump area in approximately 15 minutes. What authority does the controller have?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-2c, the controller may 'impose, as necessary, any conditions and restrictions which in your judgment would promote the safety of the operation.' This provides broad discretionary authority — the controller can restrict or condition the jump operation based on safety concerns such as approaching weather.

choices.text: No special authority beyond what the pilot requests — the controller must allow the jump to proceed as planned.
The controller may only impose restrictions when the jump aircraft explicitly asks for guidance on safety conditions.
Impose conditions and restrictions as necessary, including suspending the operation, based on the controller's safety judgment.
The controller must defer to the jump aircraft commander on all safety decisions and cannot independently restrict operations.

items.question_text: A controller hears a pilot transmit 'Mayday, we've lost an engine' just once without repeating the word. The pilot then describes the situation. How should the controller treat this call?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-1b states the word Mayday should be used 'preferably repeated three times.' 'Preferably' makes the repetition advisory, not mandatory. A single Mayday still declares a Distress condition. Additionally, 10-1-1c requires that even without the word Mayday or Pan-Pan, if you believe an emergency exists, you handle it as an emergency. The controller must treat this as a distress situation.

choices.text: Treat it as a Distress condition because a single Mayday suffices and the described emergency confirms the situation.
Treat it as a routine transmission since Mayday must be repeated exactly three times to constitute a valid emergency declaration.
Ask the pilot to repeat Mayday two more times before initiating emergency procedures.
Treat it as an Urgency condition since the pilot did not follow the preferred repetition procedure.

items.question_text: A controller receives a transmission from a pilot describing a serious fuel leak and requests immediate vectors to the nearest airport, but the pilot never uses the words 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan.' What principle governs the controller's response?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-1c states that if Mayday or Pan-Pan are not used but you believe an emergency or urgent situation exists, handle it as though it were an emergency. The controller's belief about the situation—not the pilot's choice of words—triggers the emergency response obligation. The fuel leak description provides sufficient basis to believe an emergency exists.

choices.text: The controller must ask the pilot to state either 'Mayday' or 'Pan-Pan' before emergency handling can begin.
The controller should handle it as an emergency based on the described circumstances, regardless of the pilot's word choice.
The controller can only provide routine services since the pilot declined to declare an emergency formally.
The controller must transfer the aircraft to a supervisor frequency for an emergency determination.

items.question_text: An Emergency Autoland system activates and transmits only the aircraft callsign and activation notice—omitting position, intended airport, and ETE. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: The note to 10-2-1b states that Emergency Autoland systems may not provide all required information. Per 10-1-2a, the controller should use information provided or solicit more as necessary. The controller must work with available information to develop an appropriate course of action, not wait for complete data that may never arrive.

choices.text: Wait for the system to retransmit with all five required data items before taking any action.
Disregard the transmission since it is incomplete and therefore unreliable.
Use the information provided and solicit additional information as necessary to assist the aircraft.
Treat it as a false alarm and continue normal operations.

items.question_text: A pilot of a distressed aircraft requests a specific diversion airport, but the controller believes a different airport would be a better choice due to closer emergency equipment. What is the controller's proper course of action?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-2a instructs controllers to provide assistance consistent with the pilot's requests. If the controller believes an alternative course of action may prove more beneficial, the proper response is to transmit a recommendation—not override the pilot's decision. The reference to 14 CFR §91.3 reinforces that the pilot in command has final authority. The controller recommends; the pilot decides.

choices.text: Override the pilot's request and issue a clearance to the airport with closer emergency equipment.
Decline to assist until the pilot and controller agree on the diversion airport.
Transfer communications to the supervisor to resolve the disagreement.
Provide assistance consistent with the pilot's request while transmitting the recommendation for the alternative airport.

items.question_text: A controller is working a busy sector with multiple aircraft when a pilot reports a cabin pressurization problem. The pilot has not declared an emergency and says he is 'managing the situation.' What level of assistance must the controller provide?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-3 requires maximum assistance to aircraft in distress. However, the controller may enlist outside radar and emergency resources 'when the pilot requests or when you deem necessary.' The mandate for maximum assistance is unconditional for aircraft in distress. The question is whether this aircraft—with a pressurization problem but no emergency declaration—qualifies. Per 10-1-1c, if the controller believes an emergency exists, it must be handled as an emergency.

choices.text: Provide maximum assistance because the circumstances indicate a potential emergency, and the controller may enlist resources when deemed necessary.
Provide only routine services since the pilot explicitly stated he is managing the situation and did not request assistance.
Wait until the pilot declares an emergency or requests specific assistance before taking any action beyond normal ATC service.
Enlist all available radar and emergency facilities immediately since maximum assistance is always mandatory regardless of circumstances.

items.question_text: A controller is handling an aircraft in distress when the adjacent sector controller offers to take over the emergency because they have more experience with that aircraft type. Should the original controller transfer the emergency?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-4a permits transfer of emergency-handling responsibility only 'when you feel better handling of the emergency will result.' The subjective standard is the transferring controller's judgment about whether the emergency will be better handled. The adjacent controller's experience may or may not justify the transfer—the test is whether better handling will result, not merely who has more experience.

choices.text: Transfer immediately since the other controller has more experience with that aircraft type.
Retain responsibility unless the controller handling the emergency feels that better handling will result from a transfer.
Transfer is mandatory when another controller volunteers because workload sharing is required during emergencies.
Let the pilot in command decide which controller should handle the emergency.

items.question_text: Controllers are evaluating whether to have a non-SAR aircraft escort an aircraft in distress. The maneuver would require both aircraft to fly through an overcast cloud layer. What special consideration applies?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-4e requires evaluating the close formation required by both aircraft, with special consideration if the maneuver takes the aircraft through clouds. Close formation flight in instrument conditions dramatically increases risk. This evaluation precedes and is separate from the pilot-familiarity check required by 10-1-4f.

choices.text: The escort aircraft must be IFR-certified and on an IFR flight plan before entering clouds.
Escort through clouds is prohibited under any circumstances for non-SAR aircraft.
Special consideration must be given to the close formation requirement, with the cloud penetration adding significant risk to the maneuver.
Only the distressed aircraft's cloud clearance requirements matter since the escort is providing a service.

items.question_text: An aircraft accident has occurred outside the airport perimeter fence. Emergency vehicles responding to the scene need to cross an active taxiway on the airport proper to reach the accident site. What traffic control obligation applies?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-6a applies the traffic-control requirement not only to emergencies on the airport proper but also when routes within the airport proper are required for movement of local emergency equipment going to or from an emergency outside the airport proper. The controller must control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in the areas where emergency response movement is occurring.

choices.text: No traffic control action is required since the emergency occurred outside the airport proper.
Only ground vehicle traffic needs to be controlled; air traffic is unaffected by an off-airport emergency.
Close the entire airport until emergency vehicles have cleared the movement area.
Control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts in areas needed for emergency vehicle movement, even though the emergency is outside the airport proper.

items.question_text: A military fighter-type aircraft at 1,500 feet declares an inflight engine emergency. The controller needs the aircraft's position but it is not visible on radar. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-7a instructs controllers to hold radio transmissions to a minimum and avoid frequency and transponder code changes for military fighter-type aircraft during inflight emergencies, especially at low altitude. The principle is that the pilot's full attention is required to maintain aircraft control. However, when the aircraft's position is unknown and it is not on radar, obtaining position is essential for safety—the controller must balance minimum radio transmissions against the operational need for position information.

choices.text: Request position only—this is essential safety information—while minimizing other transmissions and avoiding frequency or code changes.
Avoid all radio transmissions to the fighter and rely on other aircraft to relay information.
Request position, assign a new transponder code, and change frequency to a clearer channel to ensure reliable communication.
Wait for the pilot to report position without prompting, since any transmission could distract from aircraft control.

items.question_text: A controller has obtained aircraft identification and type and the nature of the emergency from a pilot reporting an engine failure. The pilot has not yet stated his desires. When may the controller begin assistance?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-1a states the three minimum required items for inflight emergencies are aircraft identification and type, nature of the emergency, and pilot's desires. However, the paragraph also says to 'start assistance as soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act' and that 'information requirements will vary, depending on the existing situation.' The controller can begin acting on the first two items while soliciting the pilot's desires concurrently, especially when the nature of the emergency (engine failure) clearly indicates the need for immediate action.

choices.text: Yes, all three minimum items must be collected before any action can begin.
No, begin assistance with the information obtained while concurrently soliciting the pilot's desires, since the nature of the emergency makes clear that immediate action is needed.
Assume the pilot desires vectors to the nearest airport and act accordingly without waiting for confirmation.
Only the nature of the emergency matters for beginning assistance; identification and pilot desires can be obtained later.

items.question_text: An Emergency Autoland system has declared an emergency for an aircraft. The controller transmits several queries to obtain supplementary information but receives no response. What should the controller conclude?
items.explanation: Note 2 to 10-2-1b states that if an emergency has been declared by an Emergency Autoland system, transmissions to the aircraft may go unanswered. The system may be transmitting autonomously and the pilot may be incapacitated or unable to respond. The controller should use the information the system does provide to develop an appropriate course of action, not assume an emergency doesn't exist because there's no voice response.

choices.text: The lack of response means the Emergency Autoland activation was likely a false alarm and can be disregarded.
Treat the situation as a communications failure and apply lost-comm procedures under 10-4-4.
Transmissions may go unanswered when the emergency was declared by an Emergency Autoland system; proceed using available information.
The aircraft must be considered hijacked since the pilot is not responding.

items.question_text: An aircraft has declared an emergency and is communicating well with the controller on the assigned sector frequency. A supervisor suggests moving the aircraft to 121.5 MHz to free the sector frequency. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-2 states that although 121.5 and 243.0 MHz are emergency frequencies, it might be best to keep the aircraft on the initial contact frequency, and frequencies should be changed only when there is a valid reason. Maintaining established communications takes priority over frequency management. Freeing the sector frequency for other traffic is not a valid reason to risk disrupting emergency communications.

choices.text: Move the aircraft to 121.5 MHz since that is the designated emergency frequency and is therefore the most appropriate channel.
Move the aircraft to 121.5 MHz to free the sector frequency, which constitutes a valid operational reason for a frequency change.
Keep the aircraft on the current frequency because communications are working well and there is no valid reason to change.
Split the difference by having the aircraft monitor both frequencies simultaneously.

items.question_text: A controller is working an aircraft in emergency with poor radar reception. The aircraft is currently in IMC at 4,000 feet with a 2,500-foot MEA in the area. Weather at higher altitudes is clear. What altitude recommendation may the controller make?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-4 permits the controller to recommend maintaining or increasing altitude to improve communications or radar, when considered necessary and if weather and circumstances permit. The MEA is 2,500 feet and the aircraft is at 4,000, so there is terrain clearance for a higher altitude. Clear weather above supports the recommendation. The note about volcanic ash (avoiding climb in ash clouds where terrain permits) does not apply here since there is no ash cloud.

choices.text: No altitude recommendation can be made because the aircraft is in IMC.
Only a descent can be recommended to improve radar reception, per standard radar coverage principles.
Recommend maintaining current altitude exactly since any change could disrupt communications during an emergency.
Recommend a higher altitude to improve radar reception since weather permits and terrain clearance is available.

items.question_text: An en route controller sees an emergency transponder code 7700 appear on an aircraft's data block, causing the emergency indicator to blink. No voice communication has been received from the aircraft. Must the controller consider an emergency to exist?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-5e states that an emergency transponder code displayed or reported is a trigger to consider that an aircraft emergency exists and inform the RCC or ARTCC. The note confirms ERAM displays code 7700 with a blinking emergency indicator. No voice declaration is required—the squawk code alone is a sufficient trigger.

choices.text: Yes, a displayed emergency transponder code is itself a trigger to consider an emergency exists and notify the RCC or ARTCC.
No, the controller must wait for voice confirmation from the pilot before treating it as an emergency.
Only if the code 7700 is accompanied by a loss of radar contact does it trigger emergency consideration.
Code 7700 requires the controller to ask the pilot to confirm the emergency before taking any action.

items.question_text: A USAF tower controller sees an IFR aircraft unexpectedly lose both radar contact and radio communications. Which agency must the controller notify?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-5 requires considering an emergency exists and informing the RCC or ARTCC when the listed triggers occur. The note specifies that USAF facilities are only required to notify the ARTCC—not the RCC. This is an explicit exception to the general rule that either the RCC or ARTCC must be informed.

choices.text: Both the RCC and the ARTCC, consistent with the general rule for all facilities.
Only the ARTCC, because USAF facilities have a specific exemption from notifying the RCC.
Only the RCC, since the loss of both radar and communications indicates a likely rescue situation.
Neither—USAF facilities handle emergencies internally and are exempt from all external notification requirements.

items.question_text: A pilot verbally reports a hijacking. The controller assigns code 7500 and the pilot acknowledges the code assignment and then transmits nothing further. What must the controller assume?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-6a1 states that should the pilot acknowledge assignment of code 7500 without further communication, or fail to acknowledge or communicate further, assume that the flight is being subject to hijack. The acknowledgment without further communication is treated the same as a failure to acknowledge—both lead to the assumption of hijack. The controller must then follow the communication restrictions in 10-2-6a2.

choices.text: The acknowledgment confirms the pilot heard the instruction but nothing further can be assumed about the hijacking status.
The acknowledgment indicates the situation is under control; continue normal communications with the aircraft.
Assume the flight is being subject to hijack because acknowledgment without further communication is treated the same as failure to communicate.
Request the pilot confirm the hijacking verbally before making any assumptions.

items.question_text: A controller is providing radar assistance to a VFR-only pilot who has entered IMC. The pilot is currently in a left turn in IFR conditions at 3,500 feet. The controller wants to get the aircraft to VFR conditions at 5,500 feet. What technique should the controller use?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-9d instructs controllers to avoid requiring a climb or descent while in a turn if in IFR conditions. The pilot is not IFR-qualified and is in IMC in a turn—combining a climb with the turn adds spatial disorientation risk. The controller should have the pilot roll out wings-level first, then initiate the climb to VFR conditions.

choices.text: Issue an immediate climb to 5,500 feet while continuing the turn to reach VFR conditions as quickly as possible.
Descend to a lower altitude while in the turn since descending is safer than climbing for a VFR-only pilot.
Continue the turn at the current altitude until the aircraft is pointed toward VFR conditions, then advise a climb.
Have the pilot level the wings and establish straight flight, then initiate the climb to reach VFR conditions.

items.question_text: A bomb threat is received that is 'general in nature'—it mentions an explosive device at the airport but does not reference any specific aircraft. How should the controller categorize and handle this threat?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-11a distinguishes between threats 'general in nature' and those 'targeted against a specific aircraft.' A general threat is handled as 'Suspicious Activity,' while the specific four-step procedure (advise pilot, inform about explosives expert, ask about altitude equalization, handle as emergency) applies only when the threat targets a specific aircraft and the controller is in contact with it. The supervisor notification requirement applies to both types.

choices.text: Apply all four bomb-threat actions to every aircraft on the frequency since the threat could affect any of them.
Disregard the threat because without a specific aircraft identified, no ATC action is required.
Treat it identically to a targeted threat since any bomb threat requires the full emergency response procedure.
Handle it as 'Suspicious Activity' because the threat is general in nature and not targeted against a specific aircraft.

items.question_text: While handling a bomb threat on a specific aircraft, the pilot asks 'What should I do about the bomb?' The controller has already informed the pilot that an FAA aviation explosives expert is available. What must the controller NOT do now?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-11a6 prohibits the controller from suggesting what actions the pilot should take concerning a bomb. When a pilot requests or appears to need technical assistance, the controller must obtain the specified information (aircraft type/series/model, precise location/description of device, other pertinent details) and notify the supervisor, who will contact the FAA aviation explosives expert. The expert provides the recommendations—the controller must not fill that role.

choices.text: Suggest any specific action concerning the bomb—the controller must instead obtain descriptive information and have the supervisor contact the explosives expert.
Suggest that the pilot descend to equalize cabin pressure with outside air, since that is a standard bomb-threat precaution.
Ask the pilot about fuel remaining and persons on board, since those are supplementary emergency information items.
Coordinate with other sectors, since bomb threat procedures are handled entirely by the supervisor.

items.question_text: A bomb threat is received for an aircraft parked at an airport without an operating control tower. The pilot says he wants to take off immediately. What should ATC recommend?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-11b1 states that if the aircraft is at an airport where tower control or FSS advisory service is not available, or if the pilot ignores the threat at any airport, ATC should recommend that takeoff be delayed until the pilot or aircraft operator establishes that a bomb is not aboard per 14 CFR Part 121. However, if the pilot insists on taking off and the operation will not adversely affect other traffic, the controller must issue or relay an ATC clearance. The controller recommends delay but cannot prevent takeoff.

choices.text: Issue a takeoff clearance immediately since ATC cannot override pilot-in-command authority during emergencies.
Recommend delaying takeoff until the pilot or operator establishes no bomb is aboard; if the pilot insists and traffic permits, issue the clearance.
Deny takeoff clearance because a bomb threat at a non-towered airport creates an unacceptable safety risk for all operations.
Transfer responsibility to the nearest FSS since the airport lacks an operating control tower.

items.question_text: A pilot reports a green laser illumination at 3,000 feet over a specific VOR. The controller issues the first warning broadcast. How long must general caution warnings continue, and at what interval?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-14a requires broadcasting a general caution warning on all appropriate frequencies every five minutes for 20 minutes following the last report. The clock runs from the last report—not the first. If new reports arrive during the broadcast period, the 20-minute window resets from the most recent report.

choices.text: Every 10 minutes for 30 minutes following the first report.
Once every 15 minutes for one hour following the initial event.
Every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the last report.
A single broadcast is sufficient; repetition is at the controller's discretion.

items.question_text: A terminal facility receives a laser illumination report. Beyond the general caution warning broadcast, what additional transmission requirement applies specifically to terminal facilities?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-14b requires terminal facilities to include reported unauthorized laser illumination events on the ATIS broadcast for one hour following the last report, including the time, location, altitude, color, and direction of the laser as reported by the pilot. This ATIS requirement is in addition to the general 5-minute/20-minute frequency broadcast in 10-2-14a. En route facilities do not have this ATIS obligation.

choices.text: Terminal facilities must issue a NOTAM within 30 minutes of the laser report.
Terminal facilities must contact the FBI field office since laser illumination of aircraft is a federal crime.
No additional requirement exists beyond the general 5-minute warning broadcasts.
Terminal facilities must include the event on the ATIS for one hour after the last report, with time, location, altitude, color, and direction.

items.question_text: An Emergency Autoland system selects an airport and advises ATC. The controller notices a NOTAM indicating the selected runway is closed for construction with equipment on the runway. What should the controller recognize about the Emergency Autoland system's selection?
items.explanation: The note to 10-2-15a states the Emergency Autoland system does not consider closed runways, equipment on the runway, construction, or other possible airport hazards when selecting an airport. The controller must apply the seven factors from 10-2-15a—including airport conditions—and the guidance from 10-2-16 in conjunction with the automated source. The controller may need to recommend a different airport when the system's selection is unsuitable.

choices.text: The system does not account for closed runways, equipment, or construction; the controller must apply the seven airport-recommendation factors alongside the automated selection.
The system's selection is definitive and the controller must clear the aircraft to that airport regardless of NOTAMs.
The controller should cancel the Emergency Autoland and manually vector the aircraft since the system's selection is unreliable.
Closed runways are automatically excluded by Emergency Autoland systems per manufacturer requirements.

items.question_text: An aircraft reports entering a volcanic ash cloud at FL330 and indicates a distress situation. The aircraft is a turboprop. What clearance restriction applies to this aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-18b2 prohibits initiating climb clearances to turbine-powered aircraft until the aircraft has exited the ash cloud. While the note specifically discusses high-bypass turbofan engines, the rule text says 'turbine-powered aircraft'—which includes turboprops. The restriction is based on the risk that increasing engine power (as in a climb) increases ash ingestion damage. The controller must also consider the aircraft in an emergency situation (10-2-18b1) and not provide escape vectors without pilot concurrence (10-2-18b3).

choices.text: No climb restriction applies because turboprops have different engine ingestion characteristics than turbofans.
Do not initiate any climb clearance until the aircraft has exited the ash cloud, because the restriction applies to all turbine-powered aircraft.
Issue an immediate climb to exit the ash cloud faster, since the distress situation makes expeditious action the priority.
Only turbofan aircraft are restricted from climbing; turboprops may climb at the controller's discretion.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft was given a clearance void time of 1430Z. At 1505Z, neither communications nor radar contact can be established with the aircraft. What is the aircraft's status and what action is required?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-3-1a states an aircraft is overdue when neither communications nor radar contact can be established and 30 minutes have passed since its clearance void time (among other triggers). At 1505Z, more than 30 minutes have elapsed since 1430Z. The controller must initiate ALNOT procedures. The 30-minute clock starts from the void time, not from when the controller first noticed the absence of contact.

choices.text: The aircraft is overdue; initiate ALNOT procedures since more than 30 minutes have passed since the clearance void time with no communications or radar contact.
The aircraft is not yet overdue because 30 minutes must pass from when the controller first attempted contact, not from the void time.
Wait until 30 minutes after the filed ETA before considering the aircraft overdue.
Only the destination airport can determine overdue status for IFR aircraft operating under a void time.

items.question_text: A controller experiences an abnormal, simultaneous loss of both radar contact and radio communications with a VFR aircraft receiving flight following services. How should the controller treat this aircraft, and when?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-3-1b requires considering an aircraft to be in emergency status and initiating ALNOT procedures immediately when there is an abnormal simultaneous loss of radar and communications with an IFR aircraft or VFR/SVFR aircraft receiving flight following. The key phrase is 'immediately'—the 30-minute waiting period that applies to the overdue triggers in 10-3-1a does not apply. The abnormal simultaneous loss of both radar and communications is itself the trigger.

choices.text: Wait 30 minutes before considering the aircraft overdue, then initiate ALNOT procedures.

Consider the aircraft in emergency status and initiate ALNOT procedures immediately because an abnormal simultaneous loss of radar and communications with a flight-following aircraft is an immediate trigger.
VFR aircraft on flight following are not eligible for ALNOT procedures since they are not IFR.
Only the radar loss matters; try to reestablish communications for 30 minutes before escalating.

items.question_text: An ARTCC issues an ALNOT for an overdue aircraft. The ALNOT is distributed to all centers and Area B circuits along a corridor 50 miles on either side of the route from the last reported position to destination. Under what circumstances may the ALNOT be expanded beyond this corridor?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-3-4a specifies the default ALNOT distribution is generally 50 miles on either side of the route of flight from the last reported position to destination. However, the paragraph also states that at the recommendation of the RCC or at the issuing controller's discretion, the ALNOT may be issued to cover the maximum range of the aircraft. This expansion considers the possibility the aircraft deviated from its route.

choices.text: The ALNOT must remain within the 50-mile corridor regardless of circumstances; expansion requires a new ALNOT.
The ALNOT automatically expands to maximum range after one hour if the aircraft has not been located.
At RCC recommendation or at the controller's discretion, the ALNOT may expand to cover the aircraft's maximum range.
Only the RCC can authorize ALNOT expansion; the controller has no independent authority to expand coverage.

items.question_text: An ALNOT has been active for 45 minutes and the aircraft has not been located. The estimated fuel exhaustion time was 20 minutes ago. Under which condition, if any, does responsibility for further search transfer to the RCC?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-3-5 lists three alternative conditions for transferring search responsibility to the RCC—any one triggers the transfer. Condition (a): 30 minutes after estimated fuel exhaustion time (only 20 minutes have elapsed—not yet met). Condition (b): aircraft not located within one hour after ALNOT issuance (45 minutes—not yet met). Condition (c): ALNOT search completed with negative results (unknown from the scenario). None of the conditions are satisfied yet, so transfer is premature.

choices.text: Yes, because the aircraft hasn't been located after 45 minutes, which is long enough to presume the search has failed.
Yes, because fuel exhaustion triggers immediate transfer regardless of other conditions.
Transfer is automatic whenever any one of the three conditions is even partially met, so transfer now.
Not yet—neither 30 minutes post-fuel-exhaustion nor one hour post-ALNOT-issuance has elapsed, so no transfer condition is satisfied.

items.question_text: An ARTCC controller needs to provide the last known position of an overdue aircraft to the RCC. The formal radar data extraction tool will take approximately 10 minutes to produce precise coordinates, but the RCC needs an initial position immediately to begin SAR operations. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-3-6b states that if necessary to prevent undue delay, the controller must utilize any available method to determine the initial latitude and longitude, then follow up as soon as possible with a formal position using the appropriate data extraction tools. The procedure creates a two-tier system: an immediate rough position so SAR can begin, followed by a precise formal position for accuracy. The controller should also solicit other aircraft near the distressed aircraft's route per 10-3-6c, and forward all information to the RCC or ARTCC as appropriate per 10-3-6d.

choices.text: Provide an initial position using any available method to prevent delay, then follow up with the formal coordinates as soon as possible.
Wait the 10 minutes for the formal coordinates—SAR operations cannot begin without precise position data.
Provide only a general description of the last known area since SAR teams use their own position-finding equipment.
Ask the RCC to determine the position themselves since the formal extraction tool is temporarily unavailable.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft is overdue. The pilot's EFC time was 1515Z and the controller's current estimate for the NAVAID serving the destination is 1530Z, while the pilot's estimate was 1520Z. From what time does the 30-minute traffic restriction period begin?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-1d states that when the applicable time is the current estimate at the NAVAID serving the destination airport, use the later of the control facility's estimate or the pilot's estimate. Here, the controller's estimate (1530Z) is later than the pilot's (1520Z), so the 30-minute restriction begins from 1530Z. This ensures traffic is protected until the latest reasonable arrival time has passed.

choices.text: From 1520Z, using the pilot's estimate since the pilot has the best knowledge of the aircraft's performance.
From 1530Z, using the later of the two estimates to ensure traffic protection until the latest reasonable arrival time.
From 1515Z, using the EFC time since it was the last clearance issued to the pilot.
From 1525Z, averaging the two estimates since neither is definitively correct.

items.question_text: Two-way radio communications are lost with an IFR aircraft under a controller's jurisdiction. After 5 minutes, communications have not been reestablished. What additional step must the controller now take?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-4e states that if radio communications have not been reestablished with the aircraft after 5 minutes, the controller must consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity to be suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC per FAA Order JO 7610.4 and the supervisory notification requirements. This is an additional obligation beyond the ongoing efforts to reestablish communications (10-4-4a). The 5-minute mark triggers a specific security-reporting duty separate from continuing communication-recovery efforts.

choices.text: Continue only with communication-recovery efforts; the 5-minute mark has no special significance.
Immediately declare an emergency and initiate ALNOT procedures since 5 minutes of lost communication confirms an emergency.
Consider the activity suspicious and report to the OS/CIC, while continuing communication-recovery efforts.
Transfer the aircraft to the next sector since the controller's jurisdiction over a NORDO aircraft ends after 5 minutes.

items.question_text: After losing communications, a controller wants to use the aircraft's transponder to reestablish contact. The controller transmits 'REPLY NOT RECEIVED, SQUAWK IDENT.' The aircraft's target does not change. What should the controller try next?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-4c provides a sequence of transponder requests: request Mode 3/A IDENT, then request Code 7600, then request the aircraft change to stand-by.
