The controller should progress through these options, observing for the target to appear or disappear as evidence the pilot is receiving and complying. The prescribed phraseology for an unacknowledged instruction is 'REPLY NOT RECEIVED' followed by the instruction.

choices.text: Repeat the IDENT request until the pilot complies, since persistence is the key to reestablishing contact.
Conclude the aircraft's transponder is inoperative and abandon all transponder-based communication attempts.
Immediately broadcast a clearance to the filed alternate at the MEA without further communication attempts.
Progress to the next method: request the aircraft squawk Code 7600 and observe for the code change.

items.question_text: A debris response area (DRA) has been activated following a space launch mishap. An airborne aircraft is currently inside the DRA and requests a landing clearance at an airport that also lies within the DRA. How should ATC respond?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-5-2b2(b) states that for airborne aircraft already within an activated DRA, controllers shall not withhold landing or approach clearances. The prohibition in 10-5-2b3 against issuing takeoff clearances and departure releases applies to airports underlying the DRA but does not block landing aircraft that are already airborne within the DRA. The priority is to get aircraft on the ground safely, not to keep them airborne in a debris hazard area.

choices.text: Issue the landing clearance—the rule prohibits withholding landing or approach clearances from aircraft already airborne within the DRA.
Deny the landing clearance because airports underlying an active DRA cannot conduct any operations.
Issue the landing clearance only if the pilot declares an emergency, since DRA rules override normal landing procedures.
Vector the aircraft to an airport outside the DRA since all DRA-overlying airport operations are suspended.

items.question_text: An alert broadcast about a space vehicle mishap has been issued. Workload has been high and 20 minutes have passed since the last broadcast. What is the controller's obligation regarding reissuing the alert?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-5-2a1 states that when workload permits, the alert broadcast should be reissued approximately every 15 minutes. The qualifier 'when workload permits' means this is not an absolute requirement—higher-priority duties may take precedence. However, 20 minutes without a reissue while workload was high does not violate the rule; the controller should reissue as soon as workload allows.

choices.text: The controller has violated a mandatory 15-minute rebroadcast requirement and must issue it immediately regardless of workload.
Reissue the broadcast when workload permits—the 15-minute interval is approximate and subject to higher-priority duties.
The alert broadcast expires after 15 minutes; a new and different alert format must be used for any subsequent broadcast.
No further broadcasts are needed since the 15-minute window has passed and the hazard is considered diminished.

items.question_text: An oceanic aircraft was expected to report at 1200Z. At 1235Z, no communication has been received and one unsuccessful attempt to establish communication was made at 1210Z. What emergency phase exists?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-2a1 states an INCERFA exists when communication has not been received within 30 minutes after the time a communication should have been received OR after the time an unsuccessful attempt to establish communication was first made, whichever is earlier. The communication should have been received at 1200Z (35 minutes ago). The unsuccessful attempt was at 1210Z (25 minutes ago). The earlier time is 1200Z, and more than 30 minutes have elapsed since then. Thus an INCERFA exists.

choices.text: No emergency phase yet—only 25 minutes have passed since the unsuccessful communication attempt.
ALERFA exists—exceeding 30 minutes without communication automatically escalates to the alert phase.
INCERFA exists—more than 30 minutes have passed since the time communication should have been received, which is the earlier of the two trigger times.
DETRESFA exists—any missed position report in oceanic airspace triggers the distress phase.

items.question_text: An aircraft in oceanic airspace has been in the uncertainty phase (INCERFA) for some time. Attempts to establish communications and inquiries to other sources have all failed to reveal any information about the aircraft. What phase now exists?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-2b1 states an ALERFA exists following the uncertainty phase when subsequent attempts to establish communications or inquiries to other relevant sources have failed to reveal any information. This is the natural progression from INCERFA to ALERFA when communications and inquiries remain unsuccessful. ALERFA represents a higher level of concern—apprehension about safety—compared to INCERFA's mere concern.

choices.text: INCERFA continues until 60 minutes have passed, at which point it becomes ALERFA automatically.
The phase escalates directly to DETRESFA since multiple failed communication attempts indicate grave and imminent danger.
The phase returns to normal since an INCERFA that produces no information after an unspecified time is automatically canceled.
ALERFA now exists because the failure of communications attempts and inquiries following an INCERFA is an explicit trigger for the alert phase.

items.question_text: A military aircraft is operating under 'due regard' prerogative in oceanic airspace. Who bears the responsibility for alerting service for this aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-3e states that responsibility for alerting service for flight operations conducted under the 'due regard' or 'operational' prerogative of military aircraft is assumed by the military. The ACC may assume responsibility at the proposed time filed only when due regard operations are scheduled to end with aircraft filed under ICAO procedures and a letter of agreement so specifies. Absent an LOA, the military retains alerting responsibility throughout the due regard operation.

choices.text: The military assumes alerting responsibility for due regard operations; the ACC may assume it at a specified time only if an LOA so provides for aircraft transitioning to ICAO procedures.
The ACC serving the FIR always retains alerting responsibility regardless of the aircraft's operational status.
Alerting service is waived entirely for due regard operations since military aircraft have self-rescue capability.
Alerting responsibility is shared equally between the military and the ACC for all due regard operations.

items.question_text: A DETRESFA has been declared for an oceanic aircraft. The aircraft subsequently lands safely at its destination. What must happen to the DETRESFA?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-3h2 lists the three events that end a DETRESFA: the aircraft successfully lands, the RCC advises of a successful rescue, or the RCC advises of termination of SAR activities. In all cases, a cancellation message must be sent to the same stations that received the original DETRESFA message. The phase does not end automatically—a formal cancellation is required.

choices.text: The DETRESFA ends automatically when the aircraft lands; no further action is required.
The DETRESFA ends because the aircraft successfully landed, and a cancellation message must be sent to the same stations as the original DETRESFA message.
The DETRESFA continues until the RCC advises of SAR termination, regardless of whether the aircraft has landed.
The phase downgrades from DETRESFA to ALERFA and then to INCERFA before being fully resolved.

items.question_text: An aircraft over water requests information about sea conditions and the presence of surface vessels for a possible ditching. What resources should the controller use to obtain this information?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-4a states that weather, sea conditions, ditching information, and surface vessel assistance should be requested from the RCC. Additionally, an AMVER SURPIC should be requested if the aircraft asks or if the controller deems it beneficial. The AMVER Center can rapidly provide a SURPIC showing vessels in the area with predicted positions and characteristics. The controller has independent authority to request a SURPIC if beneficial, even without a pilot request.

choices.text: Request the information from the aircraft operator's dispatch office, which maintains the most current maritime data.
Only the pilot can request AMVER data; the controller must wait for a specific pilot request before contacting the AMVER Center.
Request weather, sea conditions, and ditching information from the RCC, and obtain an AMVER SURPIC if requested by the aircraft or deemed beneficial.
Contact the nearest Coast Guard station directly since surface vessel coordination is a Coast Guard responsibility.

items.question_text: A SAR aircraft has established visual contact with the distressed aircraft and both pilots concur to discontinue IFR separation. What clearance limit should be used for the SAR aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-5b requires clearing the SAR aircraft to a fixed clearance limit rather than to the aircraft in distress, which is a moving fix. Even after visual contact is established and IFR separation has been discontinued per 10-6-5a, the clearance limit practice remains—route clearances should be consistent with the distressed aircraft's route but issued to a fixed geographical point, not to the moving aircraft.

choices.text: Clear the SAR aircraft directly to the distressed aircraft's position since visual contact has been established and IFR separation is discontinued.
No clearance limit is needed once visual contact and concurrence are established since both aircraft are operating visually.
Clear to the distressed aircraft's filed destination since that is the only fixed point relevant to both aircraft.
Clear to a fixed clearance limit—the SAR aircraft must never be cleared to the distressed aircraft as a moving fix, regardless of separation status.

items.question_text: An en route controller receives a report of a ground missile emergency. The controller's sector includes VFR company aircraft operating in the vicinity. What notification action is required specifically for these VFR company aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-7-1a requires en route controllers to have alerting advisories issued by air carrier company radio stations for each VFR company aircraft which is or will be operating in the vicinity of the emergency. This is in addition to the FSS notification in 10-7-1b. The company radio station channel is used because VFR company aircraft may be monitoring company frequencies rather than ATC frequencies.

choices.text: Have alerting advisories issued by air carrier company radio stations for each VFR company aircraft operating or that will operate in the vicinity.
Contact each VFR company aircraft individually on the ATC frequency since direct controller-to-pilot communication is the most reliable method.
Only IFR aircraft need to be notified; VFR aircraft are responsible for their own separation from ground emergencies.
Issue a general broadcast on 121.5 MHz, which is sufficient to reach all VFR aircraft in the area.

items.question_text: A ground missile emergency is occurring. The notifying official suggests a lateral separation of 3 miles rather than the standard 1 mile. What separation minimum must ATC apply when rerouting IFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-7-2 states IFR and SVFR aircraft must be rerouted by the specified minima (1 mile lateral, 6,000 feet vertical) OR by greater minima when suggested by the notifying official. When the notifying official suggests greater minima, those greater values become the required minimum. The controller must use 3 miles lateral because the notifying official suggested it and it exceeds the baseline 1 mile.

choices.text: Use 1 mile lateral since that is the standard minimum stated in the rule; the notifying official's suggestion is advisory only.
Use 3 miles lateral because the notifying official's suggested greater minimum supersedes the standard 1 mile minimum.
Use an average of 2 miles as a compromise between the standard minimum and the notifying official's suggestion.
Only the notifying official can set separation minima; the controller must request a specific figure in writing before deviating from the 1-mile standard.

items.question_text: How do the separation minima for VFR aircraft near a ground missile emergency differ from those applied to IFR and SVFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 10-7-2 requires IFR/SVFR aircraft to be rerouted by the stated minima. Paragraph 10-7-3 requires VFR aircraft to be advised to avoid the emergency location by the same minima (1 mile laterally, 6,000 feet vertically). The difference is in the controller action: IFR/SVFR aircraft are proactively rerouted; VFR aircraft are advised of the hazard and the avoidance parameters. Both sets of minima can be increased when suggested by the notifying official.

choices.text: VFR aircraft use 3 miles laterally while IFR aircraft use 1 mile laterally.
VFR aircraft have no minimum—they are simply advised of the emergency location without specific separation values.
The minima are the same (1 mile laterally, 6,000 feet vertically), but IFR/SVFR aircraft are rerouted while VFR aircraft are advised to avoid.
IFR aircraft use 6,000 feet vertically but VFR aircraft use only 3,000 feet vertically due to their different operating rules.

items.question_text: A developing weather system is forecast to reduce arrival capacity in an OS/CIC's area of responsibility within the next hour. What does the OS/CIC need to do upon learning of this forecast?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2b1, the OS/CIC must keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised of situations or circumstances that may cause congestion or delays. The word 'may' triggers the obligation before delays materialize — waiting for confirmed congestion violates the proactive reporting duty. The notification must reach both the TMU and the affected sectors, not just the STMCIC.

choices.text: Keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised, because the situation may cause congestion or delays even though delays are not yet present.
Wait until the weather actually reduces capacity and delays become measurable before notifying anyone.
Notify only the STMCIC and allow them to determine who else requires the information.
Immediately issue miles-in-trail restrictions to all affected sectors without coordinating with the TMU.

items.question_text: How does the TBFM duty of an ATCT differ from that of an ARTCC or TRACON?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2d1 and e1, ARTCCs and TRACONs must support TBFM operations and monitor TBFM equipment to improve situational awareness. Per 11-1-2f1, ATCTs must monitor TBFM equipment to improve situational awareness for a system approach to TBM operations. ATCTs have a monitoring-only role; they are not required to support TBFM operations. This distinction reflects the different operational scope of terminal facilities versus en route and TRACON facilities in the traffic management system.

choices.text: All three facility types must both support TBFM operations and monitor TBFM equipment.
ATCTs must monitor TBFM equipment; ARTCCs and TRACONs must both support TBFM operations and monitor TBFM equipment.
ARTCCs must support and monitor; TRACONs and ATCTs are only required to monitor.
ATCTs must support TBFM operations; ARTCCs and TRACONs are only required to monitor.

items.question_text: The STMCIC operational briefing must cover meteorological conditions, staffing, equipment status, runways in use, AAR, TBM use, and TMIs. Which statement accurately describes the requirement for covering these topics?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a1, the briefing itself must be conducted at least once during the day and evening shifts, but the discussion topics are introduced with 'should include' — not 'must include.' This is a meaningful regulatory distinction: the briefing is mandatory, while the listed topics are strongly recommended but not each independently required at every briefing. Facility management may adjust the agenda based on operational relevance.

choices.text: Each of the listed topics must be discussed at every briefing, and failing to cover any single item violates the order.
The topics are optional suggestions; the STMCIC may choose to cover only the items they consider relevant that day.
The briefing must occur; the listed topics should be discussed. 'Should' indicates strong recommendation, not an absolute requirement for each topic at every briefing.
The topics become mandatory only when the conditions they describe have changed since the previous briefing was conducted.

items.question_text: When TBFM metering-time accuracy is affected by factors such as vectoring outside route conformance boundaries, trajectory ground-speed calculations, or TMU resequencing, what operational precaution is required?
items.explanation: Per the note at 11-1-3, TBFM accuracy is predicated on several factors including vectoring outside route conformance boundaries, trajectory ground-speed calculations, and TMU resequencing. When these factors are present, controllers must use caution to minimize impact on surrounding sector traffic, complexity levels, flight efficiencies, and user preferences. Suspending metering, applying the standard more aggressively, or transferring responsibility are not the prescribed responses.

choices.text: Suspend all TBFM metering for the affected sector until the TMU can verify accuracy of the generated times.
Apply the plus-or-minus-1-minute standard more strictly to compensate for the accuracy limitations.
Transfer metering responsibility for the affected flights to an adjacent sector where TBFM accuracy is unaffected.
Use caution to minimize impact on surrounding sector traffic, complexity levels, flight efficiencies, and user preferences.

items.question_text: Control responsibility within Canadian airspace must be _____ delegated to the FAA by the Transport Canada Aviation Group before the FAA applies its procedures there.
items.explanation: The delegation must be formal — this is the prerequisite condition in 12-1-1. Without formal delegation, the FAA has no control responsibility in Canadian airspace and the chapter's procedural framework does not activate. The word "formally" distinguishes an official transfer from ad hoc or informal arrangements.

choices.text: formally

items.question_text: In Canadian Class B airspace, ATC procedures pertinent to _____ flights must be applied to CVFR aircraft.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2b, ATC applies IFR procedures to CVFR aircraft. This means CVFR aircraft receive the same separation, sequencing, and control treatment as IFR aircraft — the pilot's visual responsibility to the ground does not reduce ATC's procedural obligations.

choices.text: IFR

items.question_text: In Canadian Class D airspace, VFR flights must establish two-way communications with the appropriate ATC agency _____ to entering the airspace.
items.explanation: The timing requirement is explicit: communications must be established prior to entry, not after penetrating the airspace. This distinguishes Class D from Class C (where a clearance is required) and Class E (where no special VFR requirements exist).

choices.text: prior

items.question_text: What defines Canadian Class F airspace?
items.explanation: Class F is airspace of defined dimensions established for specific activities. It serves two purposes (either or both): confining activities that must be contained because of their nature, and imposing limitations on aircraft not participating in those activities. The sub-types — Class F advisory and Class F restricted — correspond to these purposes. This is a uniquely Canadian airspace class with no U.S. equivalent.

choices.text: Airspace of defined dimensions within which activities must be confined because of their nature, or within which limitations are imposed upon aircraft operations that are not a part of those activities, or both.
Airspace where only military aircraft are permitted to operate without restriction.
Airspace where ATC provides advisory service only, with no authority to issue clearances or instructions to any aircraft.
Temporary airspace activated by NOTAM for air shows, rocket launches, and other special events only.

items.question_text: Which correctly states the flight rules permitted in Canadian Class A airspace versus Canadian Class B airspace?
items.explanation: Class A and Class B represent the most restrictive Canadian controlled airspace, but they differ in one critical way: Class A permits only IFR flights, while Class B permits IFR and CVFR flights. Ordinary VFR is prohibited in both. Confusing these classes could lead a controller to clear VFR traffic into Class A or fail to apply IFR procedures to a CVFR aircraft in Class B.

choices.text: Class A: IFR and CVFR only. Class B: IFR only.
Class A: IFR only. Class B: IFR and CVFR only.
Class A: IFR only. Class B: IFR, CVFR, and VFR.
Class A: IFR and CVFR. Class B: IFR, CVFR, and VFR with two-way communications.

items.question_text: In Canadian airspace, the prescribed phraseology for the substitute clearance used in lieu of VFR-on-top is "at least _____."
items.explanation: The exact prescribed phraseology is "at least 1,000 feet-on-top." This is the Canadian equivalent of the U.S. "VFR-on-top" clearance and must be spoken as written. The phrase communicates both the vertical requirement (1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation) and the pilot's weather responsibility.

choices.text: 1,000 feet-on-top

items.question_text: The Canadian separation rule requires ATC to apply a separation minimum between aircraft on IFR or CVFR clearances. Which aircraft type falls outside this obligation?
items.explanation: The separation obligation in 12-1-4 applies specifically and exclusively to aircraft operating under an IFR or CVFR clearance. An aircraft operating as ordinary VFR — without an IFR or CVFR clearance — is outside the scope of this separation requirement. This is a critical boundary: CVFR aircraft receive IFR-equivalent separation, but ordinary VFR aircraft do not, even in the same airspace volume.

choices.text: An aircraft operating under a CVFR clearance in excellent visual meteorological conditions.
An aircraft on an IFR clearance that has reported the preceding traffic in sight.
An aircraft operating as ordinary VFR without an IFR or CVFR clearance.
A CVFR aircraft that is maintaining continuous visual reference to the ground.

items.question_text: For a turbine-powered aircraft in Canadian airspace that loses communications after departure, the pilot maintains the last assigned altitude or flight level or the MEA, whichever is _____, until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit.
items.explanation: The pilot must maintain the higher of the two altitudes: the last assigned altitude/flight level or the minimum en route IFR altitude (MEA). This ensures terrain and obstruction clearance during the hold period. The "whichever is higher" comparison is a safety floor — the MEA may be higher than the assigned altitude if the assigned altitude was for a terminal procedure or an intermediate step-climb.

choices.text: higher

items.question_text: Under the Canadian radio-failure procedure, a turbine-powered aircraft cleared to a point other than its destination must maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) until _____ minutes beyond the clearance limit before proceeding at filed altitudes.
items.explanation: The hold period is 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit. This 10-minute interval provides time for ATC to recognize the lost-communications condition and clear the route ahead before the aircraft transitions to its filed altitude. The 10-minute value is prescriptive — it is not adjustable by the pilot or controller unless alternate instructions have been issued.

items.question_text: A pilot requests authorization for a parachute jump in delegated Canadian airspace but has not obtained permission from the appropriate Canadian authority. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: Do not authorize the jump without prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority. Traffic conditions, VFR status, or a pilot assurance do not replace that prerequisite.

choices.text: Authorize it if traffic permits and the pilot remains VFR.
Coordinate locally, then approve it without prior Canadian permission.
Authorize it after the pilot verbally accepts responsibility.
Do not authorize the jump until the required Canadian permission exists.

items.question_text: When an airport controller on duty approves an SVFR request in a Canadian control zone, the controller must specify the _____ during which SVFR flight is permitted.
items.explanation: The controller must specify the period of time during which SVFR is permitted. This time limitation is a required element of the SVFR approval — it gives ATC control over how long the SVFR operation may continue and allows planning around anticipated IFR traffic. Without a specified period, the approval is incomplete.

choices.text: period of time

items.question_text: A pilot without an IFR qualification wants to fly SVFR at night in a Canadian control zone in an aircraft lacking IFR equipment. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Canadian SVFR rules explicitly permit night SVFR without IFR pilot qualification and without IFR aircraft equipment. This is a significant difference from U.S. rules under 14 CFR 91.157, which require an IFR-rated pilot and an IFR-equipped aircraft for night SVFR. Controllers working Canadian airspace must not impose U.S. night SVFR requirements on operations governed by Canadian regulations.

choices.text: Yes — Canadian rules permit night SVFR without IFR pilot qualification or IFR aircraft equipment, which differs from U.S. requirements.
No — the pilot must hold an IFR rating for any night SVFR operation, consistent with U.S. rules.
No — the aircraft must be IFR-equipped for night SVFR, though the pilot may operate without an IFR rating.
Yes — but only if the pilot has logged at least 50 hours of night flight time in the preceding 12 months.

items.question_text: A controller wants to know whether an aircraft will enter restricted airspace in 20 minutes. Which EDST data source enables the system to model the aircraft's future position for this prediction?
items.explanation: EDST uses current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data together to derive expected trajectories. No single data source alone is sufficient—the trajectory model requires all four inputs. Track data provides the current position, but the future trajectory also depends on the planned route, expected winds, and how the aircraft type performs at its filed speeds and altitudes.

choices.text: Current plan data, forecast winds, aircraft performance characteristics, and track data together—the trajectory model requires all four inputs.
Track data alone, because the current radar position is the most reliable input for trajectory predictions.
Only the pilot-filed route and altitude; other data sources are supplementary and not required for trajectory derivation.
Forecast winds and aircraft performance characteristics are sufficient, because the current plan and track data are only used for conflict detection, not trajectory modeling.

items.question_text: A controller is evaluating a conflict probe alert between two aircraft in airspace where 3-mile reduced separation is authorized. The alert shows the aircraft will have 4 miles of separation. What must the controller recognize about this alert?
items.explanation: The note to 13-1-2 states conflict probe alerts are based on approved radar separation and do not account for instances where reduced separation is permitted (e.g., 3 mile airspace). If the controller is authorized to apply 3-mile separation, the 4-mile alert may not actually represent a conflict that would violate the applicable minimum. However, the controller must verify that all conditions for reduced separation are met before relying on that determination.

choices.text: The alert is definitive and the aircraft pair requires immediate action to increase separation to the standard 5 NM regardless of whether reduced separation has been authorized.
The alert may not reflect that reduced separation is authorized in this airspace, but the controller must still verify all conditions for the reduced minimum before concluding no conflict exists.
The alert can be ignored entirely because the conflict probe is not authorized to generate alerts in reduced-separation airspace.
The alert is valid only for the standard 5 NM radar minimum and must be accepted at face value for all aircraft regardless of applicable separation standards.

items.question_text: While scanning the EDST display, a controller notices a new automated notification indicator has appeared but the trainee on the team has not noticed it. What duty does this trigger?
items.explanation: 13-1-5(b) requires the sector team to actively scan EDST to identify automated notifications that require sector team action. This is an active scanning duty—notifications are not simply alerts that demand attention; the team must proactively look for them. The ACL is the primary source of flight data (13-1-5(a)), but the scanning requirement in (b) ensures notifications are identified and acted upon.

choices.text: No special duty applies; automated notifications are processed by the system and do not require controller identification.
The trainee should be assigned to scan EDST for automated notifications while the qualified controller handles other duties, because only one team member needs to perform this scan.
The sector team must actively scan EDST to identify automated notifications that require sector team action—this is a team duty, not an individual one.
The notification can wait until the automated system escalates it visually; active scanning is only required for conflict probe alerts under a different paragraph.

items.question_text: An ACL entry for an aircraft shows a Remarks indication. After reviewing the Remarks, the controller discovers a subsequent change to the Remarks field has been made by the previous sector. Must this change also be reviewed?
items.explanation: 13-1-5(c) requires two separate actions: when an ACL or DL entry has a Remarks indication, the Remarks field must be reviewed, AND changes to the Remarks field must also be reviewed. The second requirement is independent—even if the Remarks were already reviewed, the controller must review the change to ensure awareness of the updated information.

choices.text: No, because the Remarks indication only requires one initial review; subsequent changes are automatically tracked by the automation.
Only if the change was made by a different sector. If the same sector made the change, no further review is required.
No, because changes to the Remarks field are logged by EAS and do not require controller review unless the aircraft is also nonradar.
Yes, because the requirement to review changes to the Remarks field is a separate duty from the initial review of the Remarks indication.

items.question_text: A controller needs to annotate an ACL Free Text entry indicating that a pilot reported a TCAS Resolution Advisory. Which abbreviation source authorizes the correct marking for this entry?
items.explanation: 13-1-8(c)(2) authorizes the miscellaneous abbreviations in TBL 13-1-2, which includes 'RA' for Resolution Advisory (Pilot-reported TCAS event). The clearance abbreviations in TBL 13-1-1 cover clearance-related entries; the EDST equivalents in TBL 13-1-3 cover control information symbols. The TCAS RA abbreviation falls under the miscellaneous table.

choices.text: TBL 13-1-2 Miscellaneous Abbreviations, where RA is defined as Resolution Advisory for a pilot-reported TCAS event.
TBL 13-1-1 Clearance Abbreviations, because RA represents a type of clearance amendment.
TBL 13-1-3 EDST Equivalents, because RA is a control information symbol for radar advisory.
A locally approved abbreviation, because TCAS events are facility-specific and not covered by the standard tables.

items.question_text: Which of the following pieces of control information may be left unrecorded because it is captured by another authorized recording method?
items.explanation: 13-1-8(a) requires manual recording only for control information 'not otherwise recorded via automation recordings or voice recordings.' If the automation or voice recording system has already captured the information, manual recording is not required. The hierarchy is: automation or voice recordings are primary; manual recording fills the gap for information those systems do not capture.

choices.text: A verbal point out approval, which must always be annotated manually regardless of voice recording.
An altitude assignment issued verbally on a recorded frequency, since the voice recording captures it.
A Free Text Area entry, because Free Text entries are a form of manual recording that replaces the automation recording requirement.
Any control information transmitted by CPDLC, because data link transmissions are not considered automation recordings under this paragraph.

items.question_text: An aircraft in ATOP oceanic airspace is approaching a known area of high terrain on an island. Can ATOP provide decision support for separation between the aircraft and this terrain?
items.explanation: 13-2-1(a) states ATOP provides automated decision support tools to establish, monitor, and maintain separation between aircraft, and aircraft to airspace and terrain. This terrain separation scope is broader than ERAM EDST, which predicts conflicts only with special use or designated airspace. ATOP's oceanic/remote environment includes terrain considerations that domestic en route automation typically does not address.

choices.text: No. ATOP, like all en route automation, only provides separation support between aircraft and other aircraft.
Only if the terrain is within designated special use airspace. ATOP does not provide terrain separation support for uncontrolled land masses.
Yes. ATOP includes decision support for separation between aircraft and terrain, in addition to aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-airspace separation.
No. Terrain separation is exclusively a pilot responsibility in oceanic airspace and is not supported by ATOP automation.

items.question_text: A controller in ATOP airspace is using conflict probe to maintain separation between two aircraft. The controller must decide whether to rely on ADS-C position data or a more recent verbal position report from the pilot. What is the governing principle for this decision?
items.explanation: The introductory sentence of 13-2-2 establishes the principle: the controller must use the most accurate information available to initiate, monitor, and maintain separation. If a verbal position report is demonstrably more current and accurate than the ADS-C data, that principle directs the controller to use the more accurate information. This overarching principle applies before the specific conflict probe procedures that follow.

choices.text: ADS-C data must always be used because it is the automated surveillance source and the controller must assume automation is correct.
Pilot reports always take precedence over automation data because the pilot has direct access to the aircraft's navigation instruments.
The controller may choose either source at personal discretion because the paragraph provides no guidance on information accuracy.
The controller must use the most accurate information available—whether that is ADS-C data or a more current pilot report—to make separation decisions.

items.question_text: A controller has overridden a conflict probe alert under one of the authorized override conditions. The controller has taken action intended to resolve the conflict but the aircraft have not yet reached the point where separation will be restored. What continuing duty applies?
items.explanation: 13-2-2(a)(3)(b) states that controllers must continue to ensure that separation is maintained until the overridden conflict is resolved. Overriding the probe does not end the controller's separation responsibility—it continues through the entire period until the conflict is actually resolved. Simply taking initial action is insufficient if separation has not been achieved and maintained.

choices.text: The controller must ensure separation is maintained until the overridden conflict is actually resolved—the override does not relieve the continuing separation obligation.
No continuing duty applies. Once the probe is overridden, the conflict is considered resolved and the controller's separation responsibility ends.
The controller must re-engage conflict probe immediately after taking the initial action to confirm the override was correct.
The controller must transfer responsibility for the overridden conflict to the operations supervisor, who assumes the continuing separation duty.

items.question_text: Conflict probe cannot correctly monitor separation for a specific aircraft because its flight profile has become corrupted. After verifying the flight plan data is accurate, what must the controller do until conflict probe can be utilized again?
items.explanation: 13-2-2(a)(7)(b) requires that after verifying flight plan data accuracy, the controller must utilize other decision support tools to establish and maintain the appropriate separation minima until conflict probe can be utilized again. The controller cannot simply wait for probe to recover—active separation using alternative tools is required in the interim.

choices.text: Suspend all separation monitoring for that aircraft until the profile is automatically corrected by the next system update cycle.
Utilize other decision support tools to establish and maintain the appropriate separation minima until conflict probe can function again for that aircraft.
Hand off the aircraft immediately to the next sector since the current sector's probe cannot monitor it.
Rely solely on pilot position reports without using any automation tools, because all decision support tools are compromised when a profile is corrupted.

items.question_text: A CPDLC-connected aircraft sends a request to the controller via CPDLC asking for a revised altitude. The controller is considering responding by voice because it seems faster. What does the CPDLC communication procedure direct?
items.explanation: 13-2-4(a)(4) states that when a pilot communicates via CPDLC, the response should be via CPDLC. While voice may be used when it provides an operational advantage (13-2-4(a)(2)), the default rule is to respond via the same medium the pilot used. The word 'should' establishes the expectation, even though voice may be justified in specific circumstances.

choices.text: The controller must always respond by voice regardless of how the pilot initiated contact, because voice is more reliable for clearance delivery.
The controller may freely choose either CPDLC or voice for the response at personal discretion with no preference for either method.
The controller should respond via CPDLC when the pilot initiated the exchange via CPDLC, though voice may be used when it provides an operational advantage.
The controller must use free text CPDLC messages rather than the message set, because pilot-initiated requests require customized responses.

items.question_text: A controller needs to issue a routine altitude change to a CPDLC-connected aircraft. The controller drafts a free text message rather than using the predefined CPDLC message set. Is this the preferred approach?
items.explanation: 13-2-4(a)(5) states that to the extent possible, the CPDLC message set should be used in lieu of free text messages. The accompanying note explains that using the message set ensures proper 'closure' of CPDLC exchanges—each predefined message has a defined response protocol that the system can track to completion. Free text messages lack this structured closure mechanism.

choices.text: Yes. Free text messages are preferred because they allow the controller to tailor the clearance precisely to the situation.
Either approach is equally acceptable; the paragraph provides no preference between the message set and free text.
Free text is required for all altitude changes because the message set only supports route-related clearances.
No. The CPDLC message set should be used to the extent possible rather than free text, because message set exchanges ensure proper closure of the CPDLC transaction.

items.question_text: A departure has no SID filed in the flight plan and no SID was assigned by EAS. The controller is considering populating Selectable Field 1 with a SID from the TAS menu that provides vertical guidance for this departure. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(1)(c) states that when no SID is filed or assigned by EAS, the controller must either select a SID or, if no SID is to be assigned, select the NO SID option. The controller has the discretion to choose and assign a SID from the TAS menu—this is the first option. NO SID is the alternative, not the default.

choices.text: Yes, because the controller must either select a SID or, if no SID is to be assigned, select the NO SID option.
No, because Field 1 must contain NO SID whenever none was filed or assigned by EAS.
No, because only EAS-assigned SIDs may be entered in Field 1; the controller cannot select one from the TAS menu.
Yes, but only if the selected SID was previously filed for a different aircraft in the same departure push.

items.question_text: An aircraft is on a descend via clearance on the FYRBD2 STAR. The controller issues a heading vector that takes the aircraft off the STAR's lateral path for traffic spacing. After the vector, the controller expects the aircraft to resume the STAR. What happens to the altitude restrictions on the STAR's vertical profile?
items.explanation: The note to 14-2-1 states that after a climb via or descend via clearance has been issued, a vector/deviation off a SID/STAR cancels the altitude restrictions on the procedure. The FMS may be unable to process crossing altitude restrictions once the aircraft leaves the SID/STAR lateral path. Without an assigned altitude, the FMS may revert to leveling off at the altitude set by the pilot, which may be the SID/STAR published top or bottom altitude.

choices.text: The altitude restrictions remain in effect and the FMS will continue to comply with them regardless of the vector.
The altitude restrictions are canceled. Without an assigned altitude, the FMS may revert to leveling off at the altitude set by the pilot.
The altitude restrictions are suspended during the vector and automatically reactivate when the aircraft rejoins the lateral path.
Only the bottom altitude is canceled; crossing restrictions above remain active until the aircraft reaches the cleared altitude.

items.question_text: A controller vectors an aircraft off the lateral path of a STAR on which the aircraft had a descend via clearance. The aircraft's FMS may level off at which altitude if no new altitude is assigned?
items.explanation: Per the note to 14-2-1, without an assigned altitude after a vector off a SID/STAR, the aircraft's FMS may revert to leveling off at the altitude set by the pilot, which may be the SID/STAR published top or bottom altitude. This creates a potential safety issue if the controller does not assign a specific altitude after the vector.

choices.text: The last crossing restriction altitude that the aircraft had not yet reached.
The MEA for the route segment after the vector, selected automatically by the FMS.
The published SID/STAR top or bottom altitude—the altitude set by the pilot in the altitude window.
The altitude the aircraft was at when the vector was issued, maintained indefinitely.

items.question_text: A controller has overridden a CPDLC clearance by voice and the pilot has acknowledged the new clearance. The controller now considers canceling the open CPDLC uplink from the ground system. What is the sole operational purpose for which the cancel-uplink function is provided?
items.explanation: Per note 3 to 14-2-2(d), the ability to cancel an uplink is only provided to allow controllers to clear open uplink indications in the FDB and ACL. Clearing these indications allows controllers to continue CPDLC operations with the affected aircraft. It is not an operational override mechanism—the actual override requires the DISREGARD phraseology by voice with pilot UNABLE response.

choices.text: To override the clearance operationally, serving as an alternative to the voice DISREGARD phraseology.
To notify the receiving facility that the clearance has been superseded and a new handoff is required.
To automatically close the uplink on the flight deck without requiring the pilot to send an UNABLE response.
To clear open uplink indications in the FDB and ACL so controllers can continue CPDLC operations with the affected aircraft.

items.question_text: An aircraft at FL350 requires an altimeter setting. The controller knows the CPDLC system sends automated altimeter settings only on a monitor TOC or an altitude uplink below FL180. This aircraft was handed off with a standard handoff and is cruising above FL180. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: 14-2-3(a) states that if the CPDLC system fails to provide a necessary automated altimeter setting, the controller must issue an altimeter setting in accordance with Chapter 2, Section 7, Altimeter Settings. The note explains that automated altimeters are only sent in two specific circumstances. When neither applies, the controller must manually issue the altimeter—the automated system's limitation does not relieve the controller of the obligation.

choices.text: Issue the altimeter setting manually in accordance with Chapter 2, Section 7, Altimeter Settings.
Wait for the next monitor TOC, which will automatically trigger the altimeter setting.
Descend the aircraft below FL180 so the automated altimeter can be triggered.
No action is required—aircraft above FL180 use standard pressure and do not need altimeter updates.

items.question_text: A controller composes a CPDLC free-text weather advisory reporting 'moderate occasional turbulence at flight level three five zero.' Which standard abbreviations from JO 7340.2 should replace the weather descriptors?
items.explanation: 14-2-4(a)(2) requires controllers to comply with JO 7340.2 when using abbreviations in weather-related CPDLC messages. The note lists the standard meteorological abbreviations: MOD for Moderate, OCNL for Occasional, and TURB for Turbulence. Controllers must use these standard abbreviations, not improvised ones.

choices.text: MDT OCC TBC.
MOD OCNL TURB.
MODTC OCNL TURBLNC.
MDRT OCNL TRB.

items.question_text: After an automated CPDLC transfer fails and a manual transfer attempt also fails, the controller advises the pilot to log on to the next facility. What specific CPDLC message must the pilot also send to properly close the connection with the current sector?
items.explanation: 14-3-3(a) specifies the escalation procedure: if an automated transfer fails, attempt manual transfer. If unable to complete the manual transfer, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility AND send an End Service (EOS) message. The EOS message is the formal termination of the CPDLC connection with the current data authority—without it, the connection remains open on the flight deck even after voice transfer.

choices.text: Send a DISREGARD message for the failed transfer attempt.
Send a connection request (CR1) to the current facility before logging on to the next.
Send an End Service (EOS) message.
No specific message is needed; the log-on to the next facility automatically closes the previous connection.

items.instruction: Determine the correct routing path.
items.situation: A staff member in the FAA Office of Airports (a Line of Business, not AJT or AJR) needs an interpretation of FAA Order JO 7110.65 language relating to airport operations procedures. The staff member considers sending the request directly to the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations as AJT field personnel would.
items.question_text: What is the correct routing for an interpretation request from an FAA Line of Business or Staff Office?

choices.text: Submit through the District General Manager to the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations.
Submit directly to AJV-P without any leadership routing, as a non-FAA request would.
Submit through the respective Military Service Headquarters first.
Submit in writing through appropriate leadership channels to AJV-P.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-9c, requests from all other FAA ATO service units, Lines of Business, or Staff Offices must be submitted in writing through appropriate leadership channels to AJV-P. This differs from the AJT path (through DGM/OSG/Service Area Director), the AJR path (through Systems Operations Services Director), DoD path (through Military Service Headquarters), and non-FAA/DoD path (direct to AJV-P).
items.para_context: REQUESTS FOR INTERPRETATIONS OR CLARIFICATIONS TO THIS ORDER

items.instruction: What should the controller do?
items.situation: A controller is working a busy session and notices a recurring procedural ambiguity that creates a safety risk during handoffs. A coworker says that identifying safety issues is the safety office's job and that controllers should focus only on maintaining separation. The controller recalls that FAA Order JO 7110.65 addresses two duties: maintaining safety AND continuously improving the SMS.
items.question_text: Is the coworker correct that controllers need only maintain safety and should not address procedural safety improvements?

choices.text: Yes; operational personnel focus on maintaining safety; SMS improvement is the safety office's exclusive responsibility.
Yes; controllers may only report hazards through formal UCR processes, not through SMS channels.
No; all ATO employees must strive both to maintain safety AND to continuously improve the ATO SMS.
No, but safety improvement duties apply only after a reportable incident has occurred.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-12, all ATO employees must strive not only to maintain safety in the NAS for those services they provide but also to continuously improve the ATO SMS. These are dual, ongoing duties assigned to all employees at all levels — not exclusive to safety specialists.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A military pilot calls ATC and says 'Going Tactical.' The controller assumes this means the pilot is now operating under 'due regard' and that ATC separation responsibility ends. The controller prepares to terminate radar services.
items.question_text: Does a pilot's use of the phrase 'Going Tactical' indicate Due Regard under FAA Order JO 7110.65?

choices.text: No; the note in FAA Order JO 7110.65 explicitly states that 'Going Tactical' does not indicate Due Regard.
Yes; 'Going Tactical' and 'Due Regard' are equivalent terms that both end ATC separation responsibility.
Yes, but only when the pilot also provides a discrete squawk code.
No, but 'Going Tactical' activates MARSA procedures automatically.

items.explanation: Per Note 1 in 1-2-1, a pilot's use of the phrase 'Going Tactical' does not indicate 'Due Regard.' The controller must not assume separation responsibility is transferred based on this phrase alone. Due Regard has specific requirements under 1-2-1o that must be met independently.

items.instruction: Determine whether the proposed deviation from JO 7110.65 procedures is permitted
items.situation: A controller at a U.S. facility provides service in foreign airspace under a special bilateral agreement that requires adherence to ICAO separation standards, which differ from the 7110.65 minima for that situation.
items.question_text: May the controller apply the ICAO separation standard instead of the 7110.65 minimum?

choices.text: Yes, because any controller may deviate from 7110.65 whenever local practice differs.
No, because 7110.65 procedures apply uniformly regardless of location.
No, because only a letter of agreement can authorize a deviation from 7110.65 procedures.
Yes. Deviation is permitted to conform with ICAO Documents or special agreements when the U.S. provides service outside its territory.

items.explanation: The source paragraph(1) authorizes deviation when necessary to conform with ICAO Documents, National Rules of the Air, or special agreements where the U.S. provides ATC service in airspace outside the U.S. This is distinct from deviation under an LOA (d(2)) or for emergency assistance (d(3)). The ICAO/special-agreement exception is specifically tied to service outside U.S. territory.

items.instruction: Apply the procedural preferences to determine the best separation method
items.situation: A controller is working a sector with a fully functional automation system. Traffic is light but the communications frequency is congested. Two aircraft at FL350 need separation. Radar separation would require 6 heading changes and repeated coordination. Vertical separation using nonradar procedures would require one clearance per aircraft with no further vectoring.
items.question_text: Which separation method should the controller prefer?

choices.text: Radar separation, because radar preference applies whenever the equipment is functional.
Nonradar separation, because the situation dictates that an operational advantage will be gained by avoiding excessive vectoring.
Automation procedures, because the automation system is fully functional.
Closed-loop clearance procedures, because they provide TBM operational advantage when workload permits.

items.explanation: The source paragraph allows nonradar separation preference over radar 'when the situation dictates that an operational advantage will be gained.' The note cites vertical separation that would preclude excessive vectoring as an example. While radar preference (2-1-3c) is the default when conditions permit, 2-1-3d creates an express exception when nonradar methods produce a better outcome.

items.instruction: Construct the appropriate clearance using the urgency words prescribed. Include the reason since time permits
items.situation: Aircraft N12345 is at FL240. Traffic at FL250 on an opposite course is 15 miles away and closing. The controller needs the aircraft to descend to FL220 promptly to prevent this from developing into a loss of separation.
items.question_text: Which of the following clearances correctly applies the 2-1-5 urgency standard and includes the reason?

choices.text: N12345, IMMEDIATELY DESCEND TO FLIGHT LEVEL TWO TWO ZERO, TRAFFIC ONE TWO O'CLOCK.
N12345, DESCEND AND MAINTAIN FLIGHT LEVEL TWO TWO ZERO.
N12345, EXPEDITE DESCENT TO FLIGHT LEVEL TWO TWO ZERO, REASON WHEN ABLE.
N12345, EXPEDITE DESCENT TO FLIGHT LEVEL TWO TWO ZERO, TRAFFIC ONE TWO O'CLOCK OPPOSITE DIRECTION.

items.explanation: The situation describes a developing, not yet imminent, conflict: aircraft are 15 miles apart and closing. the source paragraph requires 'expedite' when prompt compliance is needed 'to avoid the development of an imminent situation.' 'Immediately' (2-1-5a) is reserved for avoiding a situation that is already imminent. the source paragraph says to include the reason when time permits.

items.instruction: Determine what the controller must do after each pilot report, from the inflight equipment malfunction rules
items.situation: A pilot at FL370 in RVSM airspace reports 'We've lost our number two GPS receiver. Request no change to our routing.'
items.question_text: What is the controller's first responsibility?

choices.text: Determine the nature and extent of any special handling the pilot desires.
Instruct the pilot to cancel IFR since GPS is required for IFR operations in RVSM airspace.
Immediately issue a frequency change to the next sector since the aircraft cannot continue in RVSM airspace.
File an equipment malfunction report with technical operations before taking any other action.

items.explanation: The source paragraph requires the controller to first determine the nature and extent of any special handling desired. The pilot has already stated 'no change to routing,' which addresses the special-handling question. The note lists GPS as an example of reportable inflight equipment malfunctions. A single GPS receiver loss in a multi-sensor aircraft may not require any operational change — the controller's first duty is to find out, not to assume.

items.instruction: Apply the MARSA responsibilities to determine each party's role
items.situation: Two F-16 aircraft request to conduct MARSA operations in an active MOA. The MOA scheduling agency has approved their entry. A third IFR aircraft on an airway will pass within 5 miles of the MOA boundary during the proposed operation time.
items.question_text: What is the ATC facility's sole responsibility regarding these MARSA operations?

choices.text: Provide separation between the MARSA-participating F-16s and the nonparticipating IFR aircraft on the airway.
Invoke MARSA on behalf of the military unit if the pilots have not done so.
Determine whether the F-16 pilots are familiar with appropriate MARSA procedures.
Deny the MARSA request because a nonparticipating aircraft will be in the vicinity.

items.explanation: The source paragraph states that ATC facilities do not invoke or deny MARSA; their sole responsibility is 'to provide separation between military aircraft engaged in MARSA operations and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft.' Determining pilot familiarity is a DoD responsibility (2-1-11c). Invocation is the military command's prerogative, not ATC's.

items.instruction: Construct the traffic advisory using the non-radar-identified aircraft format
items.situation: A VFR aircraft (Cessna 172, not radar-identified, outside Class B airspace) needs a traffic advisory. Traffic is a King Air at 4,500 feet, 3 miles east of the VOR, eastbound.
items.question_text: Which of the following follows the correct format?

choices.text: TRAFFIC, THREE O'CLOCK, THREE MILES, EASTBOUND, KING AIR, FOUR THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED.
TRAFFIC, THREE MILES EAST OF THE (name) VOR, EASTBOUND, KING AIR, FOUR THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED.
TRAFFIC, EAST OF YOUR POSITION, THREE MILES, TYPE AND ALTITUDE UNKNOWN.
CAUTION WAKE TURBULENCE, KING AIR THREE MILES EAST OF THE VOR AT FOUR THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED.

items.explanation: The source paragraph specifies that for aircraft not radar identified, traffic should be described using a geographic position reference rather than clock-bearing-and-distance from the aircraft. The correct format includes direction and distance from a known geographic reference, direction of flight, and (if known) aircraft type and altitude.

items.instruction: Identify all required actions after receiving the bird report
items.situation: A pilot reports a large flock of geese at 2,000 feet, 3 miles north of the airport, moving southbound. The airport has an active FSS on the field and the approach control facility is in the adjacent sector.
items.question_text: Which of the following best describes the controller's complete obligations?

choices.text: Issue advisory information to aircraft on frequency only; adjacent facilities are responsible for their own bird detection.
Broadcast a one-time warning and log the report; no further action is required.
Issue advisories for 15 minutes and relay to adjacent facilities and FSSs if the activity may become a factor in their areas.
Contact the airport manager to initiate bird dispersal operations and hold all departures.

items.explanation: The source paragraph requires two distinct actions: (a) issue advisory information including position, species/size, course, and altitude for at least 15 minutes, unless later information shows the activity is no longer a factor; and (b) relay the information to adjacent facilities and FSSs whenever it appears it will become a factor in their areas. The southbound movement toward the airport makes both obligations relevant.

items.instruction: Determine whether supervisory notification is required
items.situation: You observe an unfamiliar UAS operating near the approach corridor to a major airport. The UAS does not appear on radar and is not squawking. The UAS operator has not coordinated with ATC or filed a flight plan.
items.question_text: Does this situation require supervisory notification?

choices.text: No. The list is limited to weather, equipment status, potential sector overload, emergency situations, and special flights.
Only if the UAS causes a loss of separation with a manned aircraft.
No. UAS operations are addressed in other chapters and do not fall under Chapter 2 supervisory notification.
Yes. The list of notifiable conditions includes 'aircraft/pilot activity, including unmanned aircraft system (UAS) operation that is considered suspicious.'

items.explanation: The source paragraph's list includes paragraph (f): 'Aircraft/pilot activity, including unmanned aircraft system (UAS) operation that is considered suspicious.' The list is explicitly non-exhaustive ('including, but not limited to'). An unfamiliar UAS operating near an approach corridor without coordination is a safety concern that impacts sector operations.

items.instruction: Determine when ATC separation responsibility resumes after a TCAS RA event
items.situation: An aircraft at FL330 reports 'TCAS RA, descending.' The aircraft descends to FL310 on the RA maneuver. The crew then reports 'Clear of conflict, returning to FL330.' You observe the aircraft climbing back through FL320 with another aircraft at FL310, 5 miles ahead and level.
items.question_text: When does ATC responsibility for approved separation resume?

choices.text: When the crew reports the RA maneuver is completed AND the controller observes that approved separation has been reestablished.
Only after the controller issues a new clearance and the pilot reads it back.
Immediately when the crew says 'Clear of conflict.'
When the aircraft returns to its originally assigned altitude of FL330.

items.explanation: The source paragraph(2) states that responsibility for approved separation resumes when 'a crew member informs you that the TCAS maneuver is completed and you observe that approved separation has been reestablished.' The crew's 'clear of conflict' is a step toward completion but does not by itself satisfy the condition. The controller must both receive the completion report AND observe reestablished separation.

items.instruction: Apply the TAWS alert rules to determine proper controller actions
items.situation: Aircraft A at 3,000 feet reports 'TAWS alert, pulling up, climbing.' Aircraft B is at 4,000 feet, 3 miles ahead on a crossing course. Aircraft C is at 2,500 feet on a parallel course 10 miles away.
items.question_text: What must the controller do while Aircraft A is responding to the TAWS alert?

choices.text: Issue an immediate descent instruction to Aircraft A and vector Aircraft B away.
Only provide advisories to Aircraft B since Aircraft A is already responding to its own alert.
Do not issue control instructions contrary to the TAWS maneuver, and provide safety alerts and traffic advisories to Aircraft A, B, and C as appropriate.
Assume Aircraft B and C are aware of Aircraft A's maneuver and take no action.

items.explanation: The source paragraph requires the controller to: (1) NOT issue control instructions contrary to the TAWS maneuver the crew is executing; and (2) provide safety alerts and traffic advisories for the responding aircraft AND all other aircraft under control jurisdiction, as appropriate. This mirrors the TCAS RA rule in 2-1-28 but applied to on-board low-altitude alert systems.

items.instruction: Apply the manual beacon code entry rule
items.situation: A flight plan has been forwarded to your facility with a computer-assigned beacon code of 4231. Due to a computer interface problem, you must manually enter the flight plan into the local automation system.
items.question_text: What must you do with the already-assigned beacon code 4231?

choices.text: Only use it if the pilot can confirm the code when calling for clearance.
Discard it and let the system assign a new local code, since the flight plan is being manually entered.
Change it to a non-discrete code because manual entries always use local code assignments.
Insert it into the appropriate field as part of the manual input message — do not discard or reassign it.

items.explanation: The source paragraph states that when a flight plan is manually entered into the computer and a computer-assigned beacon code has been forwarded with the flight plan data, you must 'insert the beacon code in the appropriate field as part of the input message.' This preserves the originally assigned code and prevents code conflicts that could arise from duplicate assignments.
items.para_context: MANUAL INPUT OF COMPUTER-ASSIGNED BEACON CODES

items.instruction: Apply the amended data forwarding rules
items.situation: You previously forwarded a flight plan to an adjacent center with an ETA of 1430. The aircraft now has a revised ETA of 1432. The center is operating under EAS FDP.
items.question_text: Must you forward the revised ETA as amended data?

choices.text: No, because EAS FDP operation eliminates all manual amendment forwarding requirements.
No. ETA revisions need forwarding only when the time differs by more than 3 minutes from the given estimate.
Yes. Any change to ETA, regardless of magnitude, must be forwarded as amended data.
Yes, because EAS FDP operation requires forwarding of all amended data.

items.explanation: The source paragraph states that 'revisions to ETA information in paragraph 2-2-6, IFR Flight Progress Data, need only be forwarded when the time differs by more than 3 minutes from the estimate given.' A 2-minute difference (1430 to 1432) falls below this threshold. EAS FDP operation does not change this threshold — it affects how acknowledged amendments are handled, not whether they must be sent.

items.instruction: Apply Canadian-to-U.S. flight-data format requirements.
items.situation: A Canadian ACC must send a correction to flight data previously transmitted to an FAA ARTCC.
items.question_text: Which handling is supported by the prescribed exchange format?

choices.text: Send only a cancellation, then require the pilot to file a new flight plan.
Use the U.S.-to-Canada strip format because correction messages are not supported.
Send a NADIN II correction message to the FAA ARTCC.
Delay the correction until the aircraft checks in with the FAA ARTCC.

items.explanation: Canadian ACC transmissions to FAA ARTCCs may use the NADIN II input format for correction messages. Apply the permitted message format to the operational need rather than comparing document lists.

items.instruction: Apply the clearance status symbology
items.situation: An aircraft is cleared to HOLD at BANTY intersection. The established holding pattern at BANTY is right turns, 1-minute legs. The controller needs the aircraft to hold with left turns and 2-minute legs due to airspace constraints.
items.question_text: How should the clearance status be recorded on the flight progress strip?

choices.text: Use 'H' at the clearance limit, followed by a dash and the detailed holding information: left turns, 2-minute legs.
Use 'H' alone at the clearance limit, since the holding fix is the same.
Use 'F' to indicate the delay is anticipated, with holding details in the remarks section.
Use 'O' since the holding differs from the established pattern.

items.explanation: The source paragraph states: 'Use the symbol H at the clearance limit when holding instructions have been included in the aircraft's original clearance. Show detailed holding information following the dash when holding differs from the established pattern for the fix; i.e., turns, leg lengths, etc.' The H symbol plus detail is correct because holding instructions are included and the pattern differs from the standard right-turns, 1-minute procedure.

items.instruction: Identify which aircraft identifications may and may not be abbreviated
items.question_text: After communications are established, which of the following aircraft identifications MAY be abbreviated using the prefix and last 3 digits or letters?

choices.text: N123AB, a U.S. civil aircraft using its registration number.
N123LS, which has a nearly identical call sign to N123L5 on the same frequency.
G-ABCD, a foreign-registered civil aircraft.
UAL456, an air carrier with an ICAO 3LD and FAA-authorized call sign.

items.explanation: The source paragraph permits abbreviation only for U.S. civil aircraft using the aircraft registration as identification, and only after communications have been established. Foreign-registered aircraft (2-4-9b(2)(b)), aircraft with ICAO 3LD call signs, and similar-sounding identifications (2-4-9b(1)) must never be abbreviated.

items.instruction: Determine which situation justifies using the priority interruption words
items.question_text: A controller is on an interphone line listening to a routine departure report. In which of the following situations may the controller interrupt with the prescribed words?

choices.text: The controller wants to end the call early because of an approaching break time.
The controller has an emergency message or a control message to transmit that takes priority over the current transmission.
The controller needs to ask a quick question about a previously coordinated flight.
The controller needs to relay routine weather information that just became available.

items.explanation: The source paragraph authorizes the words 'emergency' or 'control' ONLY for interrupting lower-priority messages when the interrupting controller has an emergency or control message to transmit. Routine questions, weather updates, or administrative matters do not justify priority interruption.

items.instruction: Apply prescribed phonetic and number pronunciation.
items.situation: On a noisy frequency, a controller must transmit the aircraft identification N5JQ.
items.question_text: Which transmission uses the prescribed pronunciations?

choices.text: November Five Juliett Quebec.
Nancy Fife John Queen.
November Fife Juliett Quebec.
November Fife Japan Quebec.

items.explanation: Use the prescribed spoken forms for individual characters: November, Fife, Juliett, Quebec. The operational skill is clear pronunciation; the numeral 5 is spoken "Fife," not "Five."

items.instruction: Identify the correct facility identification
items.situation: Columbus, Mississippi has both an Air Force Base and Golden Triangle Regional Airport in the same general area.
items.question_text: How should the Air Force Base tower be identified on frequency?

choices.text: Columbus Tower — the same as any other airport traffic control tower.
Columbus Air Force Base Tower — the full base name is always required.
Air Force Tower, Columbus — the facility type precedes the location name for military installations.
Air Force Columbus Tower. The military service name precedes the facility name when similar-named airports exist in the same area.

items.explanation: When military and civil airports in the same general area have similar names, state the military service, the military facility name, and "Tower." The applicable identification is "Air Force Columbus Tower."

items.instruction: Apply the prevailing visibility rules
items.situation: At a tower, the surface weather observer reports prevailing visibility of 3.5 miles. The tower-level observation shows visibility of 2.5 miles due to a low haze layer at tower height. Aircraft are operating visually in the pattern.
items.question_text: Which visibility value should the controller use for aircraft operations?

choices.text: 3.5 miles. The surface observation is always the official value.
2.5 miles. Use the lower of the tower and surface observations for aircraft operations.
3.0 miles. The average of the two observations should be used.
Either value. The controller may use whichever observation appears more reliable.

items.explanation: The source paragraph(1) requires using the lower of the two observations (tower or surface) for aircraft operations. This is mandatory not discretionary, providing a safety margin by using the more conservative assessment.

items.instruction: Apply the hazardous weather advisory broadcast rules
items.situation: A SIGMET has been issued for severe turbulence covering an area centered 80 NM west of your approach control facility. Your facility jurisdiction extends 40 NM from the primary airport. The SIGMET area overlaps the western edge of your airspace.
items.question_text: What must the controller do regarding hazardous weather advisories?

choices.text: Only advise pilots of weather within the facility 40 NM jurisdiction, since that is the extent of control responsibility.
The SIGMET is primarily an en route product; approach control has no obligation to broadcast it.
Advise pilots of hazardous weather that may impact operations within 150 NM of the sector. The SIGMET area is within this radius and must be broadcast.
Only broadcast the advisory if a pilot specifically requests hazardous weather information.

items.explanation: The source paragraph requires controllers to advise pilots of hazardous weather that may impact operations within 150 NM of their sector or area of jurisdiction. The 150 NM radius is a fixed distance, not limited to the facility airspace boundary. Since the SIGMET area is centered 80 NM away, it falls within the advisory obligation.

items.instruction: Apply the RVR issuance rules
items.situation: The prevailing visibility at an airport is 1.5 miles. The touchdown RVR for the runway in use reads 5,500 feet. A tower controller is preparing the ATIS broadcast.
items.question_text: Must the controller issue the current touchdown RVR?

choices.text: No. Prevailing visibility is above 1 mile, and RVR issuance is only required when visibility is 1 mile or less.
No. RVR 5,500 is above the reportable threshold and need not be issued.
Yes, but only because the RVR is below 6,000 feet and the ATIS must include all available weather elements.
Yes. An RVR of 5,500 feet is reportable, so issue it regardless of prevailing visibility.

items.explanation: The source paragraph has two independent triggers for issuing touchdown RVR: (1) when prevailing visibility is 1 mile or less regardless of RVR value, OR (2) when RVR indicates a reportable value (6,000 feet or less) regardless of prevailing visibility. Here, trigger (2) applies because 5,500 feet is reportable, even though prevailing visibility exceeds 1 mile.

items.instruction: What should the ground controller do?
items.situation: Ground control needs to move an airport operations vehicle across an active runway at Taxiway D. The local controller is handling three arrivals on final. Ground coordinates as required: 'CROSS RUNWAY 27 AT TAXIWAY D.' Local approves. The vehicle crosses the runway and is now clear on the other side taxiway. Another ground controller says no further action is needed — the crossing was authorized, and it's done.
items.question_text: What must ground control now do after the vehicle completes the coordinated runway crossing?

choices.text: No further communication is needed because the crossing authorization automatically expires when the vehicle exits the runway.
Wait for local control to call back and confirm the crossing is done before taking any further action.
Transfer vehicle communications to local control for a post-crossing confirmation call.
Advise local control that the coordinated runway operation is complete — verbally or via visual aids as specified in the facility directive.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-3c requires the ground controller to advise the local controller when the coordinated runway operation is complete. This may be accomplished verbally or through visual aids as specified by a facility directive. The completion notification closes the coordination loop and lets local know the runway is available again at that crossing point.

items.instruction: What should the local controller do?
items.situation: You are working local control. A landing aircraft is 3 miles out on final for Runway 18L. You see a vehicle on the runway conducting a surface inspection. The vehicle operator has an LOA and two-way radio communications with the tower. Another controller says to just clear the aircraft to land because the vehicle has an LOA and will exit before the aircraft reaches the threshold.
items.question_text: What must the local controller ensure before the landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold?

choices.text: Ensure the runway is free of the vehicle before the landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold — issue the landing clearance only when the runway is clear.
Clear the aircraft to land immediately because LOA-authorized vehicles may remain on the runway during landing operations.
Issue the landing clearance with a cautionary advisory about the vehicle — the pilot will self-separate.
Tell the vehicle to hold position on the runway and clear the arriving aircraft to land on the remaining runway length.

items.explanation: Paragraph 3-1-5b requires that the runway be free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel before a landing aircraft crosses the runway threshold. The vehicle's LOA and two-way communications authorize RSA operations up to the runway edge, but the runway itself must be clear before a landing aircraft crosses the threshold. The LOA does not override this requirement.

items.instruction: Select the traffic description that most directly helps the pilot recognize the hazard.
items.situation: An aircraft is approaching Runway 18 while a mower is operating just left of that runway.
items.question_text: Which advisory uses the prescribed plain relative-position format?

choices.text: GROUND EQUIPMENT IN THE GENERAL AIRPORT AREA.
MOWER LEFT OF RUNWAY ONE EIGHT.
CAUTION, VEHICLE SOMEWHERE NEAR THE MOVEMENT AREA.
MOWER TRAFFIC, POSITION UNKNOWN.

items.explanation: Describe vehicles, equipment, or personnel in a way that helps the pilot recognize them. A direct relative position such as "Mower left of runway one eight" supplies that useful context.

items.instruction: What should the controller do?
items.situation: An aircraft departing Runway 27 reports it is performing a wind shear escape maneuver and climbs straight ahead at full power. A second aircraft was just cleared for departure on the same runway and is beginning its takeoff roll. The second aircraft's pilot has not reported any wind shear. Another controller says to instruct the second aircraft to also climb straight ahead because wind shear must be affecting both.
items.question_text: How should the controller handle the second departing aircraft?

choices.text: Immediately cancel the second aircraft's takeoff clearance — wind shear on departure makes any continuation unsafe for all aircraft on that route.
Instruct the second aircraft to execute the same escape maneuver as the first aircraft — wind shear conditions are likely affecting both departures equally.
