All other items—flight plan, last transmission, last position, beacon code, persons on board, fuel, working facility/frequency, last known position, nearby aircraft, ELT signal, other information—appear on both lists.
items.para_context: INFORMATION TO BE FORWARDED TO RCC

items.instruction: Determine whether normal traffic may resume after an overdue-aircraft suspension period.
items.situation: The 30-minute traffic suspension period for an overdue aircraft has expired. The controller contacts the operators and pilots of other affected aircraft. One pilot concurs with resuming normal operations, but another pilot expresses concern and does not concur, saying the overdue aircraft might still be in the area.
items.question_text: May normal ATC operations resume?

choices.text: No—all operators and pilots of other aircraft must concur before normal ATC can resume after the suspension period.
Yes—only one concurrence is needed since the majority of operators have agreed.
Yes—after the 30-minute suspension expires, resumption is automatic regardless of pilot concurrence.
Only the facility manager can make the resumption decision; individual pilot concurrence is not a factor.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-3 requires that after the 30-minute traffic suspension period has expired, normal ATC may resume only if the operators or pilots of other aircraft concur. 'Operators or pilots' is plural and the concurrence condition applies to all affected aircraft—unanimous concurrence is required. The concurrence must then be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period expires.

items.instruction: Apply explosive cargo notification procedures.
items.situation: A military cargo aircraft carrying explosive ordnance declares an emergency and will be making an emergency landing at a civilian airport. The pilot requests that the appropriate military agency be notified. The controller has informed the pilot of the safest airport areas.
items.question_text: To whom must the explosive cargo information be relayed?

choices.text: Only the airport management, since the emergency is occurring at a civilian airport.
The emergency equipment crew, the airport management, and the appropriate military agencies (since the pilot requested military notification).
The emergency equipment crew only—airport management notification is the pilot's responsibility.
The FAA Operations Center and the Domestic Events Network, since explosive cargo triggers national security protocols.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-5-1 requires relaying explosive cargo information to three entities: (a) the emergency equipment crew, (b) the airport management, and (c) the appropriate military agencies when requested by the pilot. The military notification is conditional on the pilot's request, as it was here. The controller must also first inform the pilot of the safest or least congested airport areas.

items.instruction: Apply emergency descent phraseology for an oceanic aircraft descending without a clearance.
items.situation: An aircraft at FL350 over the North Atlantic is making an emergency descent without a clearance due to a pressurization failure. The controller needs to warn other aircraft in the vicinity. The aircraft is descending from FL350 to FL100 at approximately 45N 040W. The controller must issue an advisory broadcast.
items.question_text: What phraseology elements must the broadcast include?

choices.text: Aircraft identification, nature of emergency, intended altitude, and request for other aircraft to report clear of the descent path.
PAN-PAN, all stations, emergency descent in progress, (location), all aircraft avoid area below FL350 within 50 NM of (location).
ATC ADVISES, aircraft identification or 'all aircraft,' BE ALERT FOR EMERGENCY DESCENT IN THE VICINITY OF (location), FROM (altitude/FL) TO (altitude/FL).
MAYDAY, (aircraft identification), emergency descent, (location), requesting all traffic give way immediately.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-4c2(a) prescribes the exact phraseology for advising other aircraft of an emergency descent without clearance: 'ATC ADVISES (aircraft identification/all aircraft) BE ALERT FOR EMERGENCY DESCENT IN THE VICINITY OF (latitude/longitude) FROM (altitude/FL) TO (altitude/FL).' This is followed by the completion broadcast per 10-6-4c2(b) once the descent is finished.

items.instruction: Apply the public health risk reporting procedure and determine the information flow path.
items.situation: A pilot reports that three passengers on board have developed symptoms consistent with a serious communicable illness. The aircraft is 45 minutes from its destination and the pilot intends to continue. The pilot requests that emergency medical services meet the aircraft on arrival. The controller obtains all seven required information items.
items.question_text: What must the controller do with this information?

choices.text: Notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible, who must then relay the information to the DEN.
Notify the destination airport's operations office directly so they can arrange for EMS meeting the aircraft.
Broadcast the information on frequency so that all concerned facilities and agencies are simultaneously informed.
Forward the information only to the Centers for Disease Control, since public health reporting bypasses normal ATC supervisory channels.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-19a-b establishes a two-step reporting path: the controller must obtain the seven specified information items and notify the OMIC/OS/CIC as soon as possible, and the OMIC/OS/CIC must then relay the information to the DEN as soon as possible. Note 1 provides the DEN direct phone number (844-432-2962) for facilities not actively monitoring the DEN. Note 2 clarifies that follow-on coordination will not involve ATC frequencies except in extraordinary circumstances.
items.para_context: REPORTING DEATH, ILLNESS, OR OTHER PUBLIC HEALTH RISK ON BOARD

items.instruction: What should the OS/CIC do?
items.situation: An OS/CIC at an ARTCC notices that a neighboring center has posted an advisory indicating reduced acceptance rates due to an equipment outage. Aircraft departing from airports in the OS/CIC's area routinely transit the affected center's airspace. The OS/CIC's own sectors are currently operating without congestion.
items.question_text: What is the OS/CIC's obligation upon learning of this advisory?

choices.text: Keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised, because the situation may cause congestion or delays even though they are not yet present.
Take no action until the OS/CIC's own sectors begin showing measurable congestion or delays.
Notify only the STMCIC, since external facility advisories are handled at the STMCIC level.
Issue ground stops for all departures routing through the affected center without first coordinating with the TMU.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2b1, the OS/CIC must keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised of situations or circumstances that may cause congestion or delays. The trigger is 'may cause' — potential impact rather than confirmed congestion. Waiting for actual delays before reporting would violate the proactive-notification duty. The notification must reach both the TMU and the affected sectors, not just the supervisory chain.

items.instruction: What should the controller do?
items.situation: A controller is working metering at a busy ARTCC sector. The TMU has just resequenced several flights in the arrival stream, and the TBFM-generated metering times for those flights are fluctuating significantly. The controller can still meet the displayed times for most aircraft, but the resequenced flights' metering times are uncertain. The sector is adjacent to another busy sector, and several aircraft are on user-preferred routes.
items.question_text: How should the controller handle metering for the resequenced flights?

choices.text: Apply the displayed metering times exactly as shown for every aircraft, since TBFM-generated times must be followed within plus-or-minus-1-minute without exception.
Use caution when applying metering times for the resequenced flights, taking care to minimize impact on surrounding sector traffic, complexity, flight efficiency, and user preferences.
Stop metering all aircraft in the sector until the TMU confirms accurate times for the resequenced flights.
Ignore the metering times for the resequenced flights and use miles-in-trail for the entire arrival stream for the remainder of the shift.

items.explanation: Per the note at 11-1-3, when TBFM metering-time accuracy is affected by factors including TMU resequencing, controllers must use caution to minimize impact on surrounding sector traffic, complexity levels, flight efficiencies, and user preferences. Blindly following fluctuating metering times risks disrupting adjacent sectors. Abandoning metering entirely is not authorized; the standard requires cautious application, not suspension.

items.instruction: Which procedure should you use?
items.situation: You are working Canadian airspace where control responsibility has been formally delegated to the FAA. A Canadian-specific procedure in Chapter 12 requires a different clearance phraseology than the standard FAA phraseology for the same operation. A trainee argues that you should use the FAA phraseology because 12-1-1 says to apply basic FAA procedures.
items.question_text: Which phraseology should be used — the Chapter 12 Canadian phraseology or the standard FAA phraseology?

choices.text: Use the FAA phraseology because basic FAA procedures are the default and Canadian procedures are optional.
Use the Chapter 12 Canadian phraseology, because the chapter's Canadian-specific procedures must be applied where Canadian regulatory or unique operational requirements make them necessary.
Use whichever phraseology the pilot requests, because the delegation agreement lets the pilot choose between the two systems.
Use neither — coordinate with the Canadian authority to determine which phraseology applies to this specific operation.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-1, the rule is "apply basic FAA procedures except for the Canadian procedures contained in this chapter." The exception clause means Chapter 12 procedures take precedence over the corresponding basic FAA procedure where they apply. The trainee's argument omits the exception — the Canadian procedures are mandatory where they exist in the chapter, not optional.

items.instruction: Identify the airspace class.
items.situation: An aircraft is cruising at 16,000 feet ASL in Canadian controlled low level airspace. The minimum en route IFR altitude for this sector is 11,000 feet. The pilot is operating under an IFR clearance. A trainee asks what airspace class the aircraft is in and what other flight rules category shares this airspace.
items.question_text: What is the correct airspace classification?

choices.text: Class C airspace, which also permits ordinary VFR flights with an ATC clearance.
Class A airspace, which permits only IFR flights.
Class B airspace, which also permits Controlled VFR (CVFR) flights.
Class E airspace, which also permits VFR flights with no special requirements.

items.explanation: At 16,000 feet ASL, the aircraft is above 12,500 feet ASL (and above the 11,000-foot MEA) and below 18,000 feet ASL — this is Canadian Class B airspace per 12-1-2b. Class B permits only IFR and CVFR flights. Class A would be wrong because Class A begins at the base of controlled high level airspace and is IFR-only. Class C, D, and E are lower-altitude airspace classes with different VFR entry rules.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A pilot operating below 18,000 feet ASL requests "at least 1,000 feet-on-top." The pilot confirms they can maintain at least 1,000 feet above all cloud with 3 miles flight visibility. However, the filed route includes a segment through Canadian Class B airspace. No alternate radio failure instructions have been issued.
items.question_text: Should the request be approved?

choices.text: Approve it because the pilot has met the weather requirements and requested the clearance — the route through Class B is irrelevant.
Approve it but instruct the pilot to deviate around the Class B airspace while maintaining 1,000 feet-on-top.
Deny it because 1,000 feet-on-top is never permitted on filed routes — it is available only for local operations within visual range of the airport.
Deny it because the pilot will operate within Class B airspace, which is prohibited for the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-3b, the pilot must not operate within Class A or Class B airspace when using the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance. Even though the pilot meets the weather requirements (pilot's responsibility per the note), the airspace restriction is an independent, non-waivable condition. Both conditions — pilot request plus no Class A/B operation — must be satisfied.

items.instruction: What type of separation should you apply?
items.situation: Two aircraft are on IFR clearances in Canadian airspace on the same airway. One is level at FL 200 and the other at FL 210. The lower aircraft requests a climb to FL 220. The airway is a single protected route with no lateral offset available. Longitudinal separation would require the trail aircraft to reduce speed by 40 knots, creating a disruptive traffic flow.
items.question_text: Which type of separation minimum best resolves this situation?

choices.text: Vertical separation — ensure at least 1,000 feet vertical separation exists between the aircraft throughout the climb by coordinating the timing with the aircraft at FL 210.
Vertical separation — hold the climbing aircraft at FL 200 until the other aircraft is no longer a factor, then approve the climb.
Longitudinal separation — impose the speed reduction on the trail aircraft and accept the disruption to traffic flow.
Lateral separation — offset the climbing aircraft onto a parallel track outside the airway boundary.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, the controller may apply a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum. When lateral separation is unavailable (single airway) and longitudinal separation would be unduly disruptive, vertical separation is the appropriate choice. The rule gives the controller discretion to select any of the three types — it does not prioritize one over another. Here, managing the climb to maintain vertical separation throughout is the most efficient solution.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: Before departure, you issue a turbine-powered aircraft alternate radio failure instructions specifying that if communications are lost, the aircraft is to climb immediately to FL 280 and proceed direct to the destination. After departure, the aircraft loses communications. The standard Canadian radio-failure procedure would have the aircraft maintain its last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) for 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit before climbing to filed altitude. Another controller argues the standard procedure must still be followed because Canadian regulations require it.
items.question_text: Which procedure should the pilot follow?

choices.text: The standard procedure, because ATC-issued alternate instructions are advisory only and do not override the regulatory requirement.
The alternate instructions issued by ATC, because the standard procedure applies only when ATC has NOT issued alternate radio failure instructions.
Neither — the pilot should squawk 7600 and hold at the clearance limit until communications are restored or 30 minutes have elapsed.
The standard procedure for the first 10 minutes, then the alternate instructions thereafter.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the standard radio-failure procedure is conditional: it applies "except when issued alternate radio failure instructions by ATC." Once ATC issues alternate instructions, those instructions entirely replace the standard procedure. The alternate instructions become the controlling plan. The other controller's argument incorrectly treats the standard procedure as mandatory regardless of ATC action.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A helicopter pilot requests SVFR at a Canadian control zone where an airport controller is on duty. The reported ground visibility is 3/4 mile. IFR traffic is light, with one arrival expected in approximately 25 minutes. A trainee says the SVFR must be denied because the visibility is below 1 mile.
items.question_text: Should the helicopter's SVFR request be approved or denied?

choices.text: Deny it — the standard SVFR visibility minimum is 1 mile, and 3/4 mile is below that threshold.
Deny it — even though helicopters have a lower visibility minimum, the IFR arrival in 25 minutes requires that SVFR be refused.
Approve it — the 1/2 mile helicopter minimum is met, IFR traffic is light, and with a controller on duty the approval criteria are satisfied.
Approve it but limit the operation to 10 minutes, because helicopter SVFR at reduced visibility cannot exceed that duration.

items.explanation: Two rules combine here. First, the helicopter SVFR visibility minimum is 1/2 mile (12-1-7c2), not the 1 mile that applies to fixed-wing aircraft. At 3/4 mile, the helicopter meets its minimum. Second, with an airport controller on duty (12-1-7a), the approval decision is based on current or anticipated IFR traffic only — not ceiling and visibility. Light IFR traffic supports approval. The controller must specify the period of SVFR authorization.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A conflict probe alert fires between a standard B737 and a non-standard military formation flight. The alert shows the standard 5 NM radar separation minimum will be maintained. Another controller says the probe alert is definitive because it uses approved radar separation standards, so no additional evaluation is needed. A third controller says the probe does not account for non-standard formations, so the alert alone is insufficient and you need to determine whether greater separation is required for the formation.
items.question_text: How should the alert be evaluated for the non-standard formation?

choices.text: Recognize that conflict probe does not account for non-standard formations that may require greater separation.
Accept the alert as definitive. Conflict probe uses approved radar separation and the standard 5 NM is always sufficient for all aircraft types and formations.
Suppress the alert because it is a false indication for military formation flights, and rely on visual scanning of the radar display instead.
Accept the alert but apply the 3-mile reduced separation minimum instead, because military formations are authorized reduced separation in all airspace.

items.explanation: Per the note to 13-1-2, conflict probe alerts are based on approved radar separation but do not account for instances where greater separation may be needed, such as non-standard formations. The controller must evaluate the alert with awareness of this limitation and determine whether the standard minimum is adequate for the specific aircraft types and formations involved.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An ATOP sector team is working a busy period. Several messages appear in the coordination queue: a red conflict alert, three non-conformant messages in the error queue that are more than five minutes old, and a routine coordination request from the adjacent facility. A controller wants to clear the error queue first because the messages are old and the system keeps flagging them. Another controller wants to address the routine coordination request first because it's the quickest item.
items.question_text: How should the team prioritize these queue items?

choices.text: Clear the error queue first because the messages are the oldest and system-generated items take priority over controller-initiated actions.
Manage all sector and coordination queues in accordance with the appropriate message priority and the controller's priority of duties—the red conflict takes precedence as the highest-priority item.
Handle the routine coordination request first because external facility requests always take priority over internal queue management.
Process queue items in the order they were received regardless of content, because the first-in-first-out principle applies to all ATOP queues.

items.explanation: Paragraph 13-2-3(d)(1) requires managing all sector and coordination queues in accordance with the appropriate message priority and the controller's priority of duties. Message priority and duty priority together determine the processing order—a red conflict alert represents a higher-priority duty than aged non-conformant messages or routine coordination. The error queue messages should be processed timely (13-2-3(d)(2)) but do not displace higher-priority operational actions.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: RNP distance-based longitudinal separation is being applied between two aircraft at FL350 over the ocean. The trailing aircraft requests a weather deviation that would reduce the spacing. The deviation monitor window shows a pink advisory with a zero value in the +/- column. A controller says a zero value means no additional spacing is needed and the deviation can be approved. Another controller says pink advisories never prevent deviation approval—they are only informational.
items.question_text: How should this pink advisory be handled before approving the weather deviation?

choices.text: Approve the deviation immediately because zero means the spacing is exactly at the standard minimum, which is acceptable for all weather deviation requests.
Deny the deviation outright because any pink advisory automatically prohibits weather deviation approval regardless of the +/- value.

Conduct further evaluation before approving. A zero value in the +/- column means the aircraft pair is at or close to the required spacing, even with the CDI buffer applied—the clearance is not yet assured to be safe.
Approve the deviation without further evaluation because pink advisories are only informational and do not affect the controller's authority to approve weather deviations.

items.explanation: Paragraph 13-2-5(a) requires further evaluation of pink advisories before approving a weather deviation clearance. 13-2-5(b) specifies that a zero or positive value in the +/- column indicates the aircraft pair is either at or close to the required spacing—it does not mean the spacing is safely beyond the required minimum. The pink advisory and the zero/positive value together mean the controller must evaluate further, not approve immediately. A zero value does not indicate extra spacing; it indicates the aircraft are at the boundary of what is required.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A departure flight plan has no SID filed and no SID was assigned by EAS. The weather is VMC and the aircraft will depart from runway 27, which has a published obstacle departure procedure but no SID. The controller must populate Selectable Field 1. A trainee insists that Field 1 must always contain NO SID in this case.
items.question_text: How should the controller handle Selectable Field 1?

choices.text: Select NO SID, because it is the only option when no SID was filed or assigned by EAS.
Leave Field 1 blank and enter the departure procedure in Field 3 instead.
Select the ---- option, which is the standard indicator for no SID in Field 1.
The controller must either select a SID from the TAS menu or select NO SID—the trainee is incorrect that NO SID is the only option.

items.explanation: Paragraph 14-1-3(a)(1)(c) states the controller must either select a SID or, if no SID is to be assigned, select the NO SID option. The controller has discretion to select a SID from the TAS menu even when none was filed or assigned. The ---- option is only for Field 2 (Transition), and Field 1 cannot be left blank.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An aircraft is on a descend via clearance on the FYRBD2 arrival with multiple crossing restrictions at FL240, FL180, and 10,000 feet. The controller vectors the aircraft off the arrival's lateral path for spacing and intends the aircraft to rejoin after the vector. The controller does not issue a new altitude assignment. After the vector, the aircraft levels off at FL240—the altitude the pilot had set in the altitude window before the vector.
items.question_text: What operational principle explains the aircraft's level-off behavior, and what should the controller have done?

choices.text: A vector off the STAR lateral path cancels the altitude restrictions. The controller should have assigned a specific altitude after the vector.
The FMS malfunctioned—it should have continued complying with all published restrictions regardless of the lateral deviation.
The pilot incorrectly reset the altitude window. The controller's only error was not catching the pilot's mistake.
The restrictions are automatically suspended during a vector and reactivate upon rejoin; the controller should have instructed the pilot to re-arm VNAV.

items.explanation: Per the note to 14-2-1, a vector/deviation off a SID/STAR cancels the altitude restrictions on the procedure. The FMS may be unable to process crossing restrictions once the aircraft leaves the lateral path. Without an assigned altitude, the FMS may revert to leveling off at the altitude set by the pilot—which may be the SID/STAR published top or bottom altitude. The controller must assign a specific altitude when vectoring an aircraft off a climb/descend via clearance.

items.instruction: Choose the best answer.
items.question_text: What is the controller's obligation regarding an IC mismatch or confirm assigned altitude (CAA) downlink time-out indicator displayed in the Full Data Block?

choices.text: Use CPDLC to verify the altitude, since the indicator appeared in the data link system.
The controller who has the aircraft on their voice frequency must use voice communication to verify the assigned altitude and acknowledge the indicator.
Wait for the receiving sector to verify the altitude after automated transfer is completed.
Acknowledge the indicator immediately to clear the FDB display, then verify the altitude only if the pilot reports a discrepancy.

items.explanation: Paragraph 14-2-2(a) assigns the obligation to a specific controller: the one who has the aircraft on their voice frequency. This controller must use voice communication—not CPDLC—to verify the assigned altitude and acknowledge the IC mismatch or CAA downlink time-out indicator. The note adds that all sectors in the controlling ARTCC displaying an FDB will show the indicator, so other sectors may see it but the voice-frequency controller owns the response.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A CPDLC shutdown occurs. Three aircraft have open uplinks with your sector, and one aircraft experienced an unexpected CPDLC connection loss 10 minutes ago—voice communication was never established with that aircraft. A trainee wants to resolve the open uplinks by CPDLC free-text before the system goes completely dark, and suggests waiting for the disconnected aircraft to re-establish connection automatically.
items.question_text: What are the controller's obligations for both the open uplinks and the aircraft with the lost connection?

choices.text: Resolve the open uplinks by CPDLC while it is still available, and wait for the lost-connection aircraft to reconnect.
Broadcast the shutdown advisory by voice only—the open uplinks will close automatically at shutdown, and the disconnected aircraft will re-establish on the next sector.
Broadcast the shutdown advisory by voice, resolve open uplinks by voice with each aircraft, and establish voice communication with the aircraft that lost its CPDLC connection.
Transfer all open uplinks to the adjacent sector and let them handle the shutdown cleanup.

items.explanation: Paragraph 14-2-3(c)(1) requires a voice broadcast during shutdown or failure. 14-2-3(c)(2) requires resolving open or abnormally closed uplinks by voice with each aircraft. 14-2-3(b) requires establishing voice communication with an aircraft that lost its CPDLC connection when voice was not previously established. None of these obligations can be delegated or deferred.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: An aircraft at FL400 is en route and not established on any procedure with published speed restrictions. The controller wants to issue a speed adjustment of Mach.80 using CPDLC. A trainee objects, saying that speed adjustments above FL390 always require explicit voice concurrence from the pilot. Another controller says the WILCO response via CPDLC satisfies the concurrence requirement.
items.question_text: May the speed adjustment be issued via CPDLC, and what pilot response satisfies the concurrence requirement?

choices.text: No, all speed adjustments above FL390 require voice concurrence under 5-7-2(b) regardless of the communication method.
Yes, via CPDLC, but the pilot must additionally read back the speed on voice for the concurrence to be valid.
Yes, via CPDLC with any pilot response—concurrence is presumed above FL390 unless the pilot sends UNABLE.
Yes, via CPDLC. The WILCO response satisfies the 5-7-2(b) concurrence requirement for speed assignments at or above FL390.

items.explanation: Paragraph 14-2-4(b)(1) states that when using CPDLC to issue a speed assignment to an aircraft at or above FL390, the WILCO response satisfies the pilot concurrence requirement. The aircraft is not on a route with published speed restrictions, so 14-2-4(b)(2) does not apply. WILCO via CPDLC is sufficient—a separate voice concurrence is not required.

items.instruction: What should you do?
items.situation: A CPDLC-connected aircraft is operating within VHF coverage and CPDLC is available. The pilot sends a route request via CPDLC. A trainee responds by voice on the VHF frequency because voice is quicker, and uses free text in the CPDLC response for the route clearance. Another controller says the response should use the standard CPDLC message set.
items.question_text: What rules govern how the controller should respond to this pilot's CPDLC message?

choices.text: Respond via CPDLC using the standard message set to the extent possible, since the pilot initiated via CPDLC. Voice may be used if it provides operational advantage.
Since the aircraft is within VHF coverage, the controller may freely choose between voice and CPDLC for the response.
CPDLC is mandatory as primary means regardless of VHF coverage status—voice response is never permitted.
Respond by voice only, because within VHF coverage CPDLC is a secondary medium that should be used only when voice is unavailable.

items.explanation: Paragraph 14-3-1(d) states that when a pilot communicates via CPDLC, the response should be via CPDLC. 14-3-1(e) requires using the message set over free text to the extent possible. 14-3-1(a) mandates CPDLC as primary only when the aircraft is outside VHF coverage—when inside VHF, CPDLC is still preferred for CPDLC-initiated exchanges. 14-3-1(b) allows voice when it provides operational advantage.

items.instruction: Choose the best answer.
items.question_text: When CPDLC service is to be canceled for an oceanic aircraft, what is the controller's obligation regarding notification to the pilot?

choices.text: The controller may cancel CPDLC without notification; the aircraft avionics will detect the termination and alert the pilot.
Must advise the pilot as early as possible to facilitate a smooth transition. Workload permitting, should also advise the reason for termination.
Must advise the pilot of the reason for termination in all cases. Timing of the notification is at the controller's discretion.
Should wait until the aircraft is within VHF coverage of the next facility before advising of the cancellation.

items.explanation: Paragraph 14-3-3(d) contains a must/should distinction: the controller MUST advise the pilot as early as possible to facilitate a smooth transition to voice communications (mandatory timing). Workload permitting, the controller SHOULD also advise the pilot of the reason for termination (discretionary content). The mandatory element is early notification; the reason is secondary.
